name: AcopioVzla
db_description:
Acopio nació como respuesta a un problema concreto: en situaciones de emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria se dispersa porque no existe información centralizada y actualizada sobre qué necesita cada centro de acopio. La comunicación ocurre por WhatsApp, el esfuerzo solidario se concentra en los centros más conocidos mientras otros quedan desabastecidos, y los voluntarios no saben a dónde ir ni qué llevar.
Acopio resuelve esto con una plataforma web simple: cada centro registra su perfil y publica actualizaciones en tiempo real sobre sus necesidades. La ciudadanía accede a un feed público donde puede ver, filtrar y decidir cómo ayudar de forma concreta e informada.
El proyecto es gratuito, sin publicidad y de acceso abierto. Fue construido con React, Vite, Supabase y PostGIS, con foco en ser liviano, rápido de adoptar y fácil de operar incluso desde un celular básico. Entre más centros participan, más útil se vuelve para todos — es infraestructura solidaria que escala con la comunidad.

analysis.summary: Acopio (rebranded Saman) is a mobile-first React/Vite/Supabase web app for tracking humanitarian collection centers (centros de acopio) after the 2025 Venezuela earthquake, letting coordinators post real-time needs and citizens find nearby centers, with a community feed, comments, notifications, and transport/lodging listings. It is a genuinely working MVP with real Supabase auth, RLS-secured Postgres/PostGIS schema, realtime subscriptions, image upload/compression, high test coverage, and CI, and is live-deployed at acopiovzla.vercel.app.
analysis.project_type: web app

infra_deploy: Vercel (live homepage, no vercel.json but auto-deploy)

architecture.pattern: Feature-folder SPA (feature-sliced) consuming a BaaS directly from the client, no backend/API layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client (postgrest) calls in src/features/*/queries.ts and mutations.ts; DB logic (RLS, RPC functions like centros_cercanos, triggers) lives in versioned SQL migrations under supabase/migrations
architecture.api_design: No REST/GraphQL API of its own; all data access goes through Supabase's auto-generated PostgREST API and RPC functions, called via @supabase/supabase-js from React Query hooks
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation between features (auth/centros/posts/anuncios/notificaciones), UI components, and lib utilities (supabase client, storage/image compression, theme); each feature has queries.ts/mutations.ts with matching test files
architecture.modularity: Good — small, focused files per feature and component, generated DB types (database.generated.ts) kept in sync via supabase gen types
notable_patterns: Row Level Security policies enforced at the Postgres level for every table (centros_acopio, posts, comentarios, notificaciones, anuncios)
Optimistic updates with TanStack Query onMutate/onError for 'útil' reactions
Realtime subscriptions (posts/realtime.ts, anuncios/realtime.ts) via Supabase channels
Client-side image compression before upload to Supabase Storage
PostGIS-based nearest-centers RPC (centros_cercanos) using ST_Distance/KNN operator
AI-assisted dev workflow artifacts (docs/superpowers plans/specs, AGENTS.md, .opencode) showing structured planning per feature

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear feature-first layout (src/features, src/components, src/pages, src/lib, src/types) mirrored by supabase/migrations for schema evolution; docs/PRD.md and docs/ARCHITECTURE.md exist.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (centros, anuncios, comentarios, necesidades) matching the product domain; TypeScript types generated from DB schema.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average for a hackathon project — PRD, ARCHITECTURE.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, per-feature planning docs, and .env.example are present.
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized feature-sliced SPA with matching test files per module and DB-generated types, though the .opencode/.superpowers/.codegraph scaffolding folders add some clutter.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env is gitignored and .env.example provided; an early commit briefly added a .env but it only contained a placeholder test URL/key, not real secrets; CI runs gitleaks secret scanning.
production_readiness.security_notes: Auth via Supabase (email/password) with RLS policies scoping writes to the authenticated owner (coordinador_id/user_id) on every table; anon key is public by design (expected for Supabase). No rate limiting or abuse protection visible for post creation, and no server-side validation beyond Postgres constraints/RLS.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Fully offloaded to Supabase (Postgres/Auth/Storage/Realtime) which can scale reasonably for a regional MVP; PostGIS index for geo queries is a sound choice; no caching/CDN config beyond Vercel static hosting; no background jobs/queue for notification fan-out.
production_readiness.reasoning: Genuinely deployed, functioning app (live URL) with real auth, DB constraints/RLS, image handling, and CI gates, but lacks structured logging/observability, rate limiting, and a formal deploy config file (vercel.json), keeping it below full production-grade.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 33 commits, 28 test files with ~99% line coverage, GitHub Actions CI (lint/typecheck/test/build/gitleaks), pre-commit hooks via lefthook, 11 sequential SQL migrations reflecting iterative real feature work (comments, notifications, transport listings), and a live Vercel deployment — this is a functioning, actively iterated MVP, not empty scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: The core loop (find nearby centers, see real-time needs, coordinators post updates, community interacts) is fully implemented end-to-end with a live deployment, solid data model, and RLS security, making it realistically deployable to help coordinate earthquake relief; remaining gaps are operational (logging/monitoring, abuse prevention, moderation) rather than missing core functionality.

key_features: Geolocation-based discovery of nearby collection centers via PostGIS RPC
Coordinator posts with 'necesidades' (needs) tags and photo uploads
Realtime feed updates via Supabase Realtime channels
Comments and 'útil' (helpful) reactions on posts with optimistic UI updates
In-app notifications for centro-related activity
Community wall and transport/lodging (anuncios) listings
Light/dark theme toggle, mobile bottom nav and desktop sidebar layouts
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate to be a primary/base project for aid-center coordination given its working data model, RLS security, and live deployment; could merge with a shelter-mapping or people-finder project by reusing its Supabase geo-query pattern (centros_cercanos RPC) and notification system as shared infrastructure.
red_flags: No rate limiting/abuse controls on post/anuncio creation, which is risky for a public emergency-info tool
No structured logging/monitoring/error-tracking (e.g., Sentry) despite being a live production deployment
All backend logic lives in Supabase RLS/RPC with no server-side validation layer, so correctness depends entirely on getting Postgres policies exactly right
Single contributor (bus factor of 1) despite CONTRIBUTING.md and PR template suggesting an intended team

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Directly addresses a documented coordination failure in Venezuela earthquake relief: aid gets concentrated on well-known collection centers while others go unsupplied because needs information is scattered across WhatsApp chats with no central, real-time view. AcopioVzla centralizes centro-acopio profiles, live needs updates, geolocation-based discovery, and a public feed so volunteers/donors know exactly where to go and what to bring. This is a logistics/coordination problem, not a novelty, and the domain tags (aid-logistics, mapping-geo, volunteer-coordination) match a genuine relief bottleneck.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live URL (https://AcopioVzla.vercel.app) returns HTTP 200 and serves a proper React/Vite SPA shell (title 'Saman — Conectando Venezuela', hashed JS/CSS bundles, theme script) confirming a real deployed build, but it is a client-side-rendered app so a static/headless fetch cannot see the actual rendered feed, map, or centro listings — could not visually verify live data (e.g. real registered centros) without a JS-executing browser or screenshots. No demo video exists to compensate. Given the strong backend evidence (Supabase auth+RLS, PostGIS geo RPC, realtime channels, ~99% test coverage, CI with lint/typecheck/test/build/gitleaks, 11 sequential schema migrations, 33 commits over ~2 days), this is very likely a genuinely working product, but it is not independently confirmed visually.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as 'la app que centraliza qué necesita cada centro de acopio en tiempo real, para que la ayuda deje de perderse en cadenas de WhatsApp' — highlight the geolocation 'find nearest center' feature and the real-time needs feed as the concrete value for both volunteers and coordinators.
gaps: No demo video to show the actual working feed/map since the SPA can't be verified via static fetch or screenshot in this pass
No screenshots captured/available in the insight data
No evidence yet of real_users (registered centros or actual usage data) to cite in a testimonial-style post
Single contributor (bus factor 1) is a risk to mention only internally, not externally

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Acopio (Saman): la plataforma que centraliza en tiempo real qué necesita cada centro de acopio en Venezuela, para que ningún voluntario llegue con las manos vacías ni ningún centro se quede sin ayuda.

name: AidTrace | RastroAyuda
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

Construí **AidTrace | RastroAyuda**, una app offline-first compatible para desktop, mobile y Telegram para rastrear ayuda humanitaria con QR y pruebas en Celo blockchain

## ¿Para quién es?

Para equipos humanitarios, refugios, donantes y auditores que necesitan verificar entregas de agua, comida, medicinas o kits de emergencia.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Permite registrar entregas, recepciones y revisiones incluso sin internet. Cuando vuelve la conexión, guarda la prueba en Celo y muestra el enlace en Celoscan.

Los usuarios en campo no necesitan wallet, CELO ni saber de blockchain.

## ¿Qué deberían probar primero?

Crear un lote, generar el QR, registrar una entrega desde el móvil y revisar el historial con el link a Celoscan.

También pueden probar Telegram con:

`CELO1 entregar 13 cajas de ibuprofeno para el refugio mayor`


linkedin.com/in/luis-alejandro

architecture.pattern: Serverless functions (Vercel /api) + static PWA frontend + Postgres (Supabase) as queue/state store + Celo blockchain as immutable audit ledger
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw SQL via Supabase REST/RPC (fetch calls to /rest/v1/rpc/*), no ORM; DB logic (locking, rate limiting, queueing, RLS) implemented directly in PL/pgSQL functions
architecture.api_design: Vercel serverless handlers (api/*.mjs) exposed as REST-ish endpoints for webhook ingestion (Zavu/Telegram), browser relay, timeline reads (via on-chain log scanning), queue processing/retry, center inventory webhooks, and CSV export
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable separation between parsing (lib/*.mjs), chain-write logic (api/zavu.mjs), chain-read/indexing (api/timeline.mjs), and SQL-defined access-control/queue primitives (supabase/*.sql); frontend logic concentrated in a single large app.js
architecture.modularity: Backend split into small single-purpose api handlers and lib parsers; frontend is a monolithic app.js (1218 lines) and index.html rather than componentized
notable_patterns: Offline-first PWA with IndexedDB/localStorage queue and service worker background sync
Distributed lock (Supabase RPC or Upstash Redis) to serialize Celo writes and avoid nonce collisions
Idempotency keys and dedupe guards for relayed browser/telegram events
Sender allowlist + per-sender hourly rate limiting implemented in Postgres RPCs with RLS
Gasless meta-relayer pattern: server-held relayer key submits recordAction on behalf of field users so they never hold a wallet
Multi-channel ingestion (Telegram via Zavu SDK, browser relay, 'Invent' WhatsApp channel) funneled into one queue/processing pipeline

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear top-level split: api/ (serverless endpoints), lib/ (pure parsers/notify helpers), supabase/ (SQL migrations with detailed comments), scripts/ (deploy/smoke-check tooling incl. PowerShell), test/ (node:test unit tests), zavu/aidtrace-relayer (separate relayer service). Frontend is flat (index.html, app.js, styles.css) at repo root.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming across files, env vars, and SQL functions (e.g. AIDTRACE_SENDER_RATE_LIMIT_HOUR, begin_aidtrace_browser_relay_event)
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is detailed and user-facing (workflow, offline behavior, Telegram command examples); SQL files carry inline operational runbooks; scripts/ has deploy-checklist.md and setup guides
code_organization.reasoning: Well organized for a hackathon project with genuine separation between chain logic, DB logic, and channel adapters, though the frontend remains a single large file rather than modularized components.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Env vars used throughout (relayer private key, Supabase service role key, webhook tokens, Zavu API key); .gitignore excludes .env*; scripts validate key format before use; no secrets found hardcoded in source
production_readiness.security_notes: CORS allowlist enforced, timing-safe token comparisons for webhooks (Svix HMAC-style verification in zavu-proxy.mjs), Supabase RLS enabled on tables, sender allowlist + rate limiting to curb abuse, input validation on relay payloads (regex on batchId, length caps, allowed action enum). Weaknesses: single shared relayer private key model (custodial, single point of failure/compromise), CONTRACT_ADDRESS default fallback hardcoded to a specific address, and no visible authz for the coordinator dashboard beyond a client-stored 'coordinator token'.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Serverless functions plus a distributed lock/queue for serialized on-chain writes is a sensible pattern to avoid nonce races, but throughput is bottlenecked by sequential Celo writes (CELO_WRITE_GAP_MS delay) and a single relayer account — acceptable for small-scale disaster response but would need horizontal scaling design for large volume.
production_readiness.reasoning: Real error handling, structured logging, environment-driven configuration, and Vercel deploy config are present, plus a genuine security posture (RLS, rate limits, CORS, timing-safe checks) beyond typical hackathon scaffolding; still missing broader operational hardening (monitoring/alerting, key rotation automation, multi-relayer redundancy) expected of a mature production system.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working implementation with substantial custom logic (not boilerplate)
maturity.reasoning: 86 commits over ~a few days, GitHub Actions CI running unit tests and syntax checks (node --test, 28/31 tests passing locally — the 3 failures are due to a missing devDependency in this sandbox, not code defects), a deployed Vercel URL, deployed Celo mainnet contract address, and detailed operational docs (smoke-check scripts, deploy checklists) indicating active iterative development rather than a static scaffold.

viability.reasoning: Directly addresses a concrete, real disaster-relief problem (proving custody/chain-of-custody for aid supplies with offline resilience) with a working end-to-end flow: QR creation, offline queueing, gasless Celo write, Telegram bot channel, and an auditable public timeline; deployable today by a coordinator with minimal setup (Vercel + Supabase + a funded relayer wallet), though it depends on continued Celo gas funding and a single relayer key, which are operational risks for a volunteer-run deployment in Venezuela.

key_features: QR-code based aid batch creation and custody tracking
Offline-first PWA with local queueing and automatic background sync
Gasless blockchain audit trail on Celo (server-relayed transactions, no wallet needed by field users)
Telegram bot channel for text-based custody updates in low-connectivity conditions
Public, on-chain-verifiable audit timeline with Celoscan transaction links
Sender allowlist and per-sender rate limiting to prevent abuse of the relay/bot channel
Distributed locking (Supabase or Redis) to serialize blockchain writes and avoid nonce conflicts
Center inventory/delivery webhook integration for downstream systems
CSV export and queue-status/retry admin endpoints
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone candidate for aid-logistics/supply-chain tracking; could complement people-finder or shelter-mapping projects by providing an auditable custody trail for supplies delivered to shelters, but its blockchain/Celo dependency and relayer-funding requirement make it a distinct operational module rather than an easy merge target.
red_flags: Single custodial relayer private key signs all on-chain writes — compromise or loss halts the entire audit trail and is a centralization/security risk
Depends on continuous Celo gas funding for the relayer wallet to keep functioning, an ongoing operational cost/risk for a volunteer deployment
Coordinator dashboard/admin access relies on a client-stored token rather than a robust auth system
No visible handling for personal/sensitive data minimization beyond a README note asking users not to include sensitive info in free-text notes
analysis.summary: AidTrace/RastroAyuda is an offline-first PWA plus serverless backend that gives humanitarian aid shipments a QR-coded, blockchain-anchored chain-of-custody record on Celo, reachable via browser or Telegram, with a Postgres/Supabase-backed queue, distributed locking, rate limiting, and RLS-secured access control. It is a genuinely implemented, tested, and CI-covered system (not boilerplate) addressing a real disaster-logistics need, though it centers on a single custodial relayer key and requires ongoing gas funding and Supabase/Vercel operational upkeep.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web app (PWA) + serverless API + smart contract for humanitarian aid custody tracking

database: Supabase/Postgres (queue, rate limiting, access control, center inventory) with RLS and PL/pgSQL RPC functions

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the offline-first + no-wallet-needed angle: field workers in disaster zones with no signal can still create a tamper-proof, blockchain-verified record of every box of medicine or water delivered, using just a QR code or a WhatsApp/Telegram message - proof donors and auditors can trust even when the internet is down.
gaps: No evidence yet of real-world usage/pilot with an actual Venezuelan humanitarian org (real-users asset missing)
Single custodial relayer key and dependency on continuous Celo gas funding are operational risks worth flagging before heavy promotion
Frontend screenshots/GIFs of the QR + Celoscan proof flow would strengthen social assets beyond the video

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch confirms the live Vercel URL renders a real, functional dashboard (not a placeholder landing page): batch-creation form with supply type/quantity/origin/destination fields, QR generation and printing, field recording via QR scan/manual code/Telegram, GPS-tagged custody map, and a proof timeline. This matches the technical analysis's description of a working offline-first PWA backed by Supabase and a Celo smart contract.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: AidTrace | RastroAyuda: rastrea la ayuda humanitaria (agua, comida, medicinas) con QR y Telegram, incluso sin internet, y deja una prueba inmutable en blockchain que cualquiera puede verificar - sin que el equipo en terreno necesite saber nada de cripto.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Aid diversion, lack of transparent chain-of-custody, and donor distrust are persistent, well-documented problems in disaster relief and humanitarian logistics generally, and specifically relevant to Venezuela given its history of aid politicization and corruption concerns. AidTrace targets a genuine operational need for refugios/humanitarian teams/auditors: proving that a batch of supplies actually reached its destination, verifiable by anyone via Celoscan, and it explicitly designs around the offline/low-connectivity reality of disaster zones (offline-first PWA + Telegram bot fallback) and around field workers not needing crypto knowledge (gasless relayer). This is a real, non-trivial problem with clear beneficiaries (aid orgs, donors, auditors) rather than a generic blockchain-for-blockchain's-sake exercise.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**ApoyoVenezuela** es un directorio comunitario de centros de acopio y puntos de voluntariado, validado por gente real que ya donó o ayudó en cada lugar. Nació a raíz del terremoto en Venezuela (junio 2026) para responder a un problema concreto: en una emergencia aparecen decenas de puntos de ayuda, pero muchos son temporales, cambian o dejan de operar en pocos días, y no hay forma fácil de saber en cuáles confiar.

En lugar de una lista estática que envejece, es un **proyecto vivo que se sostiene entre todos**. La comunidad lo mantiene al día con tres acciones:

- **Agregar** un lugar que conozcas, para que otros lo encuentren.
- **Confirmar** que estuviste ahí (donaste o fuiste voluntario). Cada confirmación le da confianza a quien viene después.
- **Dar de baja** un lugar cuando ya cerró, tiene datos erróneos o muestra actividad sospechosa. Con tres reportes, el lugar sale del listado automáticamente, sin trámites ni esperas.

## ¿Para quién es?

Para cualquiera que quiera ayudar y no sepa por dónde empezar: personas dentro y fuera de Venezuela que buscan un punto confiable donde donar o ser voluntarias, y quienes conocen lugares de ayuda y quieren ponerlos en el mapa.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Convierte información dispersa y difícil de verificar en un listado en el que se puede confiar, cuidado por la propia comunidad. Nadie depende de un administrador central: la gente que pisa los lugares es la que valida, corrige y depura el directorio en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Explora el listado y entra a la ficha de algún centro de acopio o punto de voluntariado.
2. Si reconoces un lugar donde ya ayudaste, **confírmalo**: es la acción que más fortalece la confianza del directorio.
3. ¿Sabes de un lugar que falta? **Agrégalo** en segundos.
4. Compártelo con quien esté buscando cómo ayudar 👉 [apoyovenezuela.org](https://www.apoyovenezuela.org)

analysis.summary: apoyo-venezuela is a working MVP web directory of donation-collection and volunteering points for the Venezuelan diaspora, built on Vite/React 19 + Supabase (Postgres + Edge Functions). It is not an earthquake-response tool but a general community aid-location directory (crowdsourced places, hours, confirmations, abuse reports) with genuinely implemented validation, rate limiting, RLS and tests rather than boilerplate.
analysis.project_type: Community aid-location directory (donation/volunteering points map+list) with anonymous crowdsourced submissions

infra_deploy: implied Vercel hosting for frontend (Vercel Analytics dep, homepage domain)

architecture.pattern: SPA (React) talking directly to Supabase (BaaS) for reads via PostgREST, and to Supabase Edge Functions for anonymous writes (add-place, add-confirmation, report-place)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (PostgREST) for reads; raw SQL migrations + PL/pgSQL RPC (check_anon_rate_limit) for rate limiting; service-role client inside Edge Functions for writes
architecture.api_design: No custom REST/GraphQL API server; reads go straight to Postgres views (active_places) via Supabase client, writes go through three narrow Deno Edge Functions with CORS handling and typed payload validation
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation: src/lib for client/data helpers, src/hooks for React Query data layer, supabase/functions/_shared for validation/http/rate-limit utilities reused across functions, db/migrations for schema evolution
architecture.modularity: Small, single-purpose modules (validation.ts, rateLimit.ts, http.ts, hours.ts) with shared code factored into supabase/functions/_shared rather than duplicated per function
notable_patterns: Anonymous-write abuse mitigation via IP+UA fingerprint hashed (SHA-256) rate-limit keys, enforced by a Postgres RPC and used per-operation and per-place
Soft moderation: place_claims table + active_places view auto-hides places with >=3 claims instead of hard delete
Client falls back to bundled SEED_PLACES data and shows a Spanish error message when the Supabase query fails, so the UI degrades gracefully
Country-derived timezone stored via FK (countries table) to support an 'open now' filter, evolved across multiple migrations with explicit pre-flight orphan checks

code_organization.directory_structure: Conventional and clean: src/{components,hooks,lib,i18n,types,data}, supabase/functions/{add-place,add-confirmation,report-place,_shared}, db/migrations, docs/superpowers (design/plan docs)
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming (usePlaces, anonymousWrites, rateLimitRules, normalizeAddPlacePayload) matching domain vocabulary
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers local dev, env vars, running Supabase locally and edge functions with concrete curl example; docs/superpowers contains dated design/plan markdown for specific features, showing deliberate engineering process
code_organization.reasoning: Small (3k LOC) but coherent codebase with logical module boundaries, shared utilities extracted rather than duplicated, and unusually good docs/migration hygiene for a hackathon-stage project.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service-role key kept server-side only (Edge Functions env), anon key intentionally exposed client-side per Supabase model; README explicitly warns not to put the service role key in Vite env; .env.example provided; GitHub Actions deploy uses repo secrets (SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN, SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF)
production_readiness.security_notes: No end-user auth (fully anonymous by design), mitigated with server-side payload validation (type/length/enum checks), IP+UA-hash based rate limiting via a Postgres RPC, RLS policies on place_claims, and CORS restricted to POST/OPTIONS with permissive origin '*' (acceptable for a public read directory but slightly loose); abuse-report threshold auto-hides places instead of deleting, reducing griefing risk.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Simple read-heavy directory app; Supabase/Postgres and Edge Functions scale adequately for expected traffic; no caching layer beyond React Query client cache; rate-limit table could grow unbounded without a cleanup job (not verified).
production_readiness.reasoning: Has real input validation, rate limiting, RLS, CI deploy pipeline and tests, but lacks any structured logging/observability, no auth/roles beyond anonymous public writes, and no monitoring/alerting — solid MVP but short of full production hardening.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, actively developed application (62 commits, incremental feature history, dated design docs, working test suite of 9 passing tests across validation/rate-limit/place-filtering logic) — not AI-boilerplate scaffolding.
maturity.reasoning: Small but genuine codebase with a working build/test pipeline (verified: npm test passes 9/9), real business logic (opening-hours computation, timezone-aware 'open now', validation, rate limiting), and CI for deploying edge functions, though it is only ~5 days old with a single main contributor visible in commits.

viability.reasoning: Functionally solid and deployable MVP for crowdsourced aid-location listings for the Venezuelan diaspora, but it is a general donation/volunteering-point directory, not an earthquake-specific tool (no shelter capacity, no medical/SOS features, no offline support), limiting its direct applicability to acute earthquake disaster response despite good engineering quality.

key_features: Crowdsourced directory of donation-collection and volunteering places with country/city, address, URL, and structured weekly hours
Anonymous submission via Supabase Edge Functions with server-side validation and per-fingerprint rate limiting
Community confirmation ('left donation', 'volunteered', 'visited and still active') and abuse-report/claim system that auto-hides places after 3+ reports
'Open now' filter computed from country-derived IANA timezone and structured hours
i18n support (es/en/pt) and searchable country combobox
Graceful degradation to bundled seed data with a Spanish user-facing error message when the backend is unreachable
analysis.merge_potential: Could complement other Build4Venezuela tools as the 'donation drop-off / volunteering location directory' layer, but would need earthquake-specific fields (shelter capacity, urgent-needs tags, real-time status) and integration with a broader crisis-response platform (e.g., people-finder or aid-logistics tools) to be directly useful for earthquake response rather than general diaspora aid collection.
red_flags: Not earthquake/disaster specific — current schema and UX target ongoing diaspora donation drives, not acute post-earthquake shelter/needs coordination
No authentication/roles: anyone can add/confirm/report places anonymously, relying solely on rate limiting and a 3-report auto-hide threshold for abuse control
No structured logging/monitoring/alerting for the Edge Functions or frontend errors
CORS Access-Control-Allow-Origin is '*' on write endpoints (mitigated by validation+rate limiting but still broad)
Very young project (created 2026-06-26) with limited contributor/activity history to judge long-term maintenance

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The project addresses a real and adjacent problem: after a disaster, aid/donation points proliferate but are unreliable, ephemeral, and hard to trust — a genuine pain point for diaspora donors and volunteers. However, per the team's own analysis, this is a general crowdsourced donation/volunteering-point directory, not an earthquake-specific response tool: no shelter capacity tracking, no medical/SOS features, no urgent-needs tagging, and no offline support for victims on the ground. It helps donors/volunteers find trustworthy places to give, but does little directly for earthquake victims who need shelter, medical help, or missing-person coordination.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://www.apoyovenezuela.org returns HTTP 200 with proper meta tags, OG image, and a fully bundled Vite/React SPA (index-*.js/css assets load). It is a genuine client-rendered app (not a static mockup); WebFetch could not render the JS content directly (SPA limitation), but curl confirms real deployment infra, correct SEO/OG setup, and the codebase analysis independently confirms working reads/writes against Supabase, 9/9 passing tests, CI deploy pipeline for edge functions, and graceful fallback to seed data on backend failure.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Crowdsourced trust layer for Venezuela diaspora aid: 'confirmed by real donors, auto-removed if it goes stale' — position it as solving the '¿en cuál punto de acopio confío?' problem for people wanting to help from abroad.
gaps: Not framed or built as earthquake-specific (no shelter/medical/urgent-needs data), so promoting it as 'earthquake relief' tool overstates its scope
No screenshots/UI captured in this evaluation since the SPA content wasn't directly renderable via WebFetch — should grab real screenshots before a spotlight post
No evidence yet of real-world adoption/usage numbers (real-users asset not confirmed) since project is only ~5 days old with 0 stars/forks

evaluation.one_line_pitch: ApoyoVenezuela: el directorio vivo de centros de acopio y voluntariado, validado por quienes ya donaron — para que ayudar desde la diáspora nunca dependa de listas desactualizadas.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?

Apoyo Venezuela is a mobile-first, anonymous crowd-coordination web app built
after the June 24, 2026 earthquake in Venezuela (a doublet of roughly M7.1 and
M7.5). It lets anyone report affected zones on an interactive map, post the
specific needs of each location, mark structural status, and look up verified
emergency phone numbers, with live updates across all users.

Core features:

- **Interactive map of affected zones** with a structural-status traffic light
  (collapsed, damaged, stable, unconfirmed), always shown as color plus icon
  plus text label for accessibility.
- **Zone reporting** with address autocomplete, reverse geocoding, geolocation,
  or by dragging a pin, including safe photo uploads.
- **Needs per zone** (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter, and more) with a
  needed → on the way → covered lifecycle.
- **Verified emergency phone directory** by state, with tap-to-call and the
  source of each number.
- **Aid guide**: what to donate and what to avoid, shelters, collection centers,
  and aid organizations.
- **Live updates** on the map and lists via Supabase Realtime, a PWA with light
  and dark mode.

Who is it for? Anyone affected by or responding to the earthquake: families
reporting damage and needs, volunteers and rescuers locating where help is
required, and donors finding verified collection points. No account or login
is needed, and reports are anonymous by design.

How does it help? It turns scattered social-media pleas into a single, live,
map-based view of where help is needed and what is already covered, so aid is
not duplicated and no zone is forgotten.

What should people try first? Open the map, tap an affected zone to see its
needs and structural status, then file a report or update a need from your own
location to see it appear live for everyone.

Built with Next.js 16, React 19, strict TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4, Leaflet +
OpenStreetMap, and Supabase (Postgres + Realtime). A citizen initiative with no
political affiliation; information is community-contributed.

analysis.summary: Apoyo Venezuela is a live, production-deployed (apoyovenezuela.com) Next.js 16 / React 19 PWA for citizen-coordinated earthquake relief: an interactive map of affected zones with structural status, per-zone needs tracking, a verified emergency phone directory, fundraiser listings, and an AI assistant grounded on the live dataset. It is backed by Supabase Postgres with RLS, a public REST API v1 with OpenAPI docs, and an MCP server exposing the same data as tools. Engineering quality is unusually high for a hackathon entry: 1025 passing unit tests, clean typecheck, CI running lint/typecheck/test/build, and a migration history showing deliberate security hardening (closing an initially-open anonymous UPDATE policy, adding hashed-IP rate limiting for reports, fail-open quota checks, AI request validation/rate limiting).
analysis.project_type: web app (Next.js PWA) with public REST API and MCP server

database: Row Level Security + SECURITY DEFINER RPCs
infra_deploy: Vercel (implied by homepage + env var conventions)
ai_ml: OpenAI gpt-4o-mini via @ai-sdk/openai and Vercel AI SDK, grounded tool-calling assistant

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with a swappable data-store abstraction (in-memory demo store vs Supabase store) behind a single DataStore interface
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (supabase-js) with hand-written snake_case-to-camelCase row mappers; no ORM. Mutations mostly routed through SECURITY DEFINER Postgres RPC functions rather than direct table writes.
architecture.api_design: REST v1 under /api/v1 (zonas, pedidos, campanas, estadisticas) with OpenAPI JSON/Markdown docs and a Scalar-based reference UI, plus a parallel MCP server (/api/mcp) exposing the same read operations as tools for AI agents.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear layering: lib/data (store contract + supabase/memory implementations + pure selectors), lib/ai (assistant tools, rate limiting, validation), lib/api (shared DTO/pagination helpers reused by REST and MCP), components/ for UI, app/ for routes only.
architecture.modularity: High; shared pure functions (selectors, transforms) are reused across REST, MCP, and UI, and covered individually by unit tests.
notable_patterns: Dual-mode data store: falls back to an in-memory demo dataset when Supabase env vars are absent, so the app runs with zero config
SECURITY DEFINER Postgres functions used to lock down anonymous writes after an initial migration exposed open UPDATE policies
Hashed-IP sliding-window report throttle implemented as a fail-open DB function to avoid blocking legitimate emergency reports
AI assistant is tool-grounded (buscarZonas) with a strict system prompt, per-IP rate limiting, and payload-size validation to bound cost/abuse
Service worker with an explicit life-safety invariant: dynamic emergency data is never served stale from cache

code_organization.directory_structure: Idiomatic Next.js App Router layout with well-separated lib/data, lib/ai, lib/api, lib/geocoding, lib/sismos modules; components/ split into feature and ui/ primitives; supabase/migrations tracks incremental, well-commented schema evolution; scripts/ for one-off data imports with matching rollback scripts.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming in Spanish domain terms (locations/zonas, needs/pedidos) mapped cleanly to English code identifiers where appropriate.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Extensive inline comments explaining non-obvious security and reliability decisions (fail-open rate limiting, RLS tightening rationale, SW caching invariants); README, CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, CHANGELOG.md all present and substantive.
code_organization.reasoning: Directory layout, naming, and comments are all well above hackathon norms and reflect genuine software-engineering discipline rather than generated boilerplate.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Server-only secrets (OPENAI_API_KEY, MAPBOX_TOKEN) explicitly kept without NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix per .env.example comments; Supabase anon key is public by design (RLS-protected); .env.example present, no secrets committed.
production_readiness.security_notes: No user auth is used by design (anonymous civic tool), but the team caught and fixed a real vulnerability where the initial RLS policy let any anonymous client overwrite arbitrary columns on any row, replacing it with SECURITY DEFINER RPCs restricted to validated status transitions; report submission is rate-limited via a hashed-IP throttle function with RLS locking out direct table access; AI assistant route has request size/shape validation and per-IP rate limiting.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: In-memory AI rate limiter and demo store are explicitly documented as per-instance/non-durable and acceptable only as an interim measure; Supabase Postgres + Realtime should scale adequately for the traffic profile of a regional relief tool.
production_readiness.reasoning: CI enforces lint/typecheck/test/build on every push; live deployed at a custom domain; migrations show real production incident-driven hardening; main gaps are lack of structured server-side logging/observability and no authenticated moderation path (relies on public removal-request queue) beyond what a life-safety MVP typically needs.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, actively developed application with substantial working logic
maturity.reasoning: 1025 passing unit tests (verified by running vitest), clean tsc --noEmit, GitHub Actions CI covering lint/typecheck/test/build, 76 commits with detailed, security-aware commit messages, CONTRIBUTING/SECURITY/CODE_OF_CONDUCT docs, and a running production URL (apoyovenezuela.com) — this is not scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: Directly addresses the hackathon's real-world need (earthquake relief coordination in Venezuela) with a working, tested, deployed product covering zone mapping, needs tracking, emergency contacts, and fundraiser discovery; main viability risks are reliance on unauthenticated crowd-sourced data integrity and a two-person team's ability to moderate abuse at scale during a real crisis.

key_features: Interactive Leaflet map of affected zones with structural-status traffic light and clustering
Per-zone needs board (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter, etc.) with a needed/on-the-way/covered lifecycle
Zone reporting with address autocomplete, reverse geocoding, geolocation, or map-pin drop, and photo upload with MIME/size restrictions
Verified emergency phone directory by state with source attribution
AI assistant (/asistente) grounded on live zone data via tool-calling, rate-limited and validated
Public REST API v1 with OpenAPI docs and a companion MCP server exposing the same data to AI agents
PWA with offline fallback and a service worker that deliberately never serves stale emergency data
Supabase Realtime live updates on map/lists; anonymous-write hardening via SECURITY DEFINER RPCs and hashed-IP report throttling
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone candidate; could integrate with a separate missing-persons/family-reunification project (the README already links a sister project, desaparecidosterremotovenezuela.com) via its public REST/MCP API rather than merging codebases.
red_flags: No authenticated moderation layer; anyone can create/insert zone and need reports, relying entirely on RLS+RPC constraints and a removal-request queue for correction
AI assistant and report-throttle rate limiters are in-memory/per-instance, so protections are weaker under multi-instance serverless scaling
No structured server-side logging/monitoring evident (only ad hoc console usage), which limits incident response during an actual disaster surge

diffusion.recommended_angle: Show the live map with real zone counts and a real before/after: 'Mientras las redes sociales se llenaban de pedidos de ayuda dispersos, Apoyo Venezuela ya tiene 1,000 zonas mapeadas y 2,570 necesidades registradas en tiempo real.' Pair a screenshot of the interactive map + a stat callout (1000 zonas, 2570 pedidos, teléfonos verificados) with a CTA to report or donate.
gaps: No demo video exists — a 30-60s screen recording of reporting a zone and seeing it appear live would strengthen the pitch
No dedicated screenshots/press-kit images captured yet for social cards

evaluation.live_demo_notes: apoyovenezuela.com returns HTTP 200 and serves a real Next.js PWA titled 'Apoyo Venezuela - coordina ayuda tras el terremoto'. Verified the public REST API is live and populated with real data: /api/v1/zonas returns actual geolocated zone reports (lat/lng, structural status like 'derrumbe', uploaded photos hosted on Supabase storage, addresses in Puerto Cabello, Vargas, etc.), and /api/v1/estadisticas shows 1000 zones tracked, 2570 total need requests across categories (agua, alimentos, medicinas, refugio, etc.) with urgency levels. This is not a placeholder — it is an actively used, crowd-populated coordination tool.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Apoyo Venezuela: el mapa colaborativo en vivo que ya coordina 1,000 zonas y 2,570 necesidades tras el terremoto — sin apps que instalar, sin cuentas, solo ayuda que llega a tiempo.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Directly addresses the June 24, 2026 M7.1/M7.5 earthquake doublet in Venezuela — the exact scenario the hackathon targets. It replaces scattered, duplicative social-media aid pleas with a single live map of structural status, per-zone needs (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter) with a needed→on-the-way→covered lifecycle, and a verified emergency phone directory. This is precisely the coordination failure mode (duplicated aid, forgotten zones, unverified numbers) that costs lives and wastes donations in real disasters, and the live API confirms it is actually being used to track real reports, not a hypothetical.

name: awesome-venezuela-relief Public
db_description:
## Awesome Venezuela Relief

Una lista curada de proyectos, herramientas, datasets y recursos tecnológicos creados para apoyar la respuesta humanitaria tras el doble terremoto ocurrido en Venezuela el 24 de junio de 2026.

El objetivo de este repositorio es centralizar iniciativas open source que faciliten la coordinación de voluntarios, la difusión de información confiable, las donaciones, la búsqueda de personas, el apoyo psicológico y otras soluciones desarrolladas por la comunidad tecnológica para responder a la emergencia.

analysis.summary: A bilingual (ES/EN) awesome-list README curating links to other GitHub repos related to the fictional 2026 Venezuela earthquake relief response. The repository itself contains only a single README.md file — no application code, no package manager files, no LICENSE (despite being referenced), no CONTRIBUTING.md (also referenced but absent), and no functionality of its own.
analysis.project_type: curated link directory / awesome-list (documentation only, no application)

architecture.pattern: None — single static README file, no code architecture of any kind
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None
architecture.api_design: None
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Not applicable; there is no code to separate.
architecture.modularity: Not applicable; single markdown file only.

code_organization.directory_structure: Repo root contains only README.md and .git; no src, no config, no other directories.
code_organization.naming_quality: Not applicable — no code files exist to evaluate naming.
code_organization.documentation_quality: The README itself is thorough, well-structured, and bilingual, but it is the entirety of the repo's content; it links to files (LICENSE, CONTRIBUTING.md) that do not actually exist in the tree.
code_organization.reasoning: There is no code organization to assess since the repository consists of exactly one markdown file with no source code, configuration, or supporting files.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: N/A — no code or secrets exist in this repo
production_readiness.security_notes: No attack surface exists since there is no application; however the README itself is an unmoderated pointer list and could propagate links to broken, fake, or malicious 'relief' sites without any verification mechanism beyond volunteer review.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: N/A — this is a static markdown document, not a deployable service.
production_readiness.reasoning: There is zero deployable code; this cannot be 'production-ready' in any technical sense because there is no production system, only a list of links to other people's projects.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Not boilerplate scaffolding either — it's a pure documentation/index repo (an 'awesome-list'). It contains real, substantive written content (bilingual, detailed categories, contribution guidelines) but zero functional code.
maturity.reasoning: Single commit, single file (README.md), no tests, no CI, no LICENSE file despite being linked, no package/dependency files of any kind — this is a content-only directory repo, not a software project.

viability.reasoning: As a hackathon submission this delivers no working software, no deployable artifact, and no functionality beyond a hand-curated list of other teams' repos; it cannot itself be deployed to help earthquake victims and offers zero unique technical value beyond what a wiki page or gist could provide.

key_features: Bilingual (Spanish/English) categorized directory of external relief-related GitHub projects
Inclusion criteria and contribution guidelines for adding new projects via PR
Status badges (Active/In development/Inactive) for listed external projects
FAQ and important-disclaimer sections clarifying the repo is unofficial and volunteer-run
analysis.merge_potential: None as a code contribution; at most it could serve as an index/README section within a broader build4venezuela meta-repo, but has no code to merge and duplicates the kind of catalog that a central hub project would already need to maintain itself.
red_flags: Repository contains zero source code — literally only README.md exists in the working tree
References LICENSE and CONTRIBUTING.md files in links that do not actually exist in the repository
No verification mechanism for the legitimacy/safety of the many third-party links it aggregates, despite claiming 'verified resources' in some category descriptions
Single commit, single contributor, created and pushed within about 90 minutes on the same day, suggesting AI-generated boilerplate content rather than an organically curated community list

diffusion.recommended_angle: Not suitable for standalone promotion; at most a single line in a broader 'community resources' roundup, and even then only after LICENSE/CONTRIBUTING files are actually added.
gaps: No working product or code of any kind (0 LOC)
No live demo or video
References LICENSE/CONTRIBUTING files that don't exist, undermining credibility
No verification mechanism for the third-party links it claims are 'verified'
Single commit/contributor, built in ~90 minutes, reads as AI-generated boilerplate rather than genuine curation

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public web demo, no video, and no application to demo in the first place — the repo contains a single README.md file with zero source code, zero deployable artifacts, and broken references to LICENSE/CONTRIBUTING files that don't exist.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: A one-file link list that points to other people's relief projects, not a relief tool itself — nothing here for victims or donors to actually use.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: While a curated directory of relief resources could theoretically have some navigational value, this repo does not deliver that value: it has no verification of the links it aggregates (despite claiming some are 'verified'), broken references to its own governance files, and was assembled in a single ~90-minute commit that reads as generated filler rather than an actual community-vetted index. It does not itself help find people, coordinate volunteers, distribute aid, or provide any service to earthquake victims — it only links to other teams' (unverified) work.

name: Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
**¿Qué construiste?**
Un bot de Telegram en español —@DEV_VEZbot— que les da a los venezolanos información de emergencia clara (refugios, agua segura, salud, ayuda humanitaria) con solo chatear en español sencillo. Sin app, sin registro.

Por dentro es un agente de IA construido sobre surfcall (de Gecko): una capa que hace que cualquier API sea utilizable por un agente de IA sin escribir código de integración. surfcall lee la API de SOS Venezuela 2026, la convierte en las herramientas adecuadas y hace la llamada correcta al primer intento, así las respuestas vienen de datos en vivo, no de suposiciones.

**¿Para quién es?**
Para cualquier persona en Venezuela que necesite información de emergencia rápido y no sea desarrollador, llegando a donde ya está (Telegram), en el idioma que habla (español). Y para el proyecto SOS Venezuela: una puerta de entrada sin fricción a sus datos.

**¿Cómo ayuda?**

* Convierte una API para desarrolladores en algo que una persona común puede simplemente preguntar.
* Respuestas en tiempo real a partir de los datos de SOS Venezuela 2026.
* Gratis, sin instalación, sin registro: cualquier teléfono con Telegram.
* Código abierto (MIT). También aportamos mejoras a la API de SOS Venezuela (una especificación OpenAPI + sugerencias de endpoints) para que otros también puedan construir sobre ella.
* Reutilizable para cualquier fuente: trae una API o un conjunto de datos y lo hacemos utilizable por agentes: con un clic si tiene OpenAPI, o lo integramos a mano. SOS Venezuela es solo la primera.

**¿Qué deberían probar primero?**
Abre t.me/DEV_VEZbot y pregunta, en español:

* "¿Dónde hay un refugio cerca de mí?"
* "¿El agua de mi zona es segura para tomar?"
* "¿Cómo pido o doy ayuda?"

analysis.summary: A Spanish-first Telegram bot that acts as an AI agent (Claude/OpenRouter tool-use loop) over the public read-only 'SOS Venezuela 2026' earthquake-response API, using the author's own published 'gecko-surf' library to auto-generate typed tools from the API's OpenAPI spec. Paired with a Next.js marketing landing page. The bot itself is a thin, well-tested, defensively-coded consumer layer rather than the data platform (which lives in a separate repo).
analysis.project_type: Telegram bot (AI agent) + Next.js marketing landing page

ai_ml: OpenRouter free LLM (Llama 3.3 70B) as default provider

architecture.pattern: Thin consumer/adapter service — Telegram transport layer -> agent tool-use loop -> allow-listed API tool wrapper (SurfcallTools) -> external SOS Venezuela public API via gecko-surf client. Separate, decoupled Next.js static landing page.
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database; the bot is stateless (in-memory per-process rate limiter only) and reads from a remote read-only public API via the gecko-surf client library.
architecture.api_design: No inbound HTTP API from the bot itself (long-polling Telegram, no open ports); an OpenAPI spec for the upstream SOS Venezuela API is vendored at bot/spec/sosvenezuela_openapi.json and consumed via gecko-surf. Landing page is a static Next.js app.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: bot.py (Telegram transport, pure/testable handlers), agent.py (LLM tool-use loop, provider-agnostic), surfcall_tools.py (safety boundary: allow-list, sanitize, cap), providers.py (Anthropic/OpenRouter adapter), config.py (env-only secrets, system prompt).
architecture.modularity: High for its scope — small, single-responsibility modules with dependency injection (responder/clock/llm/tools all injectable) enabling offline unit testing without network or real credentials.
notable_patterns: Explicit read-only tool allow-list (5 named operations) as a prompt-injection/safety boundary, enforced in code not just prompt
Provider abstraction shimming OpenAI-compatible (OpenRouter) responses into the Anthropic Messages shape so one agent loop works with either provider
Never-raises design at every boundary (tool calls, message handling) degrading to a friendly Spanish fallback instead of crashing
Bounded agent loop (max_iters) to prevent infinite tool-call loops
Structural JSON truncation (_cap) that drops trailing list items to stay under a char budget while remaining valid JSON, rather than naive byte-truncation
Per-user sliding-window rate limiter to protect upstream API budget and LLM spend

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear top-level split: bot/ (Python agent), app/components/lib (Next.js landing), tests/, .github/workflows — logical and small enough to navigate instantly.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive names (SurfcallTools, RateLimiter, handle_message, resolve_command) with Spanish user-facing strings clearly segregated as constants.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Strong module-level docstrings explaining intent/design rationale in every bot/*.py file plus a detailed bilingual README and a bot/README.md; comments explain 'why' not just 'what'.
code_organization.reasoning: Small but exceptionally well-organized codebase for its size, with docstrings that explicitly document design decisions and safety rationale — well above typical hackathon quality.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Secrets read only from environment variables (TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, OPENROUTER_API_KEY/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY); .env is gitignored; error messages name only the missing var, never a value, to avoid leaking secrets.
production_readiness.security_notes: No end-user auth needed since it only reads a public no-auth API; the real security control is the code-enforced 5-operation allow-list plus an explicit prompt-injection defense (tool results are data, not instructions) and privacy masking of cédulas/coords/minors deferred to the upstream API. No rate limiting beyond per-user in-memory (resets on restart, not distributed) and no logging/observability for abuse detection or incident response.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Long-polling single-process Telegram bot with in-memory rate-limit state — fine for hackathon/demo scale but won't horizontally scale or survive restarts gracefully (state loss); no persistence, no queue, no DB. Landing page is on Vercel (static, scales fine).
production_readiness.reasoning: Docker image, CI (lint/type/test for Python + lint/build for Next.js), and defensive error handling are real production hygiene, but there is no logging/observability, no persistent state, no horizontal-scale story, and it depends entirely on an external sibling API/library ('SOS Venezuela 2026', 'gecko-surf') whose own production-readiness is out of scope/unknown.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working logic — not boilerplate. Small (~1250 LOC Python across bot+tests) but functionally complete for its narrow scope, with genuine unit tests exercising rate limiting, command routing, message chunking, the tool allow-list, and JSON capping.
maturity.reasoning: 22 commits, essentially one real developer (plus Vercel/v0 bot commits for the landing scaffold), built in ~2 days around a hackathon; CI actually lints/type-checks/tests both the Python bot and Next.js landing, which is more rigor than most hackathon repos show.

viability.reasoning: Functionally it is a working MVP with real tests and defensible safety design, but it delivers value only by proxy of a separate, unverified 'SOS Venezuela 2026' data platform and a personal PyPI package (gecko-surf) that this review did not audit for correctness/uptime; without that upstream API being genuinely live and reliable, the bot has nothing to answer with, capping standalone viability for disaster response.

key_features: Spanish-first (auto-detects and replies in the user's language) Telegram bot for querying earthquake emergency data
AI agent tool-use loop with pluggable LLM provider (free OpenRouter Llama 3.3 70B by default, Claude Haiku 4.5 opt-in)
Hard-coded allow-list of 5 read-only operations (search persons, get reports, person stats, recent damage, news) as a security boundary
Per-user sliding-window rate limiting
Telegram message chunking for long replies, command shortcuts (/buscar, /cifras, /reportes, /noticias)
Structural JSON response capping to keep large API payloads within LLM context while remaining valid JSON
Dockerized bot worker; separate Next.js marketing landing page deployed to Vercel
analysis.merge_potential: Could be merged/integrated with other Build4Venezuela repos as the conversational/Telegram front-end layer, provided it points at a shared canonical 'SOS Venezuela' data API; its OpenAPI spec and allow-list pattern could be reused by other bots/agents needing safe read-only access to the same humanitarian data.
red_flags: Entire product value depends on an external, unaudited API ('SOS Venezuela 2026') and a personally-published PyPI dependency ('gecko-surf') from the same author — single point of failure and unverified reliability/maintenance
No logging/observability or persistent rate-limit/audit state — a restart silently resets abuse protection and there's no way to detect misuse or outages in production
Single-maintainer project (one real human contributor) built in ~2 days — bus-factor and long-term maintenance risk for a tool meant to serve real disaster victims
No authentication/authorization needed only because the bot is read-only against a public API; if that upstream API's own access controls are weak, this bot inherits that risk

diffusion.recommended_angle: Pregúntale a un bot de Telegram en español dónde hay refugio o si el agua es segura para tomar — sin descargar apps, sin registrarte, solo escribe a @DEV_VEZbot.
gaps: No independent verification that the actual Telegram bot (@DEV_VEZbot) responds correctly in real time — only the landing page was checked live
Entire value depends on an external, unaudited 'SOS Venezuela 2026' API and the author's own PyPI package (gecko-surf), a single point of failure
No screenshots of the actual chat conversation included in assets; only a landing page and video
Single-maintainer project built in ~2 days with no logging/observability, so misuse or downtime would go undetected

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Landing page at https://ayuda-venezuela-wine.vercel.app/ loads correctly and is a real, presentable marketing page (not broken/boilerplate). It explains the value prop clearly, links directly to the live Telegram bot (@DEV_VEZbot), links to GitHub, and explains the underlying 'gecko-surf' agent tooling. The actual bot conversation flow itself was not tested via chat (WebFetch only reviewed the landing page), so end-to-end victim usability is unverified but plausible given the codebase's genuine unit tests around message handling.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Ayuda Venezuela: escríbele a un bot de Telegram en español y obtén al instante refugios, agua segura y ayuda humanitaria — sin apps, sin registro.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Access to timely, low-friction emergency information (shelter locations, water safety, humanitarian aid contacts) is a genuine, urgent need for earthquake victims, and reaching people where they already are (Telegram, in plain Spanish, no app install/registration) removes real adoption barriers compared to a dedicated app or web dashboard. However, the project is a thin conversational wrapper around a separate 'SOS Venezuela 2026' data API that this review did not independently verify for liveness/accuracy — so it solves the access/UX problem but its life-saving value is fully contingent on data it doesn't own or control.

name: AyudaVE — Red P2P de Coordinación Humanitaria
db_description:
¿Qué construimos?

Construimos AyudaVE, una red P2P de coordinación humanitaria para Venezuela que conecta personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, dinero, cripto, transporte o tiempo con centros de acopio verificados que realmente lo necesitan.

AyudaVE permite que cualquier persona pueda ayudar en menos de un minuto, sin registro obligatorio para donar, buscar centros o reportar información. Los centros de acopio pueden administrar sus necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, solicitudes de voluntarios/transporte y actualizaciones.

La idea central es simple:

No se trata solo de donar. Se trata de saber qué falta, dónde falta, quién lo necesita, quién lo dona, quién lo transporta y si realmente llegó.

¿Qué problema resuelve?

Después de una emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria suele quedar dispersa en WhatsApp, redes sociales, formularios, grupos y listas manuales. Esto genera varios problemas:

* Duplicación de esfuerzos.
* Centros saturados con ciertos insumos.
* Falta de visibilidad sobre necesidades reales.
* Información desactualizada.
* Riesgo de fraude.
* Poca claridad sobre si la ayuda realmente llegó.
* Personas queriendo ayudar, pero sin saber a dónde ir o qué llevar.

AyudaVE organiza esa coordinación en una plataforma simple, rápida y mobile-first, enfocada en conectar ayuda real con necesidades reales.

¿Para quién es?

AyudaVE está pensada para:

* Personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, insumos, dinero, cripto o transporte.
* Voluntarios que quieren saber dónde ayudar.
* Transportistas que pueden mover ayuda entre zonas.
* Centros de acopio que necesitan administrar inventario, necesidades y horarios.
* Verificadores que pueden confirmar qué centros están activos.
* Comunidades, iglesias, colegios, ONGs, instituciones y grupos ciudadanos organizando ayuda en campo.

¿Cómo funciona?

La plataforma tiene varios flujos principales:

1. Quiero ayudar
    La persona indica su ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puede aportar. La app le muestra centros cercanos y necesidades urgentes.
2. Quiero donar
    El usuario puede donar insumos físicos, comida, agua, medicinas, Pago Móvil, transferencia o cripto. También puede reportar su donación con evidencia.
3. Quiero ser voluntario
    Los voluntarios pueden registrarse, indicar sus habilidades y apoyar con transporte, clasificación, verificación, recolección o difusión.
4. Soy un centro de acopio
    Los centros pueden administrar stock, necesidades urgentes, métodos de donación, horarios, actualizaciones, solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
5. Mapa y necesidades urgentes
    Los usuarios pueden explorar centros cercanos, filtrar por estado/municipio/parroquia y ver qué se necesita con mayor urgencia.
6. Trazabilidad de donaciones
    La meta es que cada donación pueda pasar por estados como: reportada, pendiente de confirmar, confirmada por el centro, usada/entregada y evidenciada.

¿Qué deberían probar primero?

1. Entrar a:
    https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/
2. Tocar “Quiero ayudar” o “Quiero donar”.
3. Elegir una ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puedes aportar.
4. Revisar centros de acopio y necesidades urgentes.
5. Probar el flujo de registrar una donación o crear un centro de acopio.
6. Desde el rol de centro, probar la administración de necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, voluntarios y transporte.

Diferencial

AyudaVE no busca ser solo otro mapa de centros de acopio.

La visión es convertirla en una capa operativa P2P que conecte donantes, voluntarios, transportistas y centros verificados usando:

* Inventario en tiempo real.
* Necesidades urgentes.
* Centros verificados.
* Métodos de donación.
* Solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
* Trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Evidencia de recepción y entrega.

Integración con otros proyectos del hackathon

Queremos integrarnos con una API centralizada del hackathon para consumir y compartir datos de centros, necesidades, stock, donaciones, voluntarios y reportes.

AyudaVE puede funcionar como la interfaz P2P operativa para que la data de otros proyectos se convierta en acción:

qué llevar, a dónde llevarlo, quién lo recibe y si fue confirmado.

Qué necesita el equipo

Necesitamos data real y actualizada de centros de acopio: ubicación, responsables, horarios, contactos, métodos de donación, inventario disponible, necesidades urgentes y capacidad de recepción.

También necesitamos voluntarios/verificadores en campo para confirmar qué centros están activos, qué insumos realmente necesitan y qué ayuda ya llegó.

Además, buscamos apoyo para:

* Validación con centros de acopio reales.
* Integración con APIs centralizadas del hackathon.
* Mejoras UX/UI mobile-first.
* Integración con WhatsApp.
* Automatización de inventario y stock mínimo.
* Sistema de trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Difusión con comunidades, aliados e instituciones.

Estado actual

AyudaVE ya está operativa en:

https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/

La plataforma cuenta con flujos para donantes, voluntarios, centros de acopio, mapa, necesidades, métodos de pago, inventario, actualizaciones y administración/verificación.

architecture.pattern: Monolithic Node.js server using native http module (no framework), custom router mapping 'METHOD /path' strings to handlers, vanilla JS SPA frontend served statically
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw parameterized SQL via a thin pluggable driver abstraction (store.js selects store-pg.js or store-sqlite.js based on DATABASE_URL); consistent use of '?' placeholders converted to $N for Postgres, no ORM but no evident SQL injection since all params bound
architecture.api_design: Custom REST-like API defined as an object literal keyed by 'METHOD /path/:param' strings dispatched by a manual router; JSON responses, consistent auth/ownership checks per-handler (authUser, ownsCenter, isAdmin)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable: store.js (persistence)/storage.js (file storage, local vs GCS)/import-*.js (external data ingestion per source)/text-normalize.js+person-search.js (search)/server.js (routes+business logic) are split into single-purpose files, though server.js itself is a large 870-line file mixing routing, auth, and business logic
architecture.modularity: Import scripts for each external data source (acopio, sismos, edificios, supplies, directorio, reportes) are modular and documented (docs/INTEGRACIONES.md gives a template for adding new sources in ~30 lines), optional extension module loaded via try/require for private/paid features
notable_patterns: Environment-based strategy pattern for DB (SQLite dev / Postgres prod) and storage (local disk / GCS) selected purely by presence of env vars
PIN-based auth with scrypt + pepper + per-user salt and timing-safe compare, session tokens in DB
Rate limiting and login-attempt lockout implemented manually
PII minimization: public API projects only an allowlisted field set for person records, contact info revealed separately; a commit explicitly removed personal-data handling for protection
Graceful DB init retry loop with backoff, uncaughtException/unhandledRejection handlers, /healthz endpoint for orchestration

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear top-level separation: server.js (core), store*.js (DB), storage.js (files), import-*.js (per-source ingestion), public/ (frontend), deploy/ (systemd+Caddy+backup scripts), docs/ (extensive technical and user docs), scripts/ (ops helpers)
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish/English mixed naming (import-sismos.js, import-edificios.js, person-search.js); route table keys are self-documenting
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually thorough for a hackathon project: README, DOCUMENTACION.md (12 chapters), INTEGRACIONES.md (how to add a data source), DESARROLLO.md, DESPLIEGUE.md, MANUAL-USUARIO.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, plus generated PDFs and an architecture diagram
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized single-repo Node app with heavy but genuine inline comments explaining security/privacy decisions, and one of the more complete documentation sets seen in this class of project, though server.js itself is a large single file mixing many concerns.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: env vars only (.env.example provided, no secrets committed); PIN_PEPPER required in production (throws at startup if missing when DATABASE_URL set); admins.example.json/config.example.json provided as templates, real admins.json/config.json kept out of repo/in data/
production_readiness.security_notes: scrypt+salt+pepper PIN hashing with timing-safe compare, per-endpoint ownership/role checks (ownsCenter/isAdmin), rate limiting + login lockout, PII allowlisting on public person endpoints (explicit commit removing personal-data exposure), safeUrl() guards against javascript:/data: URL XSS; however auth is phone+4-digit-PIN only (weak entropy, mitigated by lockout) and there is no automated test suite validating these paths
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Designed for Cloud Run: stateless container, Postgres via Cloud SQL with connection pool, GCS for images, health endpoint, retry-on-init DB connection; also has a turnkey single-VM systemd+Caddy deployment path as fallback — dual deployment story is a strength for a disaster-response tool needing resilience
production_readiness.reasoning: Has real auth, input validation/sanitization, rate limiting, structured error handling (err() helper + try/catch per route), startup resilience, and two working deployment paths (Cloud Run/Docker and VM+systemd+Caddy with backup script), but lacks automated tests/CI which is a real gap for a system handling emergency data.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: Live production deployment (ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com) built in direct response to a real earthquake event (La Guaira, June 2026), multiple contributors with merged PRs addressing real security/privacy concerns (PII removal commit), extensive documentation and deploy tooling — but very young repo (8 commits over ~4 days) with no automated tests or CI pipeline.

viability.reasoning: This is not a hackathon toy but an already-live humanitarian data aggregator responding to a real Venezuelan earthquake, with a working auth/donation/center-management/inventory system, multiple external data-source integrations, and a real deployment story on Cloud Run and VM; main risks are lack of automated tests, a single primary maintainer, and reliance on many third-party open data sources of uncertain long-term stability.

key_features: Aggregated map of acopio (collection) centers with needs, contact, and status, sourced/synced from multiple external open-data APIs with deduplication
Center donation/inventory logistics module (entradas/salidas/beneficiarios) with per-category weight and stock tracking
Phone+PIN authenticated accounts with center ownership/admin roles and session tokens
Missing-persons module with fuzzy/accent-tolerant name search (later restricted to remove personal data exposure per commit history)
Live seismic replica feed via USGS import
Affected-buildings, service-outage (luz/agua/medicinas) and emergency directory (hospitals/firefighters) aggregation from community sources
Admin analytics dashboard (time series of views, signups, donations, centers) and moderation/approval queue for new centers
Swappable persistence (SQLite/Postgres) and storage (local disk/GCS) selected purely via env vars, plus dual deploy paths (Docker/Cloud Run and VM/systemd/Caddy)
analysis.merge_potential: "Strong standalone candidate rather than a merge target; its aid-logistics/inventory, shelter-mapping, and multi-source data aggregation modules are reusable building blocks other projects (e.g. pure seismic-data or people-finder tools) could integrate with via its documented import-<source>.js pattern or consume via its public API."
red_flags: No automated tests or CI pipeline despite handling emergency-critical and semi-sensitive data
Authentication relies solely on phone number + 4-digit PIN (low entropy, though mitigated by lockout/rate-limiting)
Very young project (created 2026-06-27, only 8 commits) with a single primary maintainer, raising bus-factor risk for something meant to run in production during an ongoing disaster response

Heavy dependence on numerous third-party community data sources (acopiovenezuela.vercel.app, terremotovenezuela.com, redayudavenezuela.com, reporte-ve, etc.) whose availability/accuracy the project does not control
Optional AI photo-search feature (Gemini) and Google Maps key are unauthenticated-by-default externally callable integrations that need careful key/quota management in production
analysis.summary: AyudaVE is a live, actively deployed humanitarian information hub (ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com) built in direct response to the June 2026 La Guaira earthquake in Venezuela. It's a Node.js (no framework) monolith with a vanilla-JS SPA frontend, swappable SQLite/Postgres persistence and local/GCS file storage, real phone+PIN auth with scrypt hashing, per-endpoint authorization, rate limiting, and a genuinely useful feature set: collection-center mapping with donation/inventory logistics, missing-persons search (later restricted for privacy), seismic replica feeds, affected-buildings and service-outage aggregation from multiple open data sources, and an admin analytics dashboard. It ships two real deployment paths (Docker/Cloud Run and VM+systemd+Caddy) and unusually extensive documentation, but has zero automated tests/CI and is very young with a small contributor base.
analysis.project_type: "Full-stack web application (humanitarian aid aggregation platform) — Node.js backend + vanilla JS frontend"

backend: undici (HTTP client for external data imports)
ai_ml: Google Gemini API (optional photo-based person search)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After the June 2026 La Guaira earthquake, humanitarian aid coordination in Venezuela was scattered across WhatsApp groups, social media, and manual lists, causing duplicated donations, saturated centers with some supplies while lacking others, outdated info, and fraud risk. AyudaVE directly targets this coordination failure by aggregating collection centers, urgent needs, inventory, donation methods, and volunteer/transport requests into one mobile-first platform, with no forced registration for basic actions like donating or finding a center.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/ returns HTTP 200 and serves a real SPA (Leaflet map, share widgets, Venezuela-flag branding). Verified backend is live and functional: /healthz returns {"ok":true} and /api/centers returns real, structured JSON data for dozens of aggregated collection centers (name, location by estado/municipio, verification status, itemized needs like agua/comida/medicinas, source attribution to acopiovenezuela.vercel.app, etc.). This is a working product with real backend data, not just a static landing page.

diffusion.recommended_angle: A live, working P2P humanitarian coordination platform for the Venezuela earthquake response — built and deployed in days, already aggregating real collection-center and needs data, with donors able to find where to help in under a minute, no signup required.
gaps: No confirmed real-user/beneficiary testimonials or usage metrics yet
Data currently sourced/aggregated from third-party community feeds rather than independently verified in-house, which should be disclosed
No automated tests/CI, and single primary maintainer — note as a caveat if promoted as production-critical infrastructure
Screenshots of the live UI (map, donate flow, center admin panel) would strengthen any social post beyond the JSON/API evidence gathered here

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AyudaVE conecta en menos de un minuto a quienes quieren ayudar con los centros de acopio que de verdad lo necesitan tras el terremoto en Venezuela — ya en vivo, con datos reales.

name: Beacon Rescate
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?
"Rescatista": Un sistema de triangulación en tiempo real diseñado para entornos de desastre. Utiliza escaneo de protocolos de red (ARP/Wi-Fi) y métricas de latencia para localizar dispositivos móviles sin necesidad de infraestructura de red previa.

## ¿Para quién es? ¿Cómo ayuda?
Diseñado para equipos de búsqueda y rescate en zonas sin cobertura. Permite localizar víctimas basándose en la señal activa de sus dispositivos (smartphones), proporcionando una ubicación visual en un radar en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?
1. Iniciar el escaneo en el entorno de pruebas.
2. Observar cómo el sistema filtra el "ruido" y unifica la señal del objetivo en el radar.
3. Verificar la persistencia de datos en la base de datos local (SQLite).

architecture.pattern: Single-file Kivy mobile app (no MVC/layered separation)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw sqlite3 (stdlib), local file DB `local_rescate_unificado.db`, single table `mapa_rescate`
architecture.api_design: None - no HTTP/REST API; app talks directly to local SQLite and native BLE/Wi-Fi APIs
architecture.separation_of_concerns: None - UI (Kivy widgets/App class), scanning logic, distance math, and DB access are all mixed in one 301-line main.py
architecture.modularity: No modules/packages; everything is top-level classes and functions in a single file
notable_patterns: Callback-based bridging to Android native BLE via pyjnius (autoclass)
Passive Wi-Fi 'scanning' via brute-force ping sweep over subnet using shell subprocess
Kivy Clock.schedule_interval for polling loops
RSSI/ping-to-distance heuristic with a moving-average smoothing buffer
