production_readiness.scalability_notes: Serverless functions + Supabase should scale reasonably for a hackathon MVP, but geo queries (nearby.js/community-alerts.js) do full haversine computation client-side in JS over fetched rows rather than using PostGIS server-side, which won't scale well with many users/alerts despite PostGIS being provisioned.
production_readiness.reasoning: Has real auth, RLS-backed authorization, env-based secret management, and multiple deploy configs (Dockerfile, render.yaml, netlify.toml), putting it above prototype level, but lacks CI/CD, structured logging/monitoring, and includes a questionable MAC-spoofing feature — not yet production-grade for a safety-critical deployment.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 47 commits over ~5 months with substantive, non-trivial logic (mesh transport adapters, offline sync, RLS policies, geo-fenced alerts) — this is genuinely developed, not scaffolding — but testing is minimal (only the default React Native template test survives, no tests for web/, netlify functions, or SQL) and there is no CI pipeline.

viability.reasoning: A working MVP with real mesh-networking and geo-alert logic that plausibly helps in connectivity-poor disaster scenarios (matches Venezuela earthquake use case for offline family reunification and community alerts), but it is not Venezuela-specific, lacks tests/CI/monitoring, has an unresolved product identity split (Dommedag vs iFoundYou), and includes a MAC-spoofing feature that raises trust/safety concerns for a deployment meant to help real victims.

key_features: Live map (MapLibre) showing self + nearby friends via geo-fenced Supabase queries
Off-grid Meshtastic mesh networking (BLE + HTTP transports) for communication without internet
Group and 1:1 chat with offline outbox/sync (IndexedDB) and multiple pluggable transports (mesh, Bluetooth Multipeer, internet)
Community/emergency alerts (water, food, medical, shelter, lost person) broadcast to nearby users with radius + expiry
Friend requests/discovery with privacy-preserving contact matching via a Postgres SECURITY DEFINER function
React Native mobile companion app (iOS/Android) alongside the PWA web app
Local Python companion server for WiFi scanning, device discovery, and MAC address spoofing
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate to merge with shelter-mapping or aid-logistics projects: its offline mesh chat/alert layer (Meshtastic + Supabase realtime + geo-fenced community alerts) could serve as the comms backbone for other Build4Venezuela tools that need connectivity in disaster zones, while its people-finder/friend-location features complement family-reunification-focused apps.
red_flags: No CI pipeline and near-zero automated test coverage beyond a default RN boilerplate test
Ships a MAC-address-spoofing tool (wifi/mac_spoofer.py) as a first-class feature, which is a dual-use/security-evasion capability inappropriate for a trust-sensitive emergency safety app
Project identity confusion: internal package/app name 'Dommedag' vs public name 'iFoundYou' suggests an unfinished rebrand and possible scope drift
Repo root cluttered with ad hoc AI research notes (Loopt research, mesh messaging research .md files) indicating exploratory, not fully curated, development
Client-side haversine distance filtering instead of using the already-provisioned PostGIS for geo queries, which will not scale
analysis.summary: iFoundYou (internally named "Dommedag") is a multi-platform emergency location-sharing and off-grid mesh communication app built with a Vite/React PWA, a React Native mobile app, Netlify serverless functions, and a Supabase (Postgres+PostGIS+RLS) backend, plus experimental Meshtastic BLE/HTTP mesh transports for connectivity-free messaging and geo-fenced community alerts. It is a genuinely implemented working MVP (RLS-secured auth, transport abstraction layers, offline sync) rather than boilerplate, but lacks CI/tests, has scalability shortcuts (client-side geo math despite PostGIS), unresolved rebranding artifacts, and a concerning built-in MAC-spoofing utility that undermines trust for a safety-critical deployment.
analysis.project_type: Cross-platform emergency location-sharing / offline mesh communication app (PWA + React Native mobile + serverless backend)

gaps: No demo video to show the mesh/map/chat features working since the live site is a JS SPA that appears blank/minimal without login
Unresolved branding: internal name 'Dommedag' (Norwegian for 'doomsday') vs public 'iFoundYou' — confusing for a social post
No screenshots captured showing actual UI (map, alerts, chat)
MAC-spoofing utility is a reputational red flag if surfaced in promo
Not Venezuela-specific in current framing
diffusion.recommended_angle: If promoted, frame narrowly around the offline mesh communication + geo-fenced community alerts feature for disaster zones without internet, and get a screen-recorded demo/video first

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL returns 200 with a legitimate Vite/React SPA shell (title 'Dommedag — Mesh Guardian', meta description mentions real-time location sharing and emergency mesh messaging, proper PWA manifest/icons). This is not a broken deploy, but it is a client-rendered app requiring JS execution (and likely Supabase auth/login) to reveal any real functionality — a plain fetch/crawl only sees an empty '#app' div, so a quick visitor or non-technical judge/donor would see little to nothing without actually running the JS and going through onboarding.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Dommedag (iFoundYou): a real off-grid mesh network for family reunification and community alerts when the internet goes down after an earthquake.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Loss of connectivity and difficulty locating/reuniting with family is a genuine, high-severity problem after major earthquakes (cell towers down, congested networks). This project directly targets that with real, non-trivial engineering: Meshtastic BLE/HTTP transport abstractions, geo-fenced Supabase/PostGIS community alerts (water, food, medical, shelter, lost person), offline outbox/sync, and RLS-secured friend/location matching — a genuinely built MVP (47 commits, ~14.4k LOC) rather than boilerplate. However it is not Venezuela-specific (built/registered under Peru in the hackathon metadata), has an unresolved rebrand (Dommedag vs iFoundYou) suggesting confused product identity, and ships a MAC-address-spoofing tool that is inappropriate/risky for a trust-critical safety app.

name: Crisis Map
db_description:
# CrisisMap

**CrisisMap** es una aplicación colaborativa de respuesta a desastres diseñada para ayudar a ciudadanos, voluntarios, organizaciones humanitarias y equipos de emergencia a recopilar y compartir información crítica durante terremotos, inundaciones y otras situaciones de crisis.

El proyecto está construido bajo un enfoque **offline-first**, permitiendo que cualquier persona pueda reportar incidentes como edificios colapsados, carreteras bloqueadas, personas atrapadas, hospitales, refugios, puntos de distribución de alimentos, agua o medicamentos y cortes de servicios básicos, incluso cuando no existe conexión a Internet. Cada reporte se almacena localmente en el dispositivo y se sincroniza automáticamente cuando la conectividad regresa, permitiendo que cada teléfono funcione como un nodo temporal de recolección de información durante las horas más críticas de una emergencia.

A diferencia de plataformas orientadas exclusivamente a organismos oficiales, **CrisisMap fue diseñado pensando primero en el ciudadano común**. La aplicación utiliza un lenguaje sencillo, categorías fáciles de entender y una interfaz intuitiva para que cualquier persona pueda colaborar sin necesidad de conocer protocolos técnicos o clasificaciones especializadas. La intención es reducir al máximo la barrera de participación para que la mayor cantidad posible de personas pueda aportar información útil. Posteriormente, esos reportes pueden ser revisados, validados, enriquecidos y utilizados por organizaciones humanitarias o autoridades mediante paneles de monitoreo y herramientas de análisis.

La aplicación fue desarrollada como una **Progressive Web App (PWA)** para facilitar su distribución y permitir una rápida adopción durante una emergencia. Basta con acceder a un enlace para instalarla, sin depender de Google Play o App Store, funcionando en Android, iPhone, Windows, Linux y cualquier dispositivo con un navegador moderno. Como parte de su funcionamiento, la aplicación descarga automáticamente la cartografía de la zona afectada para mantener el mapa disponible sin conexión, permitiendo consultar información, registrar nuevos incidentes y sincronizar los cambios cuando la conectividad se restablezca. Este enfoque busca minimizar las barreras de distribución y garantizar que la aplicación siga siendo útil incluso cuando la infraestructura de comunicaciones se encuentra degradada.

La visión del proyecto es construir una red distribuida de dispositivos capaces de recopilar información directamente desde el terreno y consolidarla progresivamente conforme la conectividad se restablece. En una etapa posterior, la información recopilada podrá alimentar paneles de monitoreo para autoridades y organizaciones humanitarias, donde los reportes ciudadanos se validen, se complementen con otras fuentes de información y apoyen la toma de decisiones, la priorización de recursos y la coordinación de la respuesta ante desastres.

Desde el punto de vista de ingeniería, CrisisMap fue desarrollado priorizando la **entrega rápida de valor**. Las decisiones de arquitectura y organización del código buscaron mantener una **arquitectura mínima viable**, suficientemente robusta para soportar la evolución del proyecto, pero evitando niveles de abstracción, patrones o complejidad que ralentizaran el desarrollo inicial. Algunas decisiones representan compromisos conscientes entre velocidad de implementación y mantenibilidad a largo plazo, entendiendo que, en un contexto de emergencia, disponer rápidamente de una herramienta funcional genera un impacto mayor que perseguir una arquitectura perfecta desde el primer día. La evolución natural del proyecto contempla incorporar progresivamente procesos de refactorización, paneles de administración, capacidades analíticas y enriquecimiento de datos conforme aumente su adopción y madurez. Este enfoque es consistente con prácticas ágiles que promueven una **Minimum Viable Architecture (MVA)**: una base arquitectónica lo suficientemente sólida para evolucionar sin caer en la sobreingeniería durante las primeras etapas del producto.

analysis.summary: CrisisMap is a real, non-trivial offline-first PWA for crowdsourced disaster incident reporting (earthquakes/floods context, Spanish-first), with an Angular 21 SPA frontend using Leaflet + IndexedDB for offline sync, and a serverless AWS backend (API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB + S3/CloudFront) provisioned via CDK. It is deployed live to Netlify with a working production URL and has 71 commits of substantive, incremental feature/fix work rather than boilerplate.
analysis.project_type: Offline-first crowdsourced crisis/incident reporting PWA with serverless AWS backend

database: DynamoDB (Incidents, Confirmations, Devices tables with GSIs)

architecture.pattern: Serverless microservice-per-route (Lambda) backend + offline-first SPA frontend with local-first sync engine
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw AWS SDK v3 (@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb DynamoDBDocumentClient) via a small shared db.ts helper (getItem/putItem/updateItem), no ORM
architecture.api_design: REST-ish JSON over API Gateway HTTP API, one Lambda handler per resource (incidents, confirmations, devices, images, sync, contact, seed, legend, resources, health), presigned S3 URLs for image upload
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation: apps/web (frontend, organized by feature folders: core/map/report/incident/banner/etc.), backend/lambdas (route handlers), backend/shared (db, geo, types, headers, constants), backend/cdk (infra as code)
architecture.modularity: Good modularity for an MVP scale; shared utilities (geohashing, validation, sanitize) are factored out and reused across lambdas
notable_patterns: Offline-first outbox/sync engine with exponential backoff (30s-30m) in frontend
Geohash-based duplicate incident detection using haversine distance
Demo mode with per-device quota enforced server-side via DynamoDB atomic counter
Device-based pseudonymous identity (deviceId header) instead of user accounts/auth
Token-gated seed endpoint (X-Seed-Token) for admin/demo data loading
CORS locked to specific Netlify origin, API Gateway throttling (rateLimit 100/burst 200) configured in CDK

code_organization.directory_structure: Well-organized monorepo with npm workspaces (apps/web, backend, backend/cdk), feature-folder structure on frontend, resource-folder structure on backend lambdas, plus heavy but organized 'spec-driven' scaffolding (.specify/, specs/, .opencode/) from a spec-kit workflow tool
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming throughout (e.g., sync-engine.service.ts, incident-layer.service.ts, create-incident.ts)
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is unusually thorough (bilingual product narrative + architecture diagram + repo layout + offline-sync table); AGENTS.md and specs/ directory contain detailed spec/plan/task docs from spec-driven development
code_organization.reasoning: Clear, consistent structure and naming with genuine, detailed documentation beyond typical hackathon README fluff; loses a point only because a large fraction of repo size is meta-tooling scaffolding (.specify/.opencode) unrelated to the product itself.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Secrets (SEED_TOKEN, CONTACT_EMAIL, CONTACT_APP_PASSWORD) are read from process.env and injected via CDK context/env vars, not hardcoded; no .env files committed to repo
production_readiness.security_notes: No user authentication (by design, anonymous device-based reporting) which is reasonable for the use case; security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, nosniff) set on responses; input validation and sanitization present in Lambda handlers (length caps, type/enum validation, control-char stripping); CORS restricted to a single known origin; seed/admin endpoint gated by a shared-secret token (weaker than proper auth but functional); no rate limiting beyond API Gateway's blanket throttle (no per-device abuse protection against spam incident creation besides duplicate-detection and demo quotas).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Serverless/DynamoDB architecture scales reasonably well for expected crisis-reporting load; geohash-based queries and GSIs suggest awareness of query patterns; single-table-ish design per entity is workable but not battle-tested at scale.
production_readiness.reasoning: Deployed to production (Netlify + presumably live AWS backend), real error handling and input validation exist, but there is no structured logging/observability beyond console.log, no automated tests running in CI, and no CI/CD pipeline (.github workflows) found at all, which caps readiness for a life-critical deployment.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, actively developed application with substantial custom business logic (offline sync, geohash duplicate detection, demo-mode quotas, image compression/upload pipeline) — not boilerplate or AI scaffolding
maturity.reasoning: 71 commits of genuine feature/bugfix work, live deployment, and detailed docs indicate real engineering effort; however, despite jest being configured for the backend and Angular's test tooling present, there are effectively zero real tests (only the untouched Angular CLI-generated app.spec.ts 'Hello world' test) and no CI workflow exists in the repo, so quality is unverified by automation.

viability.reasoning: This is one of the more production-grade entries: it solves a concrete, well-scoped problem (offline-capable citizen incident reporting during earthquakes) with a coherent, deployed, working architecture; main gaps before real-world reliance are lack of automated tests/CI, no abuse/spam protections beyond basic heuristics, and no admin/moderation dashboard for validating citizen reports (acknowledged as future work in the README itself).

key_features: Offline-first incident reporting with local IndexedDB storage and background sync with exponential backoff
Interactive Leaflet map with marker clustering and distance-sorted incident list
Photo attachments (up to 3) compressed client-side and uploaded via S3 presigned URLs, served via CloudFront
Geohash-based duplicate incident detection within a configurable radius
Anonymous device-based identity with alias, no login/registration required
Demo mode with per-device server-enforced report quota
i18n support (Spanish, English, Portuguese)
PWA installable app with forced-update/version-check mechanism and offline/online status banners
Contact form wired to Gmail SMTP with CloudWatch logging fallback
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone product; could integrate with a shelter-mapping or resource-coordination tool by exposing its incidents API/GSIs as a data feed, or by adding a validation/moderation dashboard for humanitarian orgs as described in its own roadmap.
red_flags: No CI/CD pipeline (.github/workflows) despite having test tooling configured
No real automated test coverage — jest configured but zero test files in backend; frontend has only the default unmodified Angular CLI scaffold test
Admin/seed endpoint protected only by a static shared-secret header token rather than proper authentication
No structured logging/monitoring/alerting beyond default CloudWatch Lambda logs and ad hoc console.log

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Addresses a core, well-documented gap in disaster response: when infrastructure degrades after an earthquake, citizens and first responders lose the ability to report/see collapsed buildings, blocked roads, trapped people, and resource points (food/water/medicine/shelters). CrisisMap targets exactly that gap with an offline-first PWA that stores reports locally (IndexedDB) and syncs when connectivity returns, letting ordinary phones act as temporary data-collection nodes -- directly useful in the chaotic first hours of a Venezuelan earthquake when cell networks are spotty.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://erqk-crisis-map.netlify.app returns HTTP 200 and serves a real Angular 21 SPA (not a placeholder) bundling Leaflet + marker-clustering, a PWA manifest/service-worker, and proper meta description ('Collaborative disaster incident map. Report and verify incidents offline.'). A backend health endpoint (/api/health) also returns 200, indicating the AWS Lambda/API Gateway/DynamoDB backend is live, not just a static frontend. WebFetch's text-only renderer could not execute the client-side JS so it reported an empty page ('CrisisMap' title only) -- this is a tooling limitation, not evidence the app is broken, since curl confirms the full JS bundle, map libraries, and PWA assets are served correctly. No demo video exists to independently confirm the UI flow visually, so some uncertainty remains about final UX polish.

diffusion.recommended_angle: "CrisisMap: convierte cualquier teléfono en un nodo de reporte de emergencias, incluso sin internet" -- lead with the offline-first angle (works when networks go down, syncs automatically once they return) since that's the most differentiated, victim-relevant claim; pair with a screen recording since no video/screenshots exist yet.
gaps: No demo video to show the map/reporting UI in action
No screenshots captured/available for social assets
No visible branding/press kit beyond the app's own theme color and icon
No evidence yet of real users or real incident reports on the live instance (likely empty/demo data)
Zero GitHub stars/forks and single contributor -- no community traction to cite

evaluation.one_line_pitch: CrisisMap: la app PWA offline-first que convierte cada teléfono en un nodo de reporte ciudadano durante terremotos -- reporta, mapea y sincroniza incidentes aunque se caiga la conexión.

name: Personas Encontradas Bot
db_description:
## What did you build?

**Personas Encontradas VE** is a Telegram bot and public API that helps people search public reports of individuals found or located after the earthquakes in Venezuela.

In emergencies, information is often scattered across spreadsheets, websites, community projects, and social posts. This project brings those public records into one searchable place, while keeping each result linked to its original source for verification.

People can use the Telegram bot to search by name or Venezuelan ID number, browse recent records, check current sources, and submit found-person reports for review. Developers and organizations can also integrate through a stable public API.

## Who is it for?

This is for families looking for loved ones, community volunteers organizing emergency information, local initiatives publishing found-person data, and developers building humanitarian tools.

## How does it help?

- Makes scattered public information searchable from Telegram.
- Keeps records source-linked so people can verify them.
- Supports citizen and partner reports.
- Provides a public API for other tools and organizations.
- Handles sensitive data carefully by avoiding unnecessary exposure of names, document IDs, raw messages, tokens, or private payloads in logs and analytics.

## What should people try first?

Start with the Telegram bot:

https://t.me/encontrados_ve_bot

Try:

- `/ayuda` to see how it works.
- `/buscar Nombre Apellido` to search by name.
- `/buscar V12345678` to search by Venezuelan ID.
- `/lista` to browse records.
- `/fuentes` to see current data sources.

The project is open source here:

https://github.com/edwinvrgs/found-people-ve-bot

analysis.summary: A production-deployed Telegram bot and public REST API for searching people reported found/located after the June 2026 Venezuela earthquakes, built with Fastify, Prisma/Postgres, and Zod, featuring a genuinely well-architected layered codebase (config/http/services/repositories/telegram/ingestion), 71 passing tests, CI, timing-safe auth, rate limiting, and an active deduplication/merge-audit pipeline for ingested data.
analysis.project_type: Telegram bot + public REST API backend (Node.js/TypeScript) for a family-reunification/people-finder use case

database: Prisma ORM (Prisma Client + raw parameterized SQL for pg_trgm/unaccent search)

architecture.pattern: Layered service architecture: Fastify HTTP layer -> services -> repositories -> Prisma/raw SQL, with a separate Telegram bot module and an ingestion pipeline for scraping/parsing public sources
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Prisma Client for schema/migrations and simple queries, with parameterized Prisma.sql raw queries for Postgres-specific search (pg_trgm, unaccent, ILIKE) - a deliberate, documented hybrid approach rather than incidental raw SQL
architecture.api_design: REST-ish Fastify routes with Zod schema validation on all inputs, versioned public API (/api/v1/found-people), separate admin/ingest endpoints behind bearer auth, and a Telegram webhook endpoint behind a secret-token guard
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Good: config/env, http (app/routes/security/utils/schemas), services, repositories, telegram, ingestion, and analytics are all separated into distinct modules with clear responsibilities
architecture.modularity: High - repository layer isolates DB access, services layer encapsulates business logic (search criteria building, dedupe, metrics), ingestion pipeline is decoupled from the HTTP server and run as a standalone script
notable_patterns: Timing-safe bearer token comparison for admin/external API auth
Per-key in-memory sliding-window rate limiting with periodic sweep
Idempotency-key support on external report creation
Deduplication/merge-audit pipeline for ingested records with an audit trail table
Privacy-conscious analytics: hashed identifiers instead of raw PII, explicit policy against logging tokens/cedulas/notes
Self-authored architecture audit doc (docs/architecture/backend-audit.md) tracking technical debt and decisions over time

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, purposeful src/ layout: config, http, services, repositories, telegram, ingestion (with a dedupe subfolder), each test file colocated next to its implementation file
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive, domain-specific naming (found-people-service.ts, found-people-repository.ts, chat-registry, search-provider) with no generic placeholder names
code_organization.documentation_quality: Strong: detailed README with live bot/API links, API examples, CONTRIBUTING.md, SECURITY.md, GitHub issue templates, and a dated architecture-audit doc that honestly tracks resolved vs open risks
code_organization.reasoning: This is one of the better-organized hackathon repos: clear module boundaries, tests colocated with source, and unusually mature process docs (CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, architecture audit) for a project this size.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env.example lists all required secrets (INGEST_SECRET, EXTERNAL_API_SECRET, TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, DATABASE_URL); no secrets committed; env.ts centralizes access; SECURITY.md documents a disclosure process
production_readiness.security_notes: Admin and external-report endpoints require bearer tokens compared with timingSafeEqual; Telegram webhook validated via a secret header; all inputs validated with Zod; SQL uses Prisma tagged-template parameterization (no injection risk found); rate limiting on public, external, and admin endpoints; body size and request timeout limits set on Fastify instance
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Rate limiting and dedup/merge caching are in-process (Map-based), so this only scales safely as a single instance - acceptable for current Railway single-service deployment but would need a shared store (Redis) for horizontal scaling
production_readiness.reasoning: No Dockerfile in-repo, but the README documents a live production deployment on Railway with Prisma migrate-deploy as the schema source of truth; CI runs schema validation, typecheck, tests, build, and dependency audit on every PR, and all 71 tests plus typecheck pass locally.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, actively developed production system
maturity.reasoning: 52 commits, 71 passing unit/integration tests across 21 suites, GitHub Actions CI (schema validate, typecheck, smoke tests, build, npm audit), a live public bot (@encontrados_ve_bot) and public API deployed on Railway, plus process docs (CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, architecture audit) - this is far beyond scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed and operating in production against real post-earthquake data sources, with a working Telegram bot, versioned Postgres schema/migrations, dedupe/merge auditing for data quality, and a security/rate-limit posture appropriate for a public-facing service - this is the most production-ready and directly deployable project of the reviewed set.

key_features: Telegram bot search by name or Venezuelan cedula/ID with paginated results
Public read API (/api/v1/found-people) for third-party integrations with name/documentId/free-text search
Authenticated external-report submission endpoint with idempotency-key support
Admin ingest and delete endpoints protected by bearer auth
Automated ingestion pipeline scraping/consolidating multiple public found-person data sources with sanitization and extraction logic
Deduplication and merge-audit system that records before/after state when merging duplicate found-person records
Telegram chat registry for future broadcast messaging, with opt-out tracking
Privacy-preserving analytics via PostHog using hashed identifiers, plus structured Pino logging
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate to be a core, standalone production service for the hackathon's people-finder need; other projects (shelter-mapping, donations-aid, volunteer-coordination) could integrate with its public /api/v1/found-people API rather than duplicating found-person search/dedupe logic.
red_flags: In-memory rate limiting and dedupe caches will not scale beyond a single server instance without a shared store
No Dockerfile or explicit deploy config committed to the repo (relies on Railway's implicit Node build), so deployment reproducibility outside Railway is undocumented
Data quality depends entirely on third-party scraped sources (OCR data, community sites) with no independent verification beyond source-hash dedup

gaps: No demo video showing the bot in action
No screenshots of Telegram bot conversation/search results
No dedicated landing page or web demo (repo + Telegram link only)
Real usage/adoption numbers (how many families actually used it) not verifiable
diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the live, working Telegram bot (@encontrados_ve_bot) as a real tool families can use RIGHT NOW to search for loved ones found/located after the Venezuela earthquakes — 'search by name or cedula in seconds, from Telegram, no app install needed' — paired with a screen-recording GIF of a /buscar query to compensate for the missing demo video.

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No web demo URL provided (repo link only). The team's pitch references a live Telegram bot at https://t.me/encontrados_ve_bot with commands (/ayuda, /buscar, /lista, /fuentes), but per task instructions this counts as a repo/chat link rather than a verifiable public web demo, so status is set to no-public-demo. Could not independently interact with the bot in this environment.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Busca a tus seres queridos encontrados tras los terremotos en Venezuela directo desde Telegram — escribe /buscar y en segundos accedes a reportes verificados de personas localizadas.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake family reunification is a textbook critical humanitarian need: information about found/located persons is scattered across spreadsheets, community sites, and social posts, making it hard for families to find loved ones or verify reports. This project directly addresses that by consolidating public found-person data into a searchable Telegram bot (low-friction, no app install, works on basic phones/data connections common in crisis conditions) plus a public API so other humanitarian tools can reuse the data instead of duplicating scraping/dedupe work. It also thoughtfully handles sensitive PII (hashed analytics identifiers, no raw cedula/token logging) and keeps every record source-linked for verification, which matters enormously for trust in a domain prone to misinformation.

name: Generador de fichas de busquedas para redes sociales
db_description:
# Generador de Fichas SOS - Sismo 2026 🚨

Una aplicación web de código abierto diseñada para la **rápida generación de fichas de búsqueda de personas desaparecidas** en situaciones de crisis y desastres naturales, enfocada en zonas con conectividad extremadamente limitada.

## 📌 Utilidad del Proyecto

Durante emergencias como el Sismo de 2026, la difusión de información veraz, estructurada y legible en redes sociales puede salvar vidas. Esta herramienta permite a cualquier persona generar rápidamente una ficha con foto y detalles clave (ubicación, señas particulares, contacto) en un formato cuadrado (1:1) optimizado para plataformas como WhatsApp, Instagram, X (Twitter) y Facebook.

### Principios de *Crisis UX* (Diseño para Emergencias):
- **Cero distracciones:** Interfaz directa y sin elementos innecesarios.
- **Alto contraste visual:** Uso estratégico de colores de alerta (rojo brillante) y tipografías grandes para información crítica (como el número de contacto).
- **Formatos estandarizados:** Oculta dinámicamente los campos vacíos para evitar generar "ruido visual" en la ficha generada.

---

## ⚡ Rendimiento en Redes de Baja Velocidad (3G / EDGE)

El mayor desafío en una zona de desastre es la caída de las telecomunicaciones. Esta aplicación ha sido desarrollada pensando estrictamente en **redes 35kbps o Edge**. 

Para lograr esto, utilizamos **[Astro](https://astro.build/)** y aplicamos las siguientes optimizaciones drásticas:

1. **Generación de Sitios Estáticos (SSG):** Todo el HTML y CSS se pre-renderiza en la compilación. El servidor no hace procesamiento en tiempo real, simplemente entrega los archivos planos al instante.
2. **Cero Javascript de Frameworks:** Por defecto, Astro envía `0kb` de Javascript del framework al cliente. La interactividad de esta aplicación se maneja con minúsculas "Islas de Astro" utilizando Vanilla Javascript (JS puro).
3. **Procesamiento de Imágenes Offline:** A diferencia de otras plataformas, **el usuario NUNCA sube la foto a un servidor**. La imagen seleccionada se procesa, recorta y dibuja localmente usando la API de `Canvas` del propio teléfono inteligente. Esto elimina el cuello de botella más grande en redes lentas: la subida de archivos pesados.
4. **Cero dependencias externas:** No cargamos tipografías desde Google Fonts ni librerías de terceros (se utilizan las `system-ui` de cada dispositivo móvil).

---

## 🛠️ Cómo Funciona

1. **Llena los datos:** El usuario ingresa la información de la persona (nombre, foto, edad, última ubicación y señas).
2. **Previsualización en Vivo:** Un motor Javascript dibuja la ficha en tiempo real sobre una etiqueta `<canvas>`.
3. **Descarga y Difunde:** Con un solo clic, se exporta una imagen `.png` de alta calidad (1080x1080) lista para compartir, junto a un texto pre-formateado que el usuario puede copiar a su portapapeles.
4. **Privacidad total:** Ningún dato, nombre o fotografía sale del navegador del usuario. Todo es temporal y se procesa localmente.

---

## 🔒 Privacidad y Manejo de Datos

Para cuidar tu seguridad y la de tus seres queridos, **no almacenamos, ni recopilamos, ni enviamos absolutamente nada de la información introducida en esta herramienta a nuestros servidores ni a ninguna base de datos externa.** Todo el procesamiento (incluyendo el manejo de fotos y la generación final de la ficha) se ejecuta 100% de manera local en el navegador o dispositivo del usuario. Al cerrar la aplicación, los datos no persisten en nuestros sistemas, garantizando privacidad total.

---

## 🚀 Guía para Desarrolladores (Haz un Fork)

¡Toda ayuda es bienvenida! Si deseas mejorar esta herramienta, traducirla, o adaptarla para otras emergencias, el proyecto es completamente tuyo.

### Requisitos previos
- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en/) (Versión 18 o superior)
- NPM o PNPM

### Instalación local

1. **Haz un Fork** de este repositorio y clónalo en tu computadora:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/TU-USUARIO/venezuela.git
   ```

2. **Instala las dependencias:**
   ```bash
   cd venezuela
   npm install
   ```

3. **Inicia el servidor de desarrollo:**
   ```bash
   npm run dev
   ```
   *La aplicación estará disponible en `http://localhost:4321`.*

### Estructura de Archivos Principal

- `src/pages/index.astro`: Contiene toda la interfaz principal, el formulario, y la lógica nativa en Javascript que dibuja sobre el `<canvas>`.
- `src/layouts/Layout.astro`: Define la estructura base HTML, los meta tags y colores generales.
- `src/styles/global.css`: Configuraciones globales de TailwindCSS.

### Comandos Útiles

| Comando | Acción |
| :--- | :--- |
| `npm run dev` | Inicia el servidor local en modo desarrollo. |
| `npm run build` | Compila la aplicación para producción en la carpeta `/dist/`. |
| `npm run preview` | Permite previsualizar localmente la versión compilada de producción. |

## 🤝 Contribuciones

Siéntete libre de abrir *Issues* si encuentras un bug o tienes sugerencias, o manda un *Pull Request* directamente. Asegúrate de probar que la generación de imágenes sigue funcionando y mantén el principio de "0 kilobytes innecesarios".

analysis.summary: A single-page, entirely client-side Astro app that lets a user fill out a missing-person form and generates a shareable 1080x1080 PNG 'flyer' via HTML Canvas, plus a copy-to-clipboard WhatsApp-style text message. There is no backend, no database, no server logic, and no data sharing between users — it is a local flyer-image generator, not a search/matching/coordination platform.
analysis.project_type: Client-side static web tool (single-page Astro app) for generating missing-person search flyers as downloadable images

architecture.pattern: Static site generation (SSG) with a single page containing an inline vanilla-JS 'island' script; no client-server split
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database of any kind. Photo and form data are stored only in the browser's localStorage for session persistence; nothing is transmitted anywhere.
architecture.api_design: None — no API routes, no server endpoints, no fetch calls to any backend
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Weak — all form markup, canvas rendering engine (~500 lines), progress logic, localStorage persistence, and DOM re-parenting logic for responsive modal behavior live inline in one ~1060-line src/pages/index.astro file
architecture.modularity: None — single monolithic file; Welcome.astro/Layout.astro are largely unused/boilerplate remnants of the Astro starter
notable_patterns: Canvas-based image generation entirely in-browser
Client-side image compression/resizing before rendering to stay lightweight for 2G/EDGE networks
localStorage-based session persistence (no backend persistence)
Responsive DOM re-parenting of a preview card between desktop sidebar, mobile inline flow, and mobile modal

code_organization.directory_structure: Default unmodified Astro starter layout (src/pages, src/layouts, src/components, src/assets, src/styles) with only index.astro meaningfully edited; Welcome.astro and background.svg/astro.svg assets are leftover starter boilerplate never referenced by the real page
code_organization.naming_quality: Reasonable Spanish-language HTML ids/names within the single file, but no componentization at all
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is thorough and well-written (privacy claims, performance rationale, dev setup) but overstates the project as an 'application' when it is really one static HTML form with inline JS
code_organization.reasoning: Everything of substance is crammed into one 1060-line file with no code splitting, no reusable components, and the rest of the repo is unedited scaffolding.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: N/A — no secrets, no server, no API keys used or needed
production_readiness.security_notes: No XSS sanitization beyond default DOM text APIs (uses .textContent mostly, low risk); localStorage.setItem for photo has only a try/catch for quota errors, no validation of file input beyond accept="image/*"; because everything is client-only there is no server attack surface, but also no way to actually connect a missing person report to responders — data literally never leaves the device unless the user manually shares the generated image, which limits real-world usefulness for actual search/rescue coordination.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Trivially scalable as a static site (can be hosted on any CDN/static host with zero backend cost), but scalability is moot since there is no shared data store — two users filling the form never see each other's data, so this cannot function as a people-finder/reunification registry, only as a personal flyer-maker.
production_readiness.reasoning: No deploy config (no Dockerfile, no CI, no hosting config found), no tests, no error boundaries beyond a single try/catch, and the core value proposition (connecting families to a shared missing-persons list) is entirely absent — it only produces a local image a user must manually distribute via WhatsApp/social media.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Mostly boilerplate (default Astro starter scaffold) with one genuinely hand-written feature file (index.astro) containing real, non-trivial canvas-rendering and form logic
maturity.reasoning: Single commit ('Initial commit'), one contributor, no tests, no CI, no lockfile changes beyond starter deps (astro, tailwind) — this is a hackathon prototype built in one sitting, not an iterated/maintained project.

viability.reasoning: The canvas-flyer generator itself works as a well-crafted, offline-friendly UX for low-bandwidth Venezuela, but as a disaster-response tool it lacks the one thing that matters most — a shared, searchable database connecting reports so responders/family members can find matches; without a backend it's a nice personal tool, not a coordination system, capping its real-world impact for reunification efforts.

key_features: Multi-section missing-person intake form (identity, last known location in Venezuela with all 24 states, medical alerts, reporter contact info)
In-browser photo upload with client-side resize/compression to JPEG (max 800px, quality 0.75) — photo never leaves the device
Live Canvas-rendered 1080x1080 flyer preview with dynamic hide-if-empty field logic
One-click PNG download of the generated flyer and one-click copy of a pre-formatted WhatsApp-style broadcast message
LocalStorage persistence of form/photo for session continuity, responsive mobile modal preview UX
Explicit zero-data-collection privacy design optimized for 2G/EDGE/3G connectivity
analysis.merge_potential: Could be merged as a lightweight, no-backend 'flyer generator' UI module feeding into a larger people-finder/reunification platform that has a real shared database and search — this repo alone would need a backend/API bolted on to become a genuine coordination tool rather than a personal image-export utility.
red_flags: No backend/database at all despite the domain being 'missing persons search' — data is never shared between users, so it cannot actually help match/find people beyond manual social-media sharing
Single commit, single contributor, no tests, no CI/CD, no deployment configuration found in repo
Repo still contains unmodified Astro starter boilerplate (Welcome.astro, starter assets) indicating minimal cleanup/iteration
All 'privacy-first' claims are true but also mean the tool provides no aggregation, deduplication, or verification of reports — duplicate/fraudulent flyers are entirely possible with no safeguards

diffusion.recommended_angle: Herramienta offline-first para crear y compartir fichas de búsqueda de personas desaparecidas por WhatsApp/redes sociales en zonas con conectividad 2G/3G tras el sismo, sin subir fotos ni datos a ningún servidor.
gaps: No demo video
No screenshots asset captured/published
No evidence of real users/adoption
No shared database — cannot actually match or connect missing-person reports between users, limiting real coordination impact

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL loads successfully and is a real, functional multi-section form (identity, last known location across Venezuela's 24 states, medical alerts, reporter contact) with live canvas preview, PNG download, and copy-to-clipboard WhatsApp text. Progress indicator and validation work. No errors observed.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Genera en segundos una ficha de búsqueda lista para compartir por WhatsApp/redes, sin subir fotos ni datos a internet — funciona incluso en 2G tras el sismo.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Sharing clear, standardized missing-person flyers on social media genuinely helps during disaster response, and the tool's privacy-first, zero-upload, low-bandwidth (2G/EDGE) design is well-suited to Venezuela's post-earthquake connectivity conditions. However, it is purely a client-side flyer/image generator with no backend, database, or matching system — two users filling out the form never see each other's data, so it cannot function as an actual reunification/coordination registry, only as a personal tool to manually export and distribute an image. It solves the 'create a shareable flyer quickly' sub-problem well but not the core 'help match/find people' problem.

name: GuacaMalla - Mesh sync entre dispositivos
db_description:
GuacaMalla es una red de comunicación de emergencia descentralizada y Offline-First, diseñada para situaciones de catástrofe (terremotos, cortes masivos, colapso de infraestructura) donde internet y las torres celulares dejan de funcionar.

## ¿Cuál es la meta?

Nuestra meta es salvar vidas asegurando que ninguna persona herida o aislada se quede incomunicada en momentos críticos.

## ¿Cómo funciona?

La app convierte teléfonos Android en balizas de radio independientes que emiten frames de 119 bytes firmados con Ed25519 vía Bluetooth (BLE 5). Los mensajes SOS y coordenadas GPS saltan de dispositivo en dispositivo formando una malla descentralizada sin necesidad de servidores ni internet. Cuando un teléfono alcanza una zona con señal actúa como "mula de datos" y sube los frames acumulados al backend opcional, que los re-verifica criptográficamente antes de persistirlos.

El enlace es intencionalmente abierto — un SOS es público, cualquiera en alcance debe recibirlo y retransmitirlo. La confianza vive en el payload: cada frame va firmado con Ed25519 y cualquier manipulación rompe la firma, de modo que el siguiente salto lo descarta silenciosamente.

**El backend es opcional.** La malla funciona 100% offline y sin infraestructura.

## Equipo

- Rodrigo Rivas — Product + UI/UX + Frontend · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rodrigorivasco)
- Victor Maldonado — Fullstack + Mobile Native · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/vmlcode/)
- David Gonzalez — Backend + Mobile Native + QA · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/david-gonzalez-dev2012/)
- Jaime Stalislav — Backend + Mobile Native + QA
- Carlos Ramirez — PM + Plataforma · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/carlosjramirez/)
- Cristopher Avila — Frontend · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/cristopher-adrián-ávila-lópez-937994402)

## Arquitectura

| Plano | Radio | Payload | Estándar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control / SOS | BLE GAP Broadcaster/Observer | 22 B payload + 32 B pubkey + 64 B firma | BLE 5 Extended Advertising |
| Datos (ligero) | Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) | ≤ 255 B | NAN Service Discovery |
| Datos (pesado) | Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) | > 255 B | NAN Data Path (NDP), efímero |

El frame de 22 bytes incluye lat/lon (int32 E7), timestamp Unix y tipo de mensaje. La autenticidad se verifica con Ed25519 en cada salto — el nodo receptor nunca confía en el remitente.

analysis.summary: GuacaMalla Net is an offline-first, decentralized emergency mesh communication system for disaster scenarios (earthquakes, infrastructure collapse) in Venezuela. Android phones broadcast Ed25519-signed 22-byte SOS/location frames over BLE 5 and Wi-Fi Aware (NAN), forming a device-to-device mesh that works with zero internet/cellular infrastructure; when a phone (acting as a 'data mule') regains connectivity it optionally uploads accumulated frames to a Bun/Fastify backend that re-verifies signatures and persists to Supabase, with a quorum-based witness 'resolve' flow to confirm rescues.
analysis.project_type: Cross-platform system: native Android app (Kotlin/Jetpack Compose) + optional TypeScript/Bun backend API + shared crypto/protocol package

database: Supabase (Postgres) with RLS enabled, service-role key bypass
in-memory store fallback for local dev
infra_deploy: GitHub Actions (Android debug APK build + Firebase App Distribution)

architecture.pattern: Modular monolith backend (Fastify route modules per domain: channels, locations, resolve, dashboard) with a repository layer abstracting Supabase vs in-memory store; Android app split into mesh/BLE/Wi-Fi Aware transport layers, crypto, ingest (data-mule upload), and UI
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw Supabase JS client (supabase-js) with hand-written upsert/select queries per repo module (locationsRepo, channelsRepo, resolvesRepo); dual-mode with in-memory fallback when SUPABASE_URL/service key unset
architecture.api_design: REST (Fastify) + WebSocket for live subscriptions; endpoints for channel records (official alerts), location ingest/query, resolve (quorum witness) flow, and a dashboard
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Good — routes/, store.ts (in-memory), db/*Repo.ts (persistence abstraction), security/ (auth, config, rate limiting, validation), crypto/ (signing) are cleanly separated modules
architecture.modularity: High — monorepo with packages/shared (crypto, types, mesh constants, resolve logic) consumed by both backend and Android via workspace protocol; Android has distinct mesh/ble/aware/crypto/ingest/backend packages
notable_patterns: Zero-trust mesh: every hop cryptographically verifies Ed25519 signatures before relaying/accepting frames
Dual-mode data layer with graceful in-memory fallback when DB is unconfigured
Timing-safe API key comparison (timingSafeEqual) with dev ephemeral key generation and production-required key enforcement
M-of-N quorum witness 'resolve' flow with cooldown sweeper and geo-radius/age checks to confirm SOS resolution without needing victim confirmation
Coordinate coarsening/sanitization for public community channels vs full-precision official channels
Docker build documented with an explicit rationale for repo-root build context (workspace resolution)

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear monorepo layout (android/, backend/, packages/shared/, docs/) with consistent per-domain subfolders and extensive docs/ (protocol flows, byte layouts, crypto specs, demo runbooks)
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming across Kotlin and TS (e.g., FloodRouter, DedupeCache, AgePolicy, resolvesRepo)
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually thorough for a hackathon project — dedicated docs/ with protocol spec, payload binary layout, crypto docs, demo runbook, and CLAUDE.md dev-agent instructions
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized monorepo with genuine module boundaries, extensive protocol/architecture documentation beyond typical README boilerplate, and consistent naming conventions across two languages.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env.example with clear placeholders, no secrets committed; production boot fails fast if GUACAMAYA_ADMIN_KEY is missing when NODE_ENV=production; timing-safe API key comparisons; Supabase service-role key never shipped to client, RLS enabled on tables
production_readiness.security_notes: Ed25519 signature verification on every mesh frame (zero-trust), rate limiting (global + per-route), helmet, CORS allowlist, coordinate coarsening for public channels to protect victim privacy, tamper test script included; API key auth is a single shared secret rather than per-user auth, which is a reasonable tradeoff for this use case but limits multi-tenant safety
production_readiness.scalability_notes: In-memory fallback store is not viable for multi-instance/production scale, but Supabase-backed path with indices and a cooldown sweeper handles the expected use case; backend is intentionally optional/stateless-ish and the core system (BLE mesh) works with zero backend at all, which is a strong resilience property for post-disaster infra collapse
production_readiness.reasoning: Backend is actually deployed on Railway with health checks, restart policy, graceful shutdown, and fail-fast production config checks; genuine automated tests pass (31 backend/shared tests); Android CI builds and distributes debug APKs via Firebase; this goes well beyond typical hackathon scaffolding.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 119 commits from 5+ distinct contributors over several days, 31 passing backend/shared unit tests plus extensive Android Kotlin unit tests (11 test files, ~1168 LOC), working CI pipeline that builds and distributes real APKs, and a deployed Railway backend — this is a substantive, actively developed system, not AI-generated scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: Directly addresses the stated hackathon goal (earthquake victims in Venezuela) by solving the hardest real problem — communication when cell towers and internet are down — via a genuinely offline BLE/Wi-Fi Aware mesh with cryptographic integrity; main viability risks are hardware/OS fragmentation (BLE 5 Extended Advertising and Wi-Fi Aware require specific Android 8+ chipsets), battery drain from continuous radio broadcast, and the need for organic adoption density for mesh relay to be effective, but the architecture and threat model (zero-trust signing, tamper rejection) are unusually rigorous for a hackathon entry.

key_features: Offline BLE 5 mesh SOS broadcasting with Ed25519-signed 22-byte frames
Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) data plane for larger payloads when BLE range/bandwidth insufficient
Data-mule pattern: any phone that regains connectivity uploads accumulated mesh frames to backend
Backend re-verification of signatures before persistence (zero-trust boundary)
M-of-N quorum witness 'resolve' flow to confirm rescue without requiring victim self-confirmation
Public/official channel separation with coordinate coarsening for privacy on community SOS broadcasts
Live WebSocket dashboard/subscription for monitoring alerts and trajectories
Tamper-test tooling and demo runbook for live demonstration of signature rejection
analysis.merge_potential: Could integrate with a shelter-mapping or people-finder project by feeding verified SOS/location data into a broader victim-tracking dashboard; the resolve/quorum witness pattern and signed-frame protocol could also generalize to other offline-first aid-logistics or volunteer-coordination tools needing tamper-resistant peer relay.
red_flags: Single shared API key model (admin/read/ws keys) rather than per-device or per-user auth — acceptable for MVP but not multi-tenant safe
In-memory store fallback means data loss on restart if Supabase is not configured, which operators must remember to configure for real deployments
Wi-Fi Aware and BLE 5 Extended Advertising require relatively modern Android hardware/OS support, which may exclude older devices common in affected populations
CI only builds/distributes a debug APK (no release/signed build pipeline), and only one workflow exists (no backend CI/test-on-PR gate visible)

gaps: No embedded interactive demo of the actual mesh app on the marketing site — a visitor cannot try the BLE relay or see it work without installing the debug APK or watching the video
No public screenshots of the Android app UI or live dashboard included alongside the landing page
Only a debug (unsigned) APK is distributed via Firebase App Distribution, not a public Play Store / signed release, limiting how easily press or donors can install and try it themselves
diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the offline-mesh survival hook: 'When cell towers fall, your phone becomes the rescue signal.' Show the BLE SOS relay concept, the Ed25519 signed-frame trust model, and the deployed Railway backend + Android APK CI pipeline as proof this isn't vaporware. Pair with the demo video showing the tamper-rejection test and multi-hop relay in action, and the polished bilingual landing page for the waitlist CTA.

evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://guacamalla.org redirects (308) to https://www.guacamalla.org/, a polished, bilingual (ES/EN) single-page marketing/landing site (Vercel/Cloudflare hosted) with hero copy ('Sin señal. Tu señal salta de mano en mano hasta encontrar ayuda.'), a 'how it works' explainer, a technical/trust section on Ed25519 signed frames, and a working waitlist signup form (posts to Formspree). It is NOT an interactive demo of the mesh app itself — the actual product (Android app + Bun/Fastify backend on Railway) lives in the GitHub repo, is CI-built (debug APK via GitHub Actions + Firebase App Distribution), and is not embedded/playable on the marketing site. A raw HTTP fetch initially showed a generic 'Bundled Page / Unpacking...' loader (client-rendered bundler output) before the real landing content resolves via JS.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: GuacaMalla convierte cualquier Android en una baliza SOS: cuando caen las torres y el internet, tu señal salta de teléfono en teléfono, firmada y a prueba de manipulación, hasta encontrar ayuda.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After a major earthquake, cell towers and internet infrastructure are among the first things to fail, cutting off trapped or isolated victims from rescuers exactly when speed matters most. GuacaMalla directly targets this failure mode with a genuinely offline (no internet, no cell towers, no central server required) BLE/Wi-Fi-Aware mesh that relays cryptographically signed SOS+GPS frames phone-to-phone until one reaches connectivity ('data mule' pattern) or another rescuer. This is one of the highest-leverage, most technically difficult problems a hackathon team tackled in this batch, and the team clearly understood the threat model (zero-trust signature verification per hop, tamper rejection, coordinate coarsening for victim privacy).

name: Puente Venezuela
db_description:
Puente Venezuela
A web-based humanitarian coordination platform built for the Build4Venezuela hackathon, following the June 24, 2026, earthquakes in Venezuela.

What it does
Connects those in need of help with those who can offer help in real time:

Interactive map with pins showing needs and offers of help
Quick reports — anyone can report a need or offer help without having to create an account
Filters by category — water, food, shelter, medicine, rescue, volunteers, transportation
Real-time status—pending, in progress, or resolved
Responsive design—works on mobile and desktop.

architecture.pattern: Full-stack React (TanStack Start SSR) app using Supabase as BaaS backend, with TanStack server functions as a thin API layer over the Supabase JS client
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (PostgREST) called both from server functions (src/lib/reports.functions.ts, using service-ish anon key from env) and directly from client (src/lib/upload.ts, src/integrations/supabase/client.ts); no ORM, raw Supabase table queries with Zod validation on server fn inputs
architecture.api_design: TanStack Start createServerFn RPC-style handlers (listReports, createReport, updateStatus, assignReport) validated with Zod schemas; no REST/GraphQL layer beyond that
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable for the app size: components/, lib/ (business logic, i18n, upload), integrations/supabase/ (client, auth middleware, generated types), routes/ (TanStack Router pages), supabase/migrations for schema
architecture.modularity: Small, single-purpose modules (ReportForm, ReportCard, ReportDetail, MapView, AttachmentsGallery); shadcn/ui component library included wholesale under src/components/ui
notable_patterns: Server functions re-instantiate a Supabase client per call instead of a shared singleton

Row Level Security policies deliberately allow anonymous (unauthenticated) INSERT and UPDATE on reports for a no-login emergency-reporting UX, mitigated by a Postgres trigger (protect_report_fields) restricting which columns anon can mutate
Client-side image compression before upload to Supabase Storage
PWA support via vite-plugin-pwa and an OfflineIndicator component
Realtime enabled via supabase_realtime publication on reports and report_attachments tables

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, conventional TanStack Start layout (src/routes, src/components, src/lib, src/integrations, supabase/migrations) that clearly separates concerns for a project of this size.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming (reports.functions.ts, attachments.functions.ts, ReportForm.tsx, auth-middleware.ts).
code_organization.documentation_quality: README (in Portuguese) clearly explains purpose, schema, and how to run locally; AGENTS.md is just Lovable boilerplate; no inline code comments beyond a few clarifying ones in migrations/upload.ts.
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for its scope with sensible file boundaries, though it's a small single-table app so the bar for 'good organization' is lower; docs are decent but not exhaustive.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: The .env file (containing Supabase project URL and anon/publishable key) is committed directly to the git repository rather than gitignored; while these are Supabase's publicly-scoped anon keys (safe by Supabase design given RLS), committing .env sets a bad precedent and no secret rotation or server-only secret is separated out.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS policies intentionally grant anonymous users INSERT and UPDATE on the reports table (justified by a no-login emergency UX) and only partially lock down which columns anon can change via a Postgres trigger; there is no rate limiting, CAPTCHA, or abuse protection, so the public endpoints are trivially spammable/floodable by anyone with the anon key (which is committed in the repo). Auth middleware (auth-middleware.ts) exists for authenticated flows but most core write paths (createReport, updateStatus) run unauthenticated using the anon key.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Backed by Supabase Postgres with realtime; fine for hackathon/MVP scale but no caching, pagination, or query limits on listReports (selects all rows), and no background job/queue infra for things like notification fanout.
production_readiness.reasoning: Functional MVP with real DB-backed CRUD, RLS, and a working UI, but lacks CI, tests, structured logging, deployment config (Dockerfile/CI pipeline), and has meaningful security gaps (open anonymous writes, committed env file) that would need hardening before real-world humanitarian use with sensitive contact data.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, functioning application logic — not empty scaffolding. Built with Lovable (AI website builder) evidenced by .lovable/ directory and AGENTS.md boilerplate, but the reports/map/attachments/realtime features are genuinely implemented with working Supabase queries, RLS policies, and UI, not just placeholder screens.
maturity.reasoning: 62 commits, 8 migrations evolving the schema (attachments, realtime, field-protection trigger), and working end-to-end feature (create/view/filter/assign/resolve reports on a map) indicate active real development, but zero automated tests and no CI pipeline hold back the maturity score.

viability.reasoning: Addresses a clear, narrow need (crowdsourced need/offer reporting mapped geographically) with a working demo (seeded with realistic Venezuelan disaster data) and a genuinely usable no-login flow, which suits real-world urgency; however it is a single-table CRUD app with no anti-spam/anti-abuse controls, no admin moderation, and a committed anon key with wide-open anonymous writes, which is risky if actually deployed to help real earthquake victims without further hardening.

key_features: Interactive Leaflet/OpenStreetMap map showing need (red) vs offer (green) pins colored/sized by urgency and status
No-login quick report form for needs/offers with category, location, contact, and urgency
Category and status filtering (water, food, shelter, medicine, rescue, volunteer, transport)
Report assignment workflow (assign a volunteer, moves report to in_progress) and status updates (pending/in_progress/resolved)
Photo attachments with client-side image compression uploaded to Supabase Storage, shown via signed/public URLs
Realtime updates via Supabase Realtime publication on reports and attachments tables
PWA/offline indicator support and i18n (Portuguese/Spanish toggle) for accessibility
analysis.merge_potential: Could merge well with a shelter-mapping or aid-logistics project that lacks a real-time crowdsourced needs/offers map — this repo's Supabase schema (reports + attachments) and Leaflet map component are a solid, reusable piece to combine with a more robust auth/moderation layer from another project.
red_flags: .env with real Supabase project URL and anon key committed to git history instead of gitignored
RLS policies allow fully anonymous INSERT and UPDATE on the reports table with only a partial column-level trigger guard, enabling spam/abuse of a system meant for real emergency data
No automated tests or CI pipeline of any kind
No rate limiting or abuse protection on public write endpoints that store real contact info (phone/email) of vulnerable people
listReports fetches all rows with no pagination or limit, a scalability concern if adopted widely
analysis.summary: Puente Venezuela is a Lovable-generated but functionally real TanStack Start + Supabase web app that lets anyone report earthquake-related needs or offers of help without logging in, displays them as color-coded pins on a Leaflet map, and supports status updates, volunteer assignment, and photo attachments with realtime sync. It is a working MVP with a genuine (if minimal) Postgres schema, RLS policies, and UI, but has no tests/CI, a committed .env file, and wide-open anonymous write access that would need security hardening before trusted deployment for real disaster response.
analysis.project_type: web app (humanitarian crisis-reporting/coordination platform)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as 'a working coordination map prototype for earthquake needs/offers' rather than a live operational system — emphasize the concept and UI polish, not real-time impact numbers (which currently show 0 activity).
gaps: No demo video
No screenshots captured of the actual map/report flow in action
No seeded/real usage data visible on the live site (all stats show 0)
Security red flags (committed .env with anon key, wide-open anonymous writes) should be fixed before spotlighting to avoid inviting abuse
No proof of real users or NGO/volunteer adoption

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL loads and presents a polished landing page in Spanish ('Cada minuto cuenta. Cada ayuda llega') describing the needs/offers matching concept, target audiences (victims, volunteers, NGOs), and 8 support categories. Dashboard stats (active reports, resolved cases, active volunteers) all show 0, indicating no real usage/seed data is live. Could not verify the interactive map or report-submission form via WebFetch (client-side routed SPA paths like /report and /mapa returned 404 to the fetch tool, likely because WebFetch doesn't execute client-side JS routing rather than the app being broken — the underlying code confirms a real Leaflet map, report form, and Supabase-backed CRUD exist). Overall: real product exists per code, but the publicly visible live experience currently reads as an empty/unproven landing page.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Puente Venezuela: a real-time map that connects earthquake victims' urgent needs (water, food, shelter, medicine, rescue) with nearby volunteers and NGOs — no login required.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake coordination between people needing water/food/shelter/medicine/rescue and volunteers/NGOs able to help is a genuine, high-urgency logistics gap in disaster response. A no-login, real-time, map-based needs/offers board directly targets this coordination failure, which is a well-documented pain point in humanitarian crises (information silos, no shared situational picture). The project's own pitch and the implemented schema (categories, urgency, status workflow, assignment) map closely to real disaster-response workflows, not a generic feature list.

name: Ruview
db_description:
Materiales: ESP32 
π RuView is a WiFi sensing platform that turns radio signals into spatial intelligence.
Every WiFi router already fills your space with radio waves. When people move, breathe, or even sit still, they disturb those waves in measurable ways. RuView captures these disturbances using Channel State Information (CSI) from low-cost ESP32 sensors and turns them into actionable data.

analysis.summary: RuView (ruvnet/ruview) is a large, mature, multi-language open-source WiFi CSI sensing platform that turns commodity WiFi signals into presence/vitals/pose detection, including a dedicated 'Mass Casualty Assessment Tool' (MAT) Rust crate explicitly designed for detecting survivors under rubble after earthquakes/collapses. It is a genuinely production-grade codebase (Rust workspace with DDD crates, Python/FastAPI backend, React/RN mobile app, extensive CI, Docker, monitoring) but it is an existing flagship open-source project by ruvnet (76k+ stars, 1000+ commits since June 2025), not a purpose-built Venezuela hackathon submission — there is no Venezuela-specific content anywhere in the repo.
analysis.project_type: WiFi-CSI based spatial sensing / vital-signs / disaster survivor-detection platform (hardware+firmware+backend+mobile+dashboard)

database: SQLite (.claude/memory.db, .swarm/state.json — local tooling state, not app data)
infra_deploy: GitHub Actions (20+ workflow files: ci, cd, firmware, security-scan, dashboard-pages, docker builds)
ai_ml: custom contrastive CSI encoder + pose estimation models (Hugging Face hosted)
spiking neural networks for edge adaptation

architecture.pattern: Domain-driven design, event-driven microservice-ish workspace (Rust crates) + separate Python FastAPI service + edge firmware; anti-corruption-layer adapters between subsystems
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: SQLAlchemy (async, Python) + Alembic migrations for v1 API; Rust side is largely in-memory/event-sourced with no traditional DB, using Redis and local state files
architecture.api_design: REST (FastAPI/Axum) + WebSocket streaming + UDP ingestion from ESP32 sensors; bearer_auth.rs module present in Rust sensing-server
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Strong — explicit DDD bounded contexts (domain/detection/localization/alerting/integration) inside the disaster-focused wifi-densepose-mat crate (~20.8k LOC), clear crate boundaries in the Rust workspace
architecture.modularity: Very high modularity (15+ independently versioned/published Rust crates, vendored submodules rvcsi/rufield/ruvector, separate mobile app, separate dashboard)
notable_patterns: Domain-Driven Design with bounded contexts
Event sourcing for survivor detection audit trail
Anti-corruption layer adapters between crates
Cryptographic (Ed25519) witness/attestation chain for measurement integrity
START-protocol-compatible triage classification (Immediate/Delayed/Minor/Deceased)

code_organization.directory_structure: Extremely well-organized for its scale: clear top-level separation of firmware/, python/, v2/ (Rust), ui/ (web+mobile+dashboard), docs/adr (150+ ADRs), scripts/, tests/, docker/, monitoring/
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming across crates and modules (e.g., wifi-densepose-mat/src/domain/survivor.rs, detection/breathing.rs)
code_organization.documentation_quality: Exceptional — extensive ADRs, user guides, troubleshooting docs, witness logs, README with benchmark tables and citations
code_organization.reasoning: This is one of the most thoroughly documented and structured repos of its kind, reflecting long-term engineering investment rather than hackathon-speed scaffolding.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: example.env template, explicit rules against committing secrets, scripts/redact-secrets.py and scripts/rotate-npm-token.sh present
production_readiness.security_notes: bearer_auth.rs + host_validation.rs in the Rust sensing server, dedicated security-scan.yml CI workflow, docs/security-audit-wasm-edge-vendor.md; however no Venezuela-specific deployment hardening, and the platform requires physical ESP32 hardware + calibration per site which is a major real-world deployment barrier for rapid disaster response
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Designed for edge/offline operation (no cloud dependency), multi-node mesh scanning, but the MAT disaster-detection crate is explicitly a P1/roadmap integration ('not wired into the live server' per some sub-modules) rather than a battle-tested field tool
production_readiness.reasoning: Engineering maturity (CI, Docker, monitoring, auth, tests) is genuinely production-grade for a hardware sensing platform, but the earthquake/disaster-specific MAT capability is architecturally designed yet not proven as an integrated, field-deployed product — it's a specialized crate within a much broader general-purpose WiFi sensing project.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, substantial, actively developed software (421k LOC, 1054 commits, 25 contributors, commits as recent as 2026-06-27) — not boilerplate
maturity.reasoning: 1463+ claimed tests, 20+ CI workflows, Rust workspace with 1000+ passing tests, Python test suite, mobile Jest+Maestro e2e tests, deterministic cryptographic proof verification pipeline — this is a mature, long-running open-source project.

viability.reasoning: As a piece of engineering it is outstanding, but as a Build4Venezuela hackathon submission it is highly questionable: it is a pre-existing, massive, general-purpose commercial-grade WiFi sensing platform by a well-known open-source author (ruvnet) with zero Venezuela-specific content, requires custom ESP32 hardware ($9-15/node) plus per-room calibration, and its disaster-survivor detection (MAT) feature is explicitly flagged in its own docs as unvalidated/roadmap ('not wired into the live server', 'no accuracy claim', synthetic benchmarks). It is not something realistically deployable by hackathon organizers to help earthquake victims in the immediate term.

key_features: WiFi CSI-based through-wall presence, breathing rate, and heart-rate detection using $9 ESP32 sensors
Mass Casualty Assessment Tool (MAT) crate implementing START-protocol triage classification for disaster survivors
17-keypoint pose estimation and multi-person counting from RF signals alone (no cameras)
Home Assistant / Apple HomeKit / Google Home / Alexa smart-home integrations
Mobile app (React Native/Expo) with live vitals, zones, and alert screens plus e2e tests
Cryptographically attested (Ed25519) witness chain and deterministic proof-verification pipeline for measurement integrity
analysis.merge_potential: Low practical merge potential for a Venezuela hackathon initiative as-is: it is a foreign, enormous, hardware-dependent codebase (Rust/Python/firmware) that would require significant integration effort, physical ESP32 procurement/deployment, and validation of its explicitly-unproven disaster-detection accuracy before any real earthquake-response use; better suited as a long-term research reference than a near-term deployable aid tool.
red_flags: No Venezuela-specific content anywhere in the repo — appears to be an existing general-purpose open-source project (ruvnet's flagship WiFi sensing platform) mapped to this hackathon slug rather than a project built for it
Disaster/survivor-detection (MAT) capability explicitly documented as unvalidated: 'not wired into the live server (P3), no accuracy claim', synthetic-only F1 benchmarks for some fusion modules
Requires specialized ESP32 hardware and per-room calibration, making rapid post-earthquake field deployment impractical without prior installation

Pose-estimation/presence accuracy claims in README were previously retracted and revised downward (100% presence claim retracted; 82.3% held-out accuracy reported instead), indicating a history of overstated results

diffusion.recommended_angle: Do not promote as a Venezuela hackathon project; if referenced at all, frame strictly as 'research reference: existing open-source WiFi sensing tech that could inspire future disaster-response tooling' — not as a submission-ready product for victims.
gaps: No Venezuela-specific content, branding, or context anywhere in repo
No live/public demo, only a GitHub repo link reused as 'live_url'
Core disaster-detection feature (MAT) is explicitly self-documented as unvalidated and not wired into the live server
Requires physical ESP32 hardware and per-room calibration, making it undeployable in an actual post-earthquake scenario
Prior overstated accuracy claims (100% presence) were retracted, undermining credibility
Appears to be an existing massive open-source project (76k stars, created before the hackathon) mapped to this slug rather than genuinely built for it

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public web demo; live_url just points back to the GitHub repo. A YouTube demo video exists (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=k-8b8ki5nE8), but the underlying disaster-survivor detection capability is documented by the project itself as not wired into the live server and lacking real accuracy claims — so even the demo likely doesn't show a validated end-to-end earthquake-victim-detection flow.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Impressive WiFi-sensing engineering, but this is ruvnet's pre-existing 76k-star open-source project repurposed for the hackathon — not a validated tool ready to help earthquake victims today.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Detecting survivors trapped under rubble via WiFi CSI addresses a genuinely critical need in earthquake response (search & rescue triage). However, this specific submission does not solve it in a usable way for Venezuela: it's a foreign, hardware-dependent, general-purpose sensing platform with zero Venezuela-specific adaptation, whose disaster/survivor-detection module (MAT) is explicitly flagged by the project's own docs as unvalidated ('no accuracy claim', 'not wired into the live server') and requires pre-installed calibrated ESP32 hardware — impossible to deploy rapidly after a real earthquake. The underlying problem is real and severe, but this project does not currently solve it for victims.

name: Bloom-Sismo
db_description: La idea consiste en crear una Progressive Web App (PWA) de una sola página HTML que pesa menos de 150 KB en total y que contiene un Filtro de Bloom de todas las personas reportadas como Localizadas o A salvo en las distintas plataformas unificadas.

architecture.pattern: Client-only static PWA (single self-contained HTML file) using Supabase as a BaaS backend for write-only report ingestion; no custom server/API layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw PostgREST REST calls to Supabase (fetch to /rest/v1/reports) with RLS policies enforcing insert-only-anonymous / select-only-authenticated; no ORM, no custom backend
architecture.api_design: No custom API — client talks directly to Supabase's auto-generated REST endpoint with header-based auth (anon key) and 'Prefer: resolution=ignore-duplicates' for idempotent inserts
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable for a single-file constraint: build-filter.js (compiler), fetch-ong-api.js (ingestion stub), verify.js (test harness) are separated from index.html (UI+client logic+SW registration); inside index.html, CSS/state/Bloom filter/IndexedDB/Supabase/QR code are logically sectioned but all in one <script> block
architecture.modularity: Deliberately monolithic (single HTML under 150KB is the core product requirement so it can be shared via WhatsApp/offline) — low code modularity but justified by the design goal; Node-side tooling is modular
notable_patterns: Bloom filter data structure for compact offline probabilistic lookup (FNV-1a double hashing)
Service Worker with differentiated caching strategy (stale-while-revalidate for app shell, cache-first for fonts/CDN, network-only bypass for Supabase)
IndexedDB offline queue + Background Sync API for deferred report submission
Build-time data injection: Node script reads CSV/JSON sources and injects base64-encoded filter bits directly into index.html as a static build artifact

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat, minimal, and matches its own README diagram exactly (index.html, sw.js, build-filter.js, fetch-ong-api.js, verify.js, datos/, vercel.json, .github/workflows/deploy.yml)
