code_organization.naming_quality: Clear Spanish-language naming throughout, consistent conventions in both Node scripts and client code
code_organization.documentation_quality: Very thorough bilingual (mostly Spanish) README with math derivation, data flow diagrams, CSV/JSON schema docs, and step-by-step deployment instructions; inline code comments are extensive and explain rationale (e.g., why no polling, why fire-and-forget)
code_organization.reasoning: Well-documented and consistently named for a hackathon project, but the single-file-by-design constraint keeps true modularity/separation low, and there are no unit tests in the conventional sense (only a manual verification script).

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Supabase URL and anon/publishable key are hardcoded directly in the public index.html source (acceptable under Supabase's anon-key model given RLS, but there is no env-based secret management, and the anon key with open INSERT policy is exposed to anyone who reads the page source)
production_readiness.security_notes: The Supabase 'insert_anon' RLS policy allows ANYONE to insert into `reports` with no auth, rate limiting, or CAPTCHA — trivially spammable/DoS-able during a real crisis when the app is most visible; the 'select_auth' policy restricts reads but there is no admin/coordinator UI in this repo to consume those reports, so operational read access is undefined by the codebase itself; two conflicting deploy targets exist (vercel.json for Vercel + GitHub Actions workflow for GitHub Pages) which is a minor deployment-hygiene issue
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Client-side search is O(1)/offline by design, which scales well; but Supabase writes are unbounded/unauthenticated so write-path scalability is really a liability under abuse rather than legitimate load; the core 'real localized-persons data' pipeline is entirely manual (run two Node scripts, then git commit+push, redeploy) — not real-time and not automated in CI (the CI workflow's compile step is commented out)
production_readiness.reasoning: The offline search UX is solid engineering, but there is no authentication, no automated data-refresh pipeline, no structured logging/monitoring beyond Vercel Analytics, and the report-submission endpoint is wide open — none of which is acceptable for handling real missing-person data at earthquake-response scale without further hardening.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working logic — not generic AI boilerplate. The Bloom filter, IndexedDB queue, and Service Worker caching are correctly implemented and self-consistently tested via verify.js. However, all shipped data (80 records in the compiled filter, CSV/JSON samples) is clearly fabricated demo data, and the actual NGO/ally API integration (fetch-ong-api.js) is an explicit unfinished stub ('PENDIENTE') that exits early if not configured.
maturity.reasoning: 11 commits, 2 contributors, functioning core mechanic verified by a real (if manual) test script, live Vercel demo — this is a genuine working MVP, but the critical real-world data pipeline that would make it operationally useful during an actual earthquake response is not yet built.

viability.reasoning: Technically clever and genuinely functional (a real Bloom filter compiled into a sub-150KB offline-first PWA is a smart response to 2G/low-bandwidth conditions), but it is not yet deployable for real earthquake relief: the NGO data source is unconnected (placeholder endpoint), updates require a manual local build+git-push+redeploy cycle rather than any automated/real-time sync, and the public report-intake endpoint has no abuse protection — all of which need to be resolved before it could safely and usefully serve real search-and-rescue coordination.

key_features: Client-side Bloom filter (FNV-1a, k=10, ~90KB) for instant offline lookup of whether a person has been reported located, with mathematically guaranteed zero false negatives
Offline-first PWA via Service Worker: stale-while-revalidate app shell caching, cache-first font/CDN caching, network-only bypass for Supabase calls

Citizen report submission to Supabase (fire-and-forget REST POST with 8s timeout and idempotent UUID-keyed inserts) with automatic IndexedDB offline queue + Background Sync retry when connectivity returns
QR code generation (lazy-loaded) to share the compiled filter/version between devices without data connectivity

Node.js build pipeline (build-filter.js) that auto-discovers CSV/JSON data sources, builds/injects the Bloom filter into index.html as a static deployable artifact, plus a verify.js integrity/test-bank script
Stub ONG (NGO) API fetcher (fetch-ong-api.js) designed to be configured once a partner organization provides a real endpoint — currently a no-op placeholder
analysis.merge_potential: Standalone, narrowly-scoped utility (offline missing-person lookup) that could plug into a broader Build4Venezuela platform as a lightweight 'check if found' widget or PWA module, provided its Supabase report table is unified with a shared people-registry backend/API rather than each project maintaining its own siloed reports table.
red_flags: Real NGO/ally data integration is unimplemented — fetch-ong-api.js is a placeholder that exits immediately if the endpoint isn't manually configured; all shipped data is fabricated demo data

Data update workflow is entirely manual (edit files, run two Node scripts locally, git commit, git push) with no automated/scheduled sync in CI (the relevant GitHub Actions step is commented out) — unsuitable for keeping located-persons data current during an active crisis

Public Supabase 'reports' table allows unauthenticated inserts from anyone with no rate limiting or CAPTCHA, making the report-intake endpoint trivially spammable at exactly the moment traffic/attention would be highest
No admin/coordinator interface exists anywhere in the repo to read or triage the reports that get submitted, despite RLS being configured for 'authenticated' read access
Conflicting/duplicated deployment configs (vercel.json for Vercel vs. GitHub Actions workflow for GitHub Pages) suggest deployment target ambiguity
No automated tests run in CI — verify.js is a real and useful test harness but must be run manually
analysis.summary: Bloom-Sismo is a single self-contained HTML PWA (~90KB) that lets Venezuelans check offline, in 0ms, whether a family member has been reported 'located' after an earthquake, using a client-side Bloom filter compiled at build time from CSV/JSON sources. It pairs this with an IndexedDB-backed offline report queue that syncs to Supabase when connectivity returns, a Service Worker for full offline installability, and QR-based filter sharing. The core offline-search mechanic is genuinely well-engineered and tested, but the real-world data pipeline (NGO/ally API integration) is an unfinished stub, all bundled data is demo/fabricated, updates require a fully manual git-push redeploy cycle, and the public report-submission endpoint lacks any abuse protection.
analysis.project_type: Client-side Progressive Web App (offline-first missing-person lookup tool) with a thin Node.js build-tooling layer and a Supabase BaaS backend for report intake

backend: None (no custom server) — Supabase used directly as BaaS from the client
database: Supabase (PostgreSQL via PostgREST) for the 'reports' table

gaps: No live demo URL (only GitHub repo, no deployed link confirmed working)
No demo video
No screenshots
Real NGO/ally data is entirely fabricated/demo data, not real located-persons data
Report intake endpoint is open to spam/abuse — risky to publicize as-is
No admin/coordinator interface to consume submitted reports
diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame as a clever technical prototype (offline Bloom-filter lookup for 2G/no-connectivity zones) aimed at developers/NGOs to adopt and connect real data, not as a ready-to-use victim-facing tool yet.

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public web demo URL was provided beyond the GitHub repo itself; live_url points to the repo, not a deployed app. Vercel/GitHub Pages deploy configs exist in the repo (vercel.json and a GitHub Actions workflow) suggesting a demo could be live, but this could not be confirmed as accessible, and no demo video exists either.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Bloom-Sismo: revisa en 0ms y sin internet si un familiar fue localizado tras el sismo — un PWA de menos de 150KB que cabe hasta en la peor señal 2G.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Checking whether a missing family member has been found is one of the most acute needs during earthquake response, and connectivity is often severely degraded — an offline-first, sub-150KB lookup tool addresses a real and severe pain point elegantly (Bloom filter for instant client-side search, IndexedDB queue + background sync for offline report submission, QR sharing of the filter for zero-connectivity device-to-device transfer). However, the project only partially solves the problem today: the real NGO/ally data feed that would populate the filter with actual located-persons records is an unimplemented stub, so right now the tool only demonstrates the mechanism on fabricated demo data, not real victims' status.

name: Psico Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
# Red de Apoyo Psicológico Venezuela

Plataforma de apoyo psicológico para respuesta a emergencias en Venezuela. Conecta a personas que necesitan contención emocional con psicólogos verificados, y permite a los profesionales registrarse, subir sus credenciales y gestionar su disponibilidad.

**Producción:** [psicoayudaven.com](https://psicoayudaven.com)

## Características

- **Ruta de paciente** (`/ayuda`): acceso rápido a ayuda presencial o a distancia.
- **Directorio de profesionales** (`/ayuda/profesionales`): psicólogos verificados con estado en línea y contacto directo por WhatsApp. Los disponibles aparecen primero. Grilla de 2 columnas en escritorio.
- **Perfil profesional** (`/ayuda/profesionales/$id`): página pública por profesional con SEO (Open Graph, Twitter Cards, JSON-LD) y botón **Compartir** (Web Share API → portapapeles).
- **Registro de profesionales** (`/profesional/registro`): formulario en dos pasos. La credencial es un único número de colegiación agnóstico de país (+ colegio certificador opcional). Soporta psicólogos dentro y fuera de Venezuela.
- **Panel del profesional** (`/profesional/panel`): activar/desactivar disponibilidad ("En línea" / "No conectado").
- **Panel de administración** (`/admin`): revisar y verificar o rechazar registros. Acceso basado en base de datos (columna `user.role`).
- **Centro de cuenta** (`/cuenta`): hub según rol (login, panel, admin, cerrar sesión).
- **SEO** en todas las páginas públicas (títulos, descripciones, Open Graph, canonical vía `src/lib/seo.ts`).
- **Autenticación** con Better Auth (email + contraseña).
- UI _mobile-first_ con estética _liquid glass_ sobre la paleta Medicall. Navegación: barra inferior en móvil, barra superior en escritorio.

analysis.summary: psico-support (Red de Apoyo Psicológico Venezuela) is a real, actively-deployed TanStack Start + Cloudflare Workers/D1/R2 application connecting earthquake/crisis victims in Venezuela with verified volunteer psychologists via a public directory and WhatsApp contact, backed by a genuine admin-verification workflow, Drizzle-managed SQLite schema with 16 migrations, Better Auth authentication, and R2-based credential document storage — a working MVP bordering on polished, but missing automated tests and CI/CD, which matters given its intended use in live emergency response.
analysis.project_type: web-app

backend: TanStack Start server functions (RPC, Node/Workers runtime)

architecture.pattern: Full-stack React meta-framework (TanStack Start) deployed as a single Cloudflare Worker, with file-based routes containing colocated server functions (RPC-style, not a separate REST/GraphQL API)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM against Cloudflare D1 (SQLite), with 16 versioned migrations in drizzle/
architecture.api_design: No traditional REST/GraphQL API surface; uses TanStack Start createServerFn RPCs colocated in src/server/*.ts, called directly from route components via TanStack Query
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable: src/server holds domain logic (professionals, follow-ups, audio-stories, locations), src/db holds schema/db access, src/lib holds auth/utils, src/routes holds UI+route-level server fns; some server files (professionals.ts at ~2000 lines) are large and mix multiple concerns (registration, docs, admin verification, availability)
architecture.modularity: Domain-oriented modules per feature area (professionals, follow-ups, audio-stories, locations) rather than layered; auth centralized in src/lib/auth.ts as a lazily-built singleton to accommodate D1's per-request binding
notable_patterns: Better Auth with Drizzle adapter and DB-column-based admin role (no env var admin list)
R2 object storage for certificates/support docs/audio, keyed by userId/professionalId with base64 transport for atomicity with DB inserts
PWA with offline shell/service worker only enabled in production builds
Sentry integration (optional via env)
Extensive inline 'ponytail' comments documenting non-obvious design tradeoffs

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear, conventional TanStack Start layout (src/routes, src/server, src/db, src/lib, src/components, drizzle/ for migrations); feature areas map cleanly to files
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish-domain route names (ayuda, profesional/panel, admin) and clear TS identifiers; English code/comments mixed with Spanish UI content
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above-average: detailed README (in Spanish) covering setup/deploy/env, AGENTS.md and docs/ directory with design notes, plus abundant inline comments explaining rationale for non-obvious decisions
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for its size (16k LOC) with a coherent domain-driven structure and unusually thorough in-code rationale, though a few server files (professionals.ts ~2000 lines) have grown large and could be split.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Uses wrangler secret put for BETTER_AUTH_SECRET/BETTER_AUTH_URL/Sentry DSN in production; .env.local for local dev with .env.example documented; t3-oss/env-core (Zod) validates client env vars
production_readiness.security_notes: Auth is real (Better Auth + Drizzle adapter, email/password, DB-role-based admin, per-server-fn session checks with 'Debes iniciar sesión' guards seen throughout professionals.ts); however no visible rate limiting on public write endpoints (registration, follow-up creation) which is a risk for an emergency-response public app; no automated tests to catch regressions in access-control logic
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Cloudflare Workers + D1 + R2 is a reasonable serverless-at-edge stack for a hackathon-scale support directory app; D1 (SQLite) has known write-concurrency/size limits but is adequate for a regional directory/booking use case, not for high-volume chat/comms
production_readiness.reasoning: This is a genuinely deployed (custom domain psicoayudaven.com configured, live push today), functioning full-stack app with real auth, DB migrations, file storage, and Sentry, but lacks automated tests/CI and explicit rate limiting for a system meant to handle sensitive mental-health/crisis submissions from the public.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, actively developed application with substantial custom domain logic (professional verification workflow, availability, follow-ups, audio stories, credential uploads) — not generic boilerplate, though scaffolding remnants exist (unused en/de i18n messages files despite the app being Spanish-only)
maturity.reasoning: 16 migrations, ~2000-line professionals server module with real business rules, deployed to a custom domain, Sentry configured — clearly beyond prototype stage; however zero test files despite vitest being configured, and no CI/CD pipeline, are notable gaps for something meant to be deployed to help real earthquake victims.

viability.reasoning: A working, deployed MVP with a coherent product loop (patients find/contact verified psychologists via WhatsApp; professionals register, upload credentials, and are admin-verified) is a plausible and useful piece of emergency mental-health infrastructure, though it depends on volunteer psychologist supply/verification workflow being actively maintained and lacks tests/CI/rate-limiting that matter for reliability under real crisis load.

key_features: Public directory of verified psychologists with online-status indicator and direct WhatsApp contact
Two-step professional registration with credential (colegiación) number and optional certificate/document upload to R2
Admin panel (DB-role based) to verify/reject professional registrations
Professional availability toggle (online/offline) and follow-up scheduling
Audio 'stories'/resources and self-help content routes (breathing, grounding, first-aid, self-check)
PWA with offline shell and installable app support
Better Auth email/password authentication with session-gated server functions
SEO (OG/Twitter/JSON-LD) on public professional profile pages
analysis.merge_potential: Could integrate as the mental-health support module of a broader disaster-response platform (e.g., linking from a shelter/aid-mapping app's resource directory), or its verified-professional-registry pattern (credential upload + admin review + R2 storage) could be reused for other volunteer-verification flows (medical, legal aid) in a combined Build4Venezuela platform.
red_flags: No automated tests despite vitest/testing-library being installed as dependencies (test script exists but zero test files found)
No CI/CD pipeline (no .github/workflows or equivalent) to catch regressions before deploy
No visible rate limiting/abuse protection on public write endpoints (registration, follow-ups) for a system likely to see spiky, sensitive public traffic during a crisis
Leftover unused i18n scaffolding (en/de messages) inconsistent with the app being Spanish-only, suggesting some boilerplate was never cleaned up
Single contributor and very young repo (created 2026-06-27, ~50 commits over a few days) — bus-factor risk for something intended for long-term crisis deployment

diffusion.recommended_angle: Mental-health-first disaster response: 'A real person answers you' — a free, verified directory connecting earthquake victims in Venezuela with volunteer psychologists via WhatsApp, live today at psicoayudaven.com.
gaps: No demo video to show the product flow
No confirmed screenshots of the live professional directory (JS-rendered content not independently verified)
No evidence yet of real professional signups/usage numbers to cite as social proof
Single contributor / very young repo (created 2026-06-27) is a risk to mention cautiously in diffusion copy

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch on https://psicoayudaven.com/ succeeded and shows a real operational platform: homepage advertises "30+ verified professionals," a clear CTA ("Necesito ayuda ahora") linking to remote therapist matching, professional registration pathway, self-care content, and messaging emphasizing human (not AI/bot) responders. A follow-up fetch of /ayuda/profesionales returned only nav/header content, likely because the directory listing is client-side rendered and WebFetch does not execute JS — this is a tool limitation, not necessarily evidence the feature is broken, but it means the actual directory contents (real professional entries) could not be independently confirmed via fetch.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Red de Apoyo Psicológico Venezuela: conecta gratis y en minutos con psicólogos verificados por WhatsApp — contención emocional real, sin bots, para las víctimas del terremoto.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Psychological/emotional trauma is a well-documented, urgent-but-often-neglected consequence of earthquakes, and Venezuela's mental-health infrastructure is already strained pre-disaster. This project directly addresses that gap by connecting victims with verified volunteer psychologists for free contention emocional via a low-friction channel (WhatsApp), and separately builds real infrastructure (credential upload, admin verification, availability status) to scale and vet the professional supply side rather than just listing unverified contacts.

name: Puente de Vida
db_description:
# Puente de Vida

**Puente de Vida** es una plataforma de coordinación de emergencias **offline-first**. Su misión es asegurar que los reportes de ayuda humanitaria y rescate sigan fluyendo de forma continua, incluso durante caídas masivas de conectividad o colapso de las telecomunicaciones.

La plataforma destaca por su alta modularidad, facilidad de integración y resiliencia en terreno.

---

### ¿Cuál es la meta?

* **Propósito Humano:** Colaborar con los equipos de respuesta en el terreno para ayudar a salvar vidas mediante información oportuna y organizada.
* **Propósito Técnico:** Resolver los problemas críticos de comunicación, registro y logística que surgen cuando no hay acceso a Internet.

---

### ¿Cómo funciona?

El sistema opera bajo un flujo de sincronización inteligente en 4 pasos:

1. **Captura sin conexión (PWA):** Los reportes se registran en una aplicación web progresiva y se guardan localmente de forma segura en `IndexedDB`.
2. **Sincronización en segundo plano:** Al recuperar señal, un `Service Worker` transmite automáticamente los reportes pendientes al servidor central.
3. **Clasificación y deduplicación:** El backend procesa cada reporte usando una lógica híbrida (heurística local ultrarrápida + inteligencia artificial opcional compatible con `OpenAI`) para extraer coordenadas, calcular prioridades y fusionar duplicados.
4. **Coordinación en terreno:** Los operadores gestionan los recursos y voluntarios desde un mapa interactivo y un panel en tiempo real según la gravedad de cada situación.

---
### Equipo
Agostina Silva — Coordinación | [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/agostinadevdc)

Christian Chávez— UI / UX | [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/christianchavez-io/)

Deyanira Alcaraz — Diseño / CM / marketing | [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/deyanira-alcaraz/)


---

### Arquitectura de Flujo

```
Canales (WhatsApp/Telegram/Web) 
   └─► PWA Offline (IndexedDB) 
         └─► Service Worker (Sync) 
               └─► API REST (Fastify) 
                     └─► Clasificación Híbrida (Heurística + IA) 
                           └─► Panel Coordinador (React + Leaflet)
```

---

### Stack Tecnológico

* **Lenguaje:** `TypeScript` (Fullstack)
* **Frontend:** `React 19` + `Vite` + `TanStack Router` + `Tailwind CSS` + `vite-plugin-pwa`
* **Backend:** `Fastify` + `@fastify/rate-limit` + `PostgreSQL`
* **Persistencia Local:** `Dexie.js` / `IndexedDB`
* **Mapas:** `Leaflet` + `OpenStreetMap`
* **Inteligencia Artificial:** Heurística de texto / API compatible con `OpenAI`

---
### Privacidad y Seguridad de Datos

* **Protección de Datos Sensibles:** Enmascaramiento automático de identificaciones y resguardo nativo de datos de menores de edad.
* **Cifrado Local:** Encriptación simétrica AES-256-GCM de todos los reportes persistidos localmente en el navegador (`IndexedDB`).
* **Acceso por Roles (RBAC):** Filtrado dinámico de información en la API según el nivel de autorización (`viewer`, `operator`, `admin`) para resguardar la privacidad.

---

### Modularidad y Buenas Prácticas

* **Arquitectura Conmutable:** Permite cambiar fácilmente entre simulación local y proveedores reales (WhatsApp/IA) mediante variables de entorno.
* **Ingesta Inteligente:** Optimizado para recibir y unificar reportes de herramientas externas, usando inserciones masivas por lotes en base de datos.

analysis.summary: Puente de Vida is a monorepo emergency-coordination platform (Fastify+Postgres API, a fairly mature Vite/React PWA, and a newly-started Expo mobile app) for reporting incidents/needs, triaging with a local heuristic or pluggable LLM classifier, deduping reports, managing shelters/orgs/volunteers, and a mock/generic WhatsApp integration. The API and web layers contain real, non-trivial business logic (privacy-tiered data filtering, audit logging, rate limiting, session auth, offline sync with IndexedDB), but the codebase just underwent a 'mobile-first' pivot and the new Expo app is almost entirely placeholder screens, and there are zero automated tests anywhere in the repo.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack monorepo: Fastify/Postgres REST API + Vite/React PWA (functional) + Expo/React Native mobile app (mostly scaffolding, in-progress rewrite)

database: PostgreSQL (raw SQL via node-pg, no ORM)
infra_deploy: GitHub Actions CI (lint/typecheck/build only, no test execution)
ai_ml: Optional OpenAI-compatible provider (switchable via AI_PROVIDER env)

architecture.pattern: Layered monorepo: apps/api (Fastify routes -> services -> raw SQL via pg), apps/web (React PWA with IndexedDB offline store + sync), apps/mobile (Expo Router, mostly stubs), packages/shared (zod schemas/types/api-client shared across apps)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw SQL via 'pg' Pool with parameterized queries (query/queryOne helpers in db.ts); schema embedded as an idempotent SQL string with CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS / ALTER TABLE IF NOT EXISTS migrations run at boot (migrate.ts)
architecture.api_design: REST-ish Fastify routes grouped by domain (reports, sync, dashboard, resources, whatsapp, admin, orgs, external) with zod request validation, custom session-token auth (in-memory Map + role hierarchy viewer/operator/admin), rate limiting (global + stricter login limiter)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonably clean: routes call service functions (services/reports.ts, services/ingestion.ts, services/whatsapp.ts) which call db.ts; privacy.ts centralizes role-based field filtering/masking applied consistently across routes
architecture.modularity: Good use of npm workspaces (api/web/mobile/shared); shared package exposes typed api-client and zod schemas reused by web and mobile
notable_patterns: Pluggable AI provider abstraction (none/openai) with graceful fallback to local heuristic on failure or missing keys
Pluggable/generic WhatsApp Business API adapter with a free 'mock' mode by default
Role-based response filtering/masking (phones, addresses, minor-protection) in privacy.ts applied per endpoint
Offline-first web client: IndexedDB local store + background sync + client-side crypto (crypto.ts) for local encryption
Idempotent embedded SQL schema run as a lightweight migration on startup instead of a migration framework

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear monorepo layout (apps/api, apps/web, apps/mobile, packages/shared) with route/service/lib separation inside each app; docs/ folder with architecture, technical and privacy docs in Spanish
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish/English mixed naming (reports, shelters, ai/heuristic, privacy.ts) that matches domain concepts
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average for a hackathon project: README, ROADMAP.md, ROADMAP-MOBILE.md, and docs/ARQUITECTURA.md, docs/PRIVACIDAD.md, docs/TECNICO.md; config.ts is heavily commented explaining provider switches
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized layered monorepo with real separation of routes/services/db and shared types package; loses a point because the mobile app directory is currently just route stubs mixed into the same tree, slightly muddying the current state vs documented roadmap.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Uses env vars with .env.example (no secrets committed); default ADMIN_PASS in docker-compose/.env.example is a weak hardcoded default (PdV2026!) meant to be overridden, which is a real risk if deployed as-is
production_readiness.security_notes: Custom scrypt-based password hashing, session tokens via crypto.randomBytes, role-based access control, rate limiting (incl. stricter login limiter), parameterized SQL throughout (no obvious injection), privacy-tier field masking, audit logging of report views/logins. Session store is in-memory (Map) which won't scale across multiple API instances and loses sessions on restart — acceptable for a single-instance MVP but not production-grade for HA deployment.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single Fastify instance + Postgres pool with conservative defaults (pool max 10); no Redis session backing despite config.ts mentioning it as optional/unused; ingestion loop and WhatsApp are synchronous/interval-based, fine for MVP scale but not built for high concurrency or multi-region resilience needed in a real disaster response at scale.
production_readiness.reasoning: The API has real production-conscious touches (Docker multi-stage builds, healthchecks, rate limiting, auth, migrations-on-boot, structured logging via Fastify/pino) but lacks automated tests, uses in-memory session state, and ships a weak default admin password — solid MVP/pilot readiness but not yet hardened for unattended production use in a crisis.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, substantial custom logic (not generic AI boilerplate) — e.g. 632-line heuristic classifier, dedupe engine, privacy masking, offline sync — but the codebase is mid-pivot: the just-added mobile app is skeletal (many screens are ~20-line placeholder components) and there is zero test coverage despite a 'test' npm script pointing to a non-existent ./test directory.
maturity.reasoning: 19 commits over ~5 days culminating in a 'rework: mobile first' pivot; substantial working web+API but a very fresh, incomplete mobile rewrite and no tests anywhere, so overall maturity is mid-MVP rather than polished.

viability.reasoning: The web+API stack is a genuinely usable MVP for incident/needs reporting and coordination with real privacy and auth considerations relevant to a sensitive humanitarian context, but the just-started mobile pivot (the stated 'target principal' per README) is largely unbuilt, there are no automated tests to guard against regressions, and default credentials/in-memory sessions need hardening before trusting it with real earthquake victim data.

key_features: Incident/need reporting via PWA and WhatsApp with local + optional LLM triage classification
Automatic report deduplication/grouping engine
Role-based (viewer/operator/admin) authenticated dashboard with masked PII and audit logging
Offline-first PWA with IndexedDB local storage, client-side encryption, and background sync
Shelter, organization, volunteer, and supply management with map view
Pluggable external data ingestion from third-party missing-persons APIs
Mobile app (Expo) scaffolded with role-based navigation (citizen/volunteer/coordinator/organization/admin) but mostly placeholder screens
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate to merge with other projects covering people-finder/missing-persons datasets (it already has an external_sources ingestion mechanism for missing-persons APIs) or with a more mature mobile-native app, given its solid API/privacy/auth backbone that other teams could adopt as a shared backend.
red_flags: Zero automated tests despite a package.json 'test' script referencing a nonexistent ./test directory; CI only runs lint/typecheck/build, no test execution
In-memory session store (Map) — sessions are lost on restart/won't work with multiple API replicas, a scalability/reliability risk for real deployment
Weak default admin credentials (ADMIN_PASS=PdV2026!) baked into docker-compose/.env.example, risky if not rotated before deployment
Mobile app (per README, the primary target platform) is largely unimplemented placeholder screens as of the latest commit — core feature parity with the web app is not yet there

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as a strong technical MVP / backend architecture story (offline-first PWA, privacy-by-design, AI triage) for a developer/technical audience once a real live demo and screenshots exist — not yet a consumer-facing spotlight piece.
gaps: No working live demo URL (current 'live_url' is a private Google Doc, not a deployed app)
No demo video
No screenshots of the actual UI
No evidence of real users or field testing
Mobile app (the stated primary target platform) is mostly placeholder screens

evaluation.live_demo_notes: The 'live_url' provided is a Google Docs link (a Technical Design Document titled "Puente de Vida_TDD_1.0"), not a deployed application. WebFetch could only see the Google Docs UI chrome, confirming this is documentation, not a live product. There is no actual public demo URL to test the PWA, API, or dashboard.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Puente de Vida: una plataforma offline-first de coordinación de emergencias con IA para triaje y mapas en tiempo real — un MVP técnico sólido que aún necesita una demo pública para mostrar su impacto.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Coordinating humanitarian aid/rescue reporting when connectivity collapses (a very real scenario in earthquake disasters) is a genuine, high-value problem. The project's design (offline-first PWA with IndexedDB, background sync, deduplication, privacy-tiered RBAC for sensitive data including minors) shows real understanding of field conditions during a disaster. However, the primary target platform per their own roadmap (mobile app) is still mostly placeholder screens, and there's no evidence yet of use by actual response teams or victims, so real-world validation is unclear.

name: Rastro
db_description:
# Rastro - Plataforma de Respuesta a Emergencias

Rastro es una plataforma de respuesta a emergencias post-terremoto, diseñada para facilitar la comunicación y logística en situaciones críticas. Está optimizada para ofrecer fricción mínima (no requiere registro para reportar) y garantizar un flujo de información verificada.

## 🎯 ¿Para qué sirve?

La aplicación está dividida en tres módulos principales:

1. **Mapa Colaborativo (`/mapa`):** Muestra reportes geolocalizados en tiempo real sobre necesidades de rescate, suministros y estado de vías.
2. **Buscador de Personas (`/personas`):** Permite registrar y buscar personas en refugios (por nombre o cédula de identidad), facilitando la reunificación familiar.
3. **Panel de Moderación (`/moderacion`):** Un área restringida donde los moderadores revisan, aprueban o rechazan los reportes ciudadanos antes de publicarlos en el mapa, evitando desinformación.

## 🛠️ Stack Tecnológico

- **Framework:** [Next.js 14](https://nextjs.org/) (App Router)
- **Lenguaje:** [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- **Estilos:** [Tailwind CSS](https://tailwindcss.com/)
- **Base de Datos, Auth, Storage y Realtime:** [Supabase](https://supabase.com/)
- **Mapas:** [Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) + `react-leaflet` + OpenStreetMap
- **Formularios y Validación:** `react-hook-form` + `zod`
- **Búsqueda:** `fuse.js` (Búsqueda fuzzy)
- **Íconos:** `lucide-react`
- **Hosting y Cron Jobs:** [Vercel](https://vercel.com/)

analysis.summary: Rastro is a Next.js 14 + Supabase single-app MVP for post-earthquake emergency response in Venezuela, with three real modules: a collaborative map of citizen-reported incidents (rescue/supplies/road status) with moderation workflow, a missing-persons/shelter registry-search tool, and a moderator approval panel. The code is coherent, uses Zod validation, RLS policies, a service-role/anon Supabase client split, Realtime subscriptions, and a Vercel cron job to auto-archive old reports, and is live-deployed at rastro-flax.vercel.app.
analysis.project_type: web app (Next.js full-stack)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with server route handlers calling a BaaS (Supabase) directly
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (PostgREST) — anon client for public reads, service-role admin client for privileged writes/moderation, plus a couple of Postgres RPC functions (incrementar_upvote, buscar_personas)
architecture.api_design: REST-ish Next.js route handlers under src/app/api (reportes, personas, refugios, moderacion, moderacion/login, cron/archivar) with Zod-validated bodies and consistent JSON error responses
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear split between client-safe supabase.ts (anon key) and server-only supabase-admin.ts (service role), centralized Zod schemas in validaciones.ts reused across API routes and forms
architecture.modularity: Feature-based routing (mapa, reportar, personas, moderacion) each with page.tsx + Client component, plus shared hooks (useReportes, usePersonas, useGeolocalizacion) and small ui/mapa component folders
notable_patterns: Supabase Realtime subscription in useReportes for live map updates
RLS policies enforcing insert-as-pending and public read of only published/unverified rows
SECURITY DEFINER Postgres function for atomic upvote increments
Vercel Cron with Bearer-token auth to auto-archive stale reports

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, conventional Next.js App Router layout with logical grouping by feature and shared lib/hooks/types/components directories; only ~2800 LOC total so it stays easy to navigate
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (reportes, personas, refugios, moderacion) matched 1:1 between DB schema, types, and API routes
code_organization.documentation_quality: README, AGENTS.md and MANUAL.md all present with real usage/demo flow descriptions, not generic boilerplate
code_organization.reasoning: Small but well-structured codebase with consistent naming and real docs; loses a point for having no code comments beyond file headers and no architecture diagram.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env.example documents required vars (Supabase URL/anon/service-role keys, MODERACION_SECRET, CRON_SECRET); service-role key correctly isolated to server-only module with a warning comment
production_readiness.security_notes: Moderator 'auth' is a single shared plaintext secret compared to a cookie value (no hashing, no per-user accounts, no rate limiting on login attempts); RLS is reasonably designed (public insert forced to 'pendiente', public select restricted to visible states) but the 'personas' table SELECT/INSERT is fully open to anon with no auth, meaning anyone can read all registered persons' names, birthdates, health status, and family phone numbers (PII exposure risk for a vulnerable population) and anyone can spam-insert fake person records; no rate limiting anywhere; cron endpoint auth is skipped entirely outside NODE_ENV=production.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Relies entirely on Supabase managed Postgres + Vercel serverless functions, which scales fine for hackathon/pilot load; no caching layer, no pagination beyond a hardcoded limit(100) on personas search, no queueing for spikes in report submissions.
production_readiness.reasoning: Deployed and functionally real (live URL, working Supabase backend, RLS, cron), but lacks structured logging/monitoring, has a weak single-secret moderator auth model, and exposes sensitive personas PII to any anonymous client — significant concerns for an app meant to protect real disaster victims' data.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, working implementation (not boilerplate) but very early-stage
maturity.reasoning: 6 commits total, single contributor, no automated tests, no CI/CD pipeline; despite functioning end-to-end logic, this is a few-day hackathon build with no hardening or quality gates yet.

viability.reasoning: The core loop (report incident -> moderate -> publish to live map; register/search displaced persons in shelters) is genuinely implemented and deployed, addressing real earthquake-response needs (family reunification, aid/rescue reporting, shelter tracking), but the open PII exposure on the personas table, single-secret moderation auth, and total absence of tests/CI mean it needs real security and reliability work before trusting it with real victims' data at scale.

key_features: Public collaborative incident map (rescue/supply/road-status reports) with geolocation and upvoting
Moderation queue with approve/reject/unverified workflow before reports go public
Realtime updates on the map via Supabase Realtime subscriptions
Missing-persons/shelter registry with fuzzy + cédula search across refugios
Automatic daily archival of stale reports via Vercel Cron
Zod-validated forms and API inputs shared between client and server
analysis.merge_potential: Could serve as the incident-reporting/map + shelter-registry module in a combined platform; its Supabase schema (refugios/reportes/personas) and moderation pattern are reusable building blocks, but its personas PII exposure should be fixed before combining with any people-finder feature from another project.
red_flags: personas table RLS allows anonymous SELECT and INSERT with no restriction, exposing full name, birthdate, health status, and family contact phone numbers of registered displaced persons to any unauthenticated client
Moderator authentication is a single shared plaintext secret string compared directly (no hashing, no per-account auth, no brute-force protection)
No automated tests or CI pipeline of any kind
Cron endpoint authorization is bypassed whenever NODE_ENV !== 'production', and CRON_SECRET defaults to empty string if unset

diffusion.recommended_angle: Show the three real modules in action (mapa colaborativo, búsqueda de personas en refugios, panel de moderación) with a short screen-recording, since there's no demo video yet — emphasize 'reporta sin registrarte, encuentra a tu familia' as the hook.
gaps: no demo video exists — needs a screen recording since the live app is real and demoable
no screenshots captured/curated yet for social assets
no evidence of real users/pilot testing with actual disaster-response orgs
PII exposure bug on personas table should be flagged/fixed before wide public promotion, since promoting it could actually funnel real victims' sensitive data into an insecure table

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Verified via curl: https://rastro-six.vercel.app redirects (307) to /mapa, and /mapa, /personas, /moderacion all return 200 with fully rendered Next.js pages (real nav, title 'Rastro — Respuesta a Emergencias', map page chunk, forms). This is a working, deployed multi-page app, not a static landing page — WebFetch itself was blocked by the redirect but curl confirms real functional routes.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Rastro: reporta emergencias sin registrarte, encuentra a tus familiares en refugios y coordina ayuda en un mapa colaborativo en tiempo real — ya funcionando en vivo.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Addresses two of the most acute post-earthquake needs: family reunification (missing-persons/shelter registry with fuzzy + cédula search) and real-time coordination of rescue/supply/road-status reports, both with zero-friction reporting (no signup required) which matters enormously in a crisis where victims have limited connectivity/time. A moderation layer to prevent disinformation on the map is a thoughtful, realistic touch for chaotic post-disaster information environments.

name: Red Acopio Vnzla
db_description:
# Centros de Acopio — Ayuda que llega donde se necesita

## ¿Qué es?

Una aplicación que conecta a quienes **quieren ayudar** con los **centros de acopio** que reciben y reparten insumos durante una emergencia.

En vez de no saber a dónde llevar una donación o qué hace falta realmente, la app muestra, en un mapa, los centros cercanos y **qué necesita cada uno en este momento**: agua, medicamentos, ropa, alimentos o herramientas.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

- **A quien quiere donar:** encuentra el centro más cercano y ve qué le hace falta, para llevar lo que de verdad se necesita y no lo que sobra.
- **A los centros:** llevan el control de lo que entra y sale, y avisan automáticamente cuándo un insumo está **urgente**, **normal** o **suficiente**.
- **A la comunidad:** la información está siempre actualizada y es confiable, porque un equipo revisa que los centros sean reales.

El resultado: menos desorden, menos desperdicio y la ayuda llegando más rápido a donde hace falta.

## ¿Quiénes la usan?

- **Personas que donan** — buscan un centro y ven sus necesidades.
- **Voluntarios** — ayudan a registrar lo que llega y se reparte.
- **Responsables de un centro (jefes)** — administran el centro, su equipo y su inventario.
- **Equipo de verificación** — confirma que cada centro sea legítimo.

## Cómo se usa

### Si quieres donar
1. Abre la app y mira el **mapa** o la **lista de centros** cerca de ti.
2. Entra a un centro para ver **qué necesita** y si está **recibiendo en este momento**.
3. Arma tu donación en la app y llévala al centro: ahí la registran al recibirla.

> No necesitas crear una cuenta para mirar el mapa y las necesidades.

### Si eres voluntario o responsable de un centro
1. **Crea tu cuenta** (con tu cédula y una contraseña, o con Google).
2. **Registra tu centro**: nombre, ubicación en el mapa y el inventario con el que inicias (puedes cargarlo a mano o importarlo desde un Excel).
3. Suma a tu equipo: **invita voluntarios** con un enlace o un código QR.
4. **Mantén el inventario al día**: cada vez que entra una donación o sale un envío, queda registrado. El sistema lleva la cuenta por ti.
5. **Configura las alertas**: define, para cada insumo, cuándo se considera *urgente* y cuándo *suficiente*. A partir de ahí, **la etiqueta de cada insumo se actualiza sola** según el stock, sin que tengas que cambiarla a mano.

### Cómo se mantiene la confianza
- Un **equipo de verificación** revisa los centros y los marca como verificados.
- Cualquier persona puede **reportar** un centro que ya no exista o tenga información incorrecta.

## En resumen

La app es el puente entre la **ayuda disponible** y la **ayuda necesaria**: muestra dónde están los centros, qué les falta y lo mantiene actualizado, para que cada donación cuente.

analysis.summary: A genuinely mature, well-tested Turborepo monorepo (NestJS API + Next.js 16 web + Prisma) implementing a real emergency-logistics platform for Venezuelan donation collection centers post-earthquake, with device-fingerprint identity, transactional inventory tracking, QR-based intake, volunteer/admin verification workflows, Redis caching/rate-limiting, Sentry monitoring, full CI (build/test/migration-drift checks), Docker + Railway deploy config, and a documented history of real security vulnerabilities found and fixed.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web platform (monorepo: REST API backend + Next.js frontend + Prisma DB layer) for emergency donation-center logistics

architecture.pattern: Turborepo monorepo: NestJS REST API (apps/api) + Next.js 16 App Router web (apps/web) + shared Prisma DB package + shared Venezuela domain-data package
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Prisma 7 with @prisma/adapter-pg driver adapter, shared singleton client in packages/database, pooled connections (DB_POOL_MAX)
architecture.api_design: REST via NestJS controllers/services/guards (centros, insumos, historial, envios, usuarios, auth, admin), device-fingerprint based identity (no traditional login required to browse) plus JWT auth (cédula+password, Google OAuth, admin login)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear layering: guards for authZ (SesionGuard, IdentidadGuard, VoluntarioGuard, JefeGuard, AdminGuard, RateLimitGuard), services for business logic, Prisma for persistence, Redis for caching/rate-limiting, frontend hooks/lib layer separate from components
architecture.modularity: Well modularized into apps/packages workspaces with barrel index.ts files per feature folder per project conventions in AGENTS.md
notable_patterns: Fixed-window Redis rate limiter
Read-through Redis cache with version-key invalidation for directory listing
Transactional inventory movements (Historial) instead of direct mutation of cantidadTotal
Device fingerprint + progressive identity completion instead of forced login
Security sentinel log (.jules/sentinel.md) documenting real vulnerabilities found and fixed (IDOR, validation bypass, missing security headers)
External cédula verification integration (api.cedula.com.ve) with fail-open handling

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean monorepo layout: apps/api, apps/web, packages/database, packages/venezuela, docs/, consistent Next.js App Router route-group conventions ((app)/(full)), colocated tests next to source files
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (Centro, Insumo, Historial, Voluntario) matching the Venezuelan context, clear and descriptive throughout
code_organization.documentation_quality: Extensive docs/ folder with per-feature markdown specs, detailed README with endpoint table and explicit 'deferred on purpose' section, AGENTS.md with coding conventions, inline comments explaining non-obvious decisions ('ponytail' markers for known tech debt)
code_organization.reasoning: Extremely well organized for a hackathon project: workspace packages, barrel files, colocated unit tests (54 test files), docs per feature, and an explicit conventions guide (AGENTS.md) that is actually followed in the code.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env.example with clear placeholders, JWT_SECRET/GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SENTRY_DSN/cédula API tokens externalized, no secrets committed
production_readiness.security_notes: Helmet, CORS allow-list via WEB_ORIGIN, class-validator ValidationPipe with whitelist, bcrypt password hashing, JWT-based guards with role checks, Redis-backed rate limiting on write endpoints, and a documented security-sentinel log showing 3 real vulnerabilities (IDOR, validation bypass via TS intersection types, missing security headers) that were found and fixed during development
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Redis caching (30s TTL) for public directory, small Postgres connection pool sized for hobby-tier DBs, geospatial ordering done in-memory (explicitly documented as a known limitation to migrate to PostGIS if centers scale to thousands), photo uploads stored on local disk (explicitly flagged as needing object storage for real production use)
production_readiness.reasoning: Has CI (3 workflows: API build+test, Web build+test, DB migration drift check against a real Postgres service), Dockerfile for API, railway.json deploy config with pre-deploy migration step, Sentry instrumentation on both API and web, and honest documentation of known gaps (no PostGIS, local disk uploads, no DB trigger for totals) rather than hiding them.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 198 commits from 4 contributors (including a bot-assisted security review workflow), 54 test files covering services/controllers/components, 3 CI workflows enforcing tests+build+migration integrity, and a documented history of real security vulnerabilities found and remediated — this is far beyond boilerplate or empty scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: Addresses a concrete, well-scoped problem (donation center directory, inventory tracking, QR-based intake, volunteer/admin verification) with a working, tested, deployable full-stack implementation; main gaps before real-world use are moving off local-disk photo storage, load-testing the in-memory geo sort, and completing a couple of explicitly deferred UI pieces (donation cart/QR scan front-end).

key_features: Public directory of donation centers with geolocation-based (Haversine) sorting and Redis caching
Center creation with initial inventory import (manual or Excel upload) and photo evidence
Inventory movement tracking via transactional Historial records (never direct mutation of totals)
QR-code based batch drop-off/reception flow for donations
Volunteer invitation system via JWT tokens (link/QR) with role-based guards (JEFE vs VOLUNTARIO)
Admin/moderation panel for verifying or rejecting centers with accountability tracking
Cédula identity verification against external government-adjacent API (api.cedula.com.ve) with fail-open handling
Google OAuth and cédula+password authentication with progressive profile completion
Community-driven reporting of invalid/incorrect centers
Redis-backed rate limiting on write-heavy endpoints
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone candidate; could integrate with a shelter-mapping or people-finder project via shared geolocation/center-directory infrastructure, or expose its centros/insumos API for other aid-coordination tools to consume.
red_flags: Uploaded photos stored on local disk rather than object storage — will not survive redeploys/scale on platforms like Railway with ephemeral filesystems
Geospatial sorting done in-memory rather than PostGIS — could become a bottleneck if center count grows into the thousands
cantidadTotal invariant enforced only at the application layer (transaction), not by a DB trigger, so it could be corrupted by out-of-band writes
Two admin accounts noted as seeded manually with no admin-management UI yet

diffusion.recommended_angle: From-the-ground logistics fix for earthquake relief: a real, tested platform (not just a landing page) that tells donors exactly what each acopio center needs right now, and gives center managers live inventory control with urgency alerts — built and deployed on a custom domain with 198 commits, 54 tests, and documented security fixes.
gaps: No public screenshots captured yet of the actual map/dashboard UI (site needs JS to render, so static crawlers/social-card previews show a 'Cargando...' loading state — should generate real screenshots or a GIF for social posts)
No evidence yet of real-world adoption/verified centers (real-users asset not confirmed)
Repo has no license file

evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site (https://www.redacopiovnzla.com/) returns HTTP 200, serves a real deployed Next.js 16 app with correct branding, meta description, and PWA manifest ("Red Acopio Venezuela — directorio de centros de acopio y donaciones de emergencia en Venezuela"). A static-HTML fetch only sees a client-side "Cargando…" loading state because the app is a JS-hydrated SPA that fetches data from a separate NestJS API at runtime — this is expected SPA behavior, not a broken deploy. The backend API is not exposed under the same domain (common /api/* paths 404, confirming it's a separate service per the documented monorepo/Railway architecture) so it could not be independently probed without a browser, but the codebase analysis shows a fully implemented, tested REST API (guards, rate limiting, Redis caching, Prisma transactions) behind it. Overall this reads as a genuinely working, deployed product, not a static mockup — though a human/JS-rendering check (headless browser) would be needed to see the live map and center listings render end-to-end.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Red Acopio Vnzla conecta donaciones con el centro de acopio más cercano que de verdad las necesita — un mapa en vivo, inventario transaccional y alertas de urgencia, ya desplegado en redacopiovnzla.com.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake donation logistics in Venezuela suffer from exactly the failure mode this app targets: donors don't know which centers are legitimate, where they are, or what they actually need right now, leading to misallocated donations (excess of some items, shortages of others) and wasted volunteer effort reconciling inventory by hand. The product directly maps donors to nearby verified centers, shows live urgency-tagged inventory needs (urgent/normal/sufficient), tracks intake/outflow via transactional history records (not ad-hoc counters), supports QR-based batch drop-off, and includes a verification/reporting workflow to keep the directory trustworthy — this is a well-scoped, concrete solution to a real coordination bottleneck in disaster response, not a generic donation platform.

name: Reencuentros Terremotos Venezuela
db_description:
## **Nuestra propuesta de valor**

Plataforma que centraliza reportes visuales (fotos y videos) recientes y georreferenciados de quienes necesitan ser encontrados —personas y animales—. Esta información es capturada en el terreno por medios de comunicación acreditados e instituciones de ayuda autorizadas. Ponemos la inteligencia artificial al servicio de esta causa para procesar grandes volúmenes de datos e identificar coincidencias que faciliten reencuentros seguros y oportunos, asegurando que solo las organizaciones certificadas validen cada hallazgo dentro del sistema para una gestión privada de la información.

## **Nuestro norte**

Tener completamente ajustados los protocolos de ciberseguridad, protección de datos sensibles, bienestar animal y no revictimización. La seguridad de las personas y los animales siempre estará por encima de la velocidad de lanzamiento.

[Reporte cierre de hackathon Build 4 Venezuela | 28-junio-2026
](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ROkUtXL9PnpGrWa12x4zw6e7_sWmyfjEA_1AJ4x44QE/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.3o36mzss0moq)

## **Reencuentros team**

**Coordinación general:**

Alejandra Castaño Alzate (contacto directo para ingresar al equipo) | [Linkedin](www.linkedin.com/in/alejandra-castano-alzate) 


**Equipo de Operaciones**

Karla Reyes | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karlareyes/)


**Equipo de Desarrollo**

Iván Castillo | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/iv%C3%A1n-castillo-iligaray-03b25b243/)

Jefferson Cabrera | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jeffersoncabrera/)

Valeria Villacorta | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/valeria-villacorta-landeo-666313220/)

Emanuel López | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/emanuel-lopez-f/)


**Equipo de UX | UI | Contenido | Marketing**

Miguel Ángel Ruiz | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/everydecision/)

**Equipo de Políticas, Seguridad y Relaciones**

Mary Hengy Torres | [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/maryhengytorres/)


**Equipo de Integraciones**

## ¡Gracias!

Esta es la estructura que lidera la fase actual. Sin embargo, queremos expresar nuestra más sincera gratitud a los múltiples profesionales que participaron previamente. Sus valiosos aportes y dedicación han sido fundamentales para alcanzar los objetivos trazados.

analysis.summary: A genuinely substantial, actively developed platform (287 commits, deployed to Vercel+Render) that centralizes missing-persons reports for Venezuela earthquake victims: a React 19/TS frontend and an Express 5/TS/Mongoose backend with JWT+Google OAuth auth, BullMQ/Redis job queues, MinIO/S3 storage, pluggable AI providers (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini) for text structuring, and cron jobs ingesting real external data sources (USGS, GDACS, FIRMS, INAMEH, Cruz Roja, Proteccion Civil, Venezuela Te Busca, VenezuelaReporta) with deduplication via SHA-256 idHash and upsert-based reconciliation.
analysis.project_type: full-stack web app (missing-persons/disaster platform) with separate front/ (React+Vite) and back/ (Express+Mongoose) services

infra_deploy: GitHub Actions CI (build only, no test/lint gate)
Deployed: frontend on Vercel, backend on Render

architecture.pattern: layered service architecture (routes -> validators -> services -> models) with separate BullMQ workers for async AI processing and disaster-data sync jobs; monorepo with front/ and back/ as independent deployable services
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Mongoose ODM against MongoDB, with defined schemas, compound/geospatial indexes, and Zod-based request validation layered on top of Mixed-type fields
architecture.api_design: REST API under /api/* (persons, disasters, media, auth, admin, partners, localizados, contacts, cne, webhooks) with role- and API-key-based route protection
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation between routes, validators (Zod), services, models, adapters (external data source normalization), jobs (cron ingestion), queues/workers (BullMQ async processing), middlewares (auth, CSRF, audit)
architecture.modularity: Good modularity: dedicated adapter pattern for external sources, an AI provider abstraction (ai.interface/ai.factory) supporting 3 vendors, and per-source job files for each disaster/person data feed
notable_patterns: idempotent fetch->transform->idHash->upsert pattern for deduplicating person records across sources
AI provider factory/strategy pattern (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini interchangeable)
double-submit CSRF token pattern with httpOnly JWT cookie
tokenVersion-based JWT revocation on logout
audit logging middleware for security-sensitive actions
separate BullMQ worker process/container to isolate CPU-heavy AI work from the API

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, conventional layout separating front/back, with backend further split into routes/services/models/validators/adapters/jobs/queues/workers/middlewares/utils — well organized for a project of this size (~13k LOC).
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive kebab-case/PascalCase naming across files (e.g. unified-person.model.ts, ia-processor.worker.ts, reconciliation.service.ts) that reflects real domain concepts.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Extensive Spanish-language docs: Readme.md, DEVELOPERS.md, TDD.md, Integraciones.md, plus a Doc/index.html architecture doc — unusually thorough for a hackathon entry, with inline code comments explaining security tradeoffs (e.g. CSRF cookie rationale).
code_organization.reasoning: Directory layout, naming, and documentation all reflect deliberate engineering practice rather than generated scaffolding; minor deduction because some scripts (scraper-venezuelareporta.js) mix JS into an otherwise all-TS codebase.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Uses .env files (gitignored) with a documented .env.example listing only variable names, fail-fast startup checks for JWT_SECRET/WEBHOOK_API_KEY, and explicit warnings never to commit secrets/PII to the public repo.
production_readiness.security_notes: Strong for a hackathon project: Helmet with custom CSP, HSTS, CORS allowlist, hpp, express-rate-limit (global + strict auth-specific), CSRF double-submit protection, JWT with tokenVersion revocation, password hashing util, role-based admin middleware with JWT+legacy API-key fallback, and an audit-log middleware recording security events.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Multi-stage Docker build, separate worker container for AI processing decoupled from the API via BullMQ/Redis, MongoDB geospatial indexes for map queries, and docker-compose healthchecks/dependency ordering; horizontal worker scaling is explicitly documented.
production_readiness.reasoning: Recent commits (same day as review) show active production deployment fixes (Vercel proxy rewrites, trust proxy for secure cookies behind Render's load balancer, sameSite=none for cross-origin cookies), indicating the app is live and being hardened in real time, though CI only builds (no automated test/lint gate) and the live homepage currently returned HTTP 404 at check time.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 287 commits from 4 contributors, 20 backend test files (unit tests with mocked Mongoose models covering services/routes/middlewares/queues/workers), frontend Vitest+RTL tests, real third-party integrations (USGS, GDACS, FIRMS, Google OAuth, Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini, Pinecone), and CI pipeline building both front and back — this is far beyond boilerplate, though CI does not run the test suites.

viability.reasoning: Directly and concretely addresses the hackathon's core need (family reunification and disaster alerting for Venezuela) with working deduplication logic, geospatial search, real disaster-data feeds, and a live deployment; main risks to production use are unverified data-source reliability/legal access (scraping third-party sites), the live URL returning 404 at check time, and reliance on paid third-party AI/vector APIs for core matching functionality.

key_features: Centralized, deduplicated missing-persons registry (UnifiedPerson) with SHA-256 idHash upsert logic
Multi-source ingestion jobs for earthquakes/disasters (USGS, GDACS, FIRMS, INAMEH) and missing persons (Venezuela Te Busca, Cruz Roja, Proteccion Civil, VenezuelaReporta, web-form/WhatsApp adapters)
AI-assisted structuring of free-text reports via a pluggable Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini provider factory, processed asynchronously via BullMQ workers
Geospatial map search using MongoDB 2dsphere indexes and Leaflet clustering on the frontend
Google OAuth + email/password auth with JWT (httpOnly cookie), CSRF protection, role-based access, and admin/partner/webhook API-key layers
Human-in-the-loop match/reconciliation service (matcher.service, reconciliation.service) rather than auto-notification
Admin dashboard, verified-organization directory, and a documented humanitarian/seismic-safety manual in the frontend
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone candidate rather than a merge target — it already implements a full missing-persons pipeline (ingestion, dedup, AI structuring, geospatial search, auth) that other people-finder or seismic-data hackathon entries could plausibly consume via its adapter/webhook pattern instead of duplicating effort.
red_flags: Live homepage (reencuentro-terremoto-venezuela.vercel.app) returned HTTP 404 at time of review, suggesting deployment/config issues despite same-day fix commits
CI pipeline only builds front/back — it does not run the Jest/Vitest test suites, so regressions in the 20+ backend test files are not enforced
Relies on scraping third-party government/NGO sites (scripts/scraper-venezuelareporta.js) whose legality/stability is unverified
requireAdminApiKey retains a legacy static API-key admin bypass alongside JWT auth, widening the attack surface if that key leaks
Core deduplication/matching depends on paid external AI and vector-DB APIs (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini, Pinecone), creating cost and availability risk for a humanitarian-critical feature

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as a serious, security-hardened humanitarian-tech infrastructure story (287 commits, real USGS/GDACS/Cruz Roja data ingestion, AI-assisted matching) rather than a consumer-facing demo — appeal to technical/donor audiences and potential partner NGOs once the live site is fixed, since there is nothing visually demoable to the public right now.
gaps: The public-facing frontend (reencuentro-terremoto-venezuela.vercel.app) returns HTTP 404 right now, so there is no live product a victim or donor can actually open today
The 'live_url' field in the project record actually points to a private/editable Google Doc (a TDD/testing document titled 'TDD_Reencuentro VE'), not a live demo of the product
No demo video exists
No screenshots or visual assets captured/available for social content
No evidence of real users or field usage yet (still in active development, created only days ago)

evaluation.live_demo_notes: The 'live_url' supplied for this project is not the actual product but a private Google Doc ('TDD_Reencuentro VE', a testing/spec document) with unsaved-changes state — it is not a demo of the platform. The actual deployed frontend referenced in the technical analysis (reencuentro-terremoto-venezuela.vercel.app) returns HTTP 404 Not Found when fetched now, confirming the red flag already noted in the insight file (deployment/config issues despite same-day fix commits). No working, publicly reachable version of the product could be verified.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Reencuentros VE: una plataforma con IA que cruza reportes de organizaciones certificadas, Cruz Roja y datos sísmicos en tiempo real (USGS, GDACS) para reencontrar de forma segura a personas y animales desaparecidos tras el terremoto en Venezuela.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family/animal reunification after a mass-casualty earthquake is one of the most urgent, life-and-safety-critical needs for disaster victims (locating missing loved ones, avoiding scams/misinformation, coordinating with verified relief organizations). The project's architecture — deduplicated missing-persons registry, multi-source ingestion from real disaster/humanitarian feeds (USGS, GDACS, FIRMS, INAMEH, Cruz Roja, Protección Civil, Venezuela Te Busca), AI-assisted structuring of free-text field reports, geospatial search, and a human-in-the-loop verification/reconciliation step (rather than blind auto-matching) — shows the team engineered directly against this real problem and its risks (misidentification, revictimization, data privacy), not a generic hackathon idea.

name: Reestructuramos - Inspeccion Estructural Post Sismo
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**Reestructuramos** es una PWA **offline-first** para la **evaluación rápida de seguridad estructural de edificios después de un terremoto**, construida para los sismos **M7.2 + M7.5 del 24 de junio de 2026** en Venezuela.

Cada edificio se clasifica con el sistema universal de tres carteles — **VERDE** (habitable), **AMARILLO** (uso restringido) y **ROJO** (inseguro) — siguiendo la metodología **ATC-20** adaptada a la norma venezolana **COVENIN 1756**.

Lo que la hace distinta:

- **100% sin conexión.** Tras un sismo la red celular colapsa: ese es el caso de uso central, no el borde. Todo se guarda primero en el teléfono (IndexedDB) y se sincroniza solo cuando vuelve la señal. Incluye caché de mapas offline.
- **Evidencia ligada al hallazgo.** Cada foto se ata al daño concreto (esta columna, esta grieta) con GPS + fecha/hora embebidos. Nada de "álbum suelto".
- **Nunca sobrescribe el historial.** Una re-inspección tras una réplica es un registro NUEVO; el QR del cartel muestra todo el historial del edificio en el tiempo.
- **Sugiere, no decide.** La app propone el cartel según los daños observados, pero el inspector confirma o anula con su criterio (ambos quedan registrados).
- **Mapa de daños en vivo** de la ciudad, con pines a color por cartel.
- **PDF de inspección** + **cartel imprimible con QR**, generados en el dispositivo (funcionan offline).
- **Guía de campo offline** (patrones de daño, árbol de decisión, glosario) + mini-inducción con quiz para habilitar inspectores.

## ¿Para quién es?

Inspectores en campo: **voluntarios, ingenieros y arquitectos**. Acceso público y anónimo, sin barreras — cualquiera entra desde su teléfono y empieza de inmediato. También sirve como herramienta de formación.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Le da a Protección Civil, FUNVISIS, el colegio de ingenieros y a los voluntarios un **instrumento común** para triar miles de edificios rápido, con datos confiables (GPS + timestamp, trazabilidad antifraude) y un **mapa de daños que coordina la respuesta** — incluso con la red caída. Diseñado "listo para aval", alineado a COVENIN 1756.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Abre **https://app.reestructuramos.com** en el teléfono (instálala: "Agregar a pantalla de inicio").
2. Haz la **inducción de 2 minutos** + el mini-quiz.
3. Toca **"Nueva inspección"**: fija la ubicación (GPS o toca el mapa), marca los peligros con el selector de severidad, mira el **cartel sugerido**, confírmalo y **finaliza**.
4. Genera el **PDF** y el **cartel imprimible con QR**.
5. **Pon el teléfono en modo avión y recarga la app** — sigue funcionando. Ese es el corazón del proyecto.

## Tecnología

Next.js 16 + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind v4 · IndexedDB (Dexie) con cola de sincronización propia y reintentos · Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Storage + Auth anónima + RLS) · Leaflet + OpenStreetMap con caché offline · service worker propio · PDF y QR en el cliente. Diseño "instrumento de ingeniería": tipografía IBM Plex, alto contraste para uso al sol.

architecture.pattern: Next.js 16 App Router monolith (client-heavy PWA) with a thin server API route, backed by Supabase (Postgres+PostGIS) as BaaS and Dexie/IndexedDB as the true local-first data layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No ORM. Local: Dexie (IndexedDB) as source of truth in lib/db.ts. Remote: @supabase/supabase-js query builder (upsert/select) against Postgres tables defined via raw SQL migrations in supabase/migrations, with RLS policies and a SECURITY DEFINER helper (is_coordinator/owns_inspection).
architecture.api_design: Minimal: a single Next.js Route Handler (app/api/analizar-grieta/route.ts) proxies to Gemini Vision server-side to keep the API key off the client; all other reads/writes go directly from the browser to Supabase via RLS-scoped queries, not through a custom REST/GraphQL API.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation: lib/db.ts (local persistence), lib/sync.ts (sync/queue engine), lib/supabase.ts (auth/client), lib/placard.ts (pure domain logic for ATC-20 triage), components/wizard/* (UI flow) — logic is not smeared into components.
architecture.modularity: Good modularity via lib/* single-purpose modules and components grouped by domain (wizard, map, guide, onboarding, result, building); a wizard step model plus a Zustand store (lib/store.ts) for sync/UI state.
notable_patterns: Local-first architecture: all writes go to IndexedDB first, background sync queue with exponential backoff, no dependency on Background Sync API (explicitly for iOS Safari compatibility)
Client-generated UUIDs (clientUuid) used as idempotency keys for upsert-based sync to Supabase

Postgres RLS with SECURITY DEFINER helper functions and a public 'safe columns' view (inspections_public) added specifically to fix a privacy leak (documented in migration 0002 as 'issue #1, Build4Venezuela')
Server-side AI proxy pattern to avoid leaking Gemini API key to the client, with graceful 503 when unconfigured
Custom offline service worker (public/sw.js) with NetworkFirst navigation / CacheFirst assets, plus custom offline map tile caching in IndexedDB

code_organization.directory_structure: Conventional and clean Next.js App Router layout (app/, components/ grouped by feature, lib/ for domain+infra, supabase/migrations/ for schema) with no stray clutter.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish/English mixed naming that matches domain vocabulary (placardSuggested, syncStatus, ownsInspection) and is easy to follow.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average: README explains architecture and setup, CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md give contributor conventions, inline comments explain non-obvious decisions (e.g. why patchInspection uses partial updates, why RLS was tightened), migration file has a written rationale for a security fix.
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized single-domain app with intentional architectural comments and rationale, though there's no docs/ADRs beyond a pitch doc and CLAUDE.md, and some files (sync.ts) are dense multi-responsibility modules.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Server-only GEMINI_API_KEY read from process.env and never exposed to client; Supabase anon key is intentionally public (NEXT_PUBLIC_*) which is standard for Supabase RLS-protected apps. No .env.example file is committed despite README referencing one.
production_readiness.security_notes: Real Postgres RLS with anonymous auth, a SECURITY DEFINER coordinator/ownership model, and a documented follow-up commit that fixed an actual data-exposure bug (public read of PII columns) — shows security awareness in practice, not just boilerplate. Storage bucket policies scope upload/update/delete to authenticated owner or coordinator. No rate limiting on the AI route (analizar-grieta) which could allow API-key cost abuse.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Client directly hits Supabase with RLS, which scales reasonably for a hackathon/relief use case; offline-first sync with backoff prevents server hammering. PostGIS/GIST indexes exist for geo queries. No caching/CDN strategy beyond Vercel/PWA SW; single Node route (analizar-grieta) has maxDuration=30 but no explicit concurrency/cost guard.
production_readiness.reasoning: Deployment target (Vercel) and env var handling are addressed, auth (anonymous + optional email OTP) and RLS are implemented correctly with evidence of a real security fix, but there is no logging/observability, no automated tests, no CI, and no rate-limiting on the paid AI endpoint — leaving it a strong MVP rather than fully production-hardened.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, substantive implementation — not boilerplate. Domain logic (ATC-20/COVENIN placard triage in lib/placard.ts), a genuine offline-first sync engine, PostGIS schema with RLS, and a documented security patch all indicate real engineering effort, not scaffolding.
maturity.reasoning: Built in ~2-3 days by a single contributor (10 commits, repo created 2026-06-29) yet unusually complete for that timeframe: working wizard flow, offline storage, AI-assisted damage classification, geospatial map, and a real RLS security fix — but it has zero automated tests and no CI pipeline, which caps maturity despite feature depth.

viability.reasoning: Directly targets the stated disaster scenario (post-earthquake structural triage for Venezuela) with a coherent, testable domain model (ATC-20/COVENIN placard suggestion), works fully offline with sync-on-reconnect (critical for disaster conditions with poor connectivity), and has real deployment (app.reestructuramos.com) and a working Supabase backend with sane RLS. Missing tests/CI and no rate limiting on the paid AI feature are the main gaps before hardening for large-scale field use.

key_features: Offline-first PWA (IndexedDB/Dexie) with automatic background sync and exponential backoff, usable with zero connectivity
ATC-20/COVENIN-1756 adapted placard triage engine (verde/amarillo/rojo) implemented as pure, reasoned domain logic
Structural inspection wizard: building info, hazards, damage findings, photo capture with annotation, crack width measurement, phone-sensor inclinometer/compass
AI-assisted crack/damage photo classification via a server-side Gemini Vision proxy that keeps the API key private
Collaborative damage map (Leaflet + PostGIS) with offline tile caching and a privacy-hardened public view (RLS) for crowd-sourced building status
Printable/QR-stamped placard generation (jsPDF) and Web Share for viral distribution/reporting
Anonymous-first auth with optional email OTP login for coordinators, tied to Postgres RLS ownership rules
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone product with a well-defined, narrow domain (structural post-quake triage) — could serve as the structural-inspection/data-collection module feeding shared alerts/mapping or shelter-coordination platforms from other hackathon entries via its Supabase/PostGIS schema (buildings, inspections, placards) as a common data source, rather than needing to merge its own architecture with others.
red_flags: No automated tests despite non-trivial triage logic that determines building safety placards
No CI pipeline configured
No rate limiting on the Gemini AI proxy endpoint (cost/abuse risk)
Single contributor, ~2-3 day old repo — bus factor risk and limited real-world field validation
README references a .env.example file that is not actually committed to the repo

ai_ml: Google Gemini 2.5 Flash Vision (server-side proxy for damage/crack classification)

analysis.summary: reestructuramos-pwa is an offline-first Next.js/React PWA for rapid post-earthquake structural safety triage of buildings in Venezuela, implementing an ATC-20/COVENIN-1756-adapted green/yellow/red placard system. It combines a genuine local-first architecture (Dexie/IndexedDB as source of truth with a custom exponential-backoff sync queue) with a Supabase/PostGIS backend secured by real, iteratively-hardened Row Level Security, a server-side Gemini Vision proxy for AI-assisted damage photo classification, and a collaborative offline-capable damage map.
