Despite being built by a single contributor in about three days with no tests or CI, the code shows unusually mature engineering: documented domain logic, a real security-fix commit, and end-to-end offline resilience suited to disaster conditions.
analysis.project_type: Progressive Web App (disaster-response structural inspection tool)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the offline-first proof point: 'Funciona sin señal' — show phone in airplane mode still classifying a building VERDE/AMARILLO/ROJO and generating a printable QR placard, tied directly to the real June 24 2026 M7.2/M7.5 Venezuela earthquakes.
gaps: No evidence yet of real-world adoption by Protección Civil/FUNVISIS or actual field inspectors
Screenshots/GIFs of the wizard flow, damage map, and printed placard would strengthen a social post beyond the shorts video
No visible testimonials or usage numbers (0 stars, 1 contributor, brand new repo)

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch confirms app.reestructuramos.com loads a real working PWA: 'Mapa de daños' page with map component, navigation (Map/New inspection/My inspections/Guide), the VERDE/AMARILLO/ROJO (Habitable/Uso restringido/Inseguro) classification system visible, a 'Nueva inspección' button, and an offline-area-preparation feature — not a static landing page.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Reestructuramos: la app offline-first que triaje edificios post-sismo en segundos — sin señal, sin excusas, con el cartel verde/amarillo/rojo que Protección Civil necesita YA.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake structural triage is a textbook critical disaster-response need: after Venezuela's stated M7.2/M7.5 quakes, thousands of buildings must be rapidly assessed for habitability before people re-enter, and cellular networks typically collapse right when this decision-making is most urgent. This project directly implements the internationally recognized ATC-20 methodology (adapted to Venezuela's own COVENIN 1756 building code) with a genuine offline-first architecture (IndexedDB source of truth, background sync with backoff) specifically because 'after an earthquake the cellular network collapses: that is the core use case, not the edge case.' This is not a generic app retrofitted to the disaster theme — it is purpose-built domain logic (placard suggestion engine), evidence capture (photo+GPS+timestamp tied to specific damage), historical record-keeping (re-inspections after aftershocks never overwrite prior records), and coordination tooling (live citywide damage map) aimed squarely at the responders (volunteers, engineers, Protección Civil) who need a shared triage instrument during chaos.

name: refugio-mental
db_description:
## Refugio Mental

Plataforma web orientada a facilitar el acceso a apoyo psicológico para personas afectadas por la emergencia humanitaria ocasionada por el doble terremoto ocurrido en Venezuela el 24 de junio de 2026.

El proyecto conecta a psicólogos voluntarios con personas que necesitan acompañamiento emocional mediante un directorio público de profesionales. Los psicólogos pueden crear un perfil con información básica y compartir su enlace de Calendly para que los pacientes agenden sesiones de forma sencilla y segura.

Esta primera versión funciona completamente del lado del cliente (sin backend), demostrando un MVP rápido, accesible y fácilmente escalable hacia una plataforma comunitaria de apoyo psicológico.

architecture.pattern: Client-only Next.js App Router SPA (no backend/API routes)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None. lib/storage.ts wraps window.localStorage; obtenerPerfiles() merges hardcoded seed data (data/seed.ts) with whatever the current browser has stored under a fixed key. No server, no database, no persistence across devices/users.
architecture.api_design: None — zero fetch/axios/API route usage anywhere in the codebase; grep for fetch(/axios/api/ /process.env found nothing.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable folder split (app/ features/ components/ hooks/ lib/ types/) typical of a scaffolded Next.js project, but the abstraction is superficial since there is no real data layer behind it.
architecture.modularity: Feature-folder structure (directory, registration, home) with shared ui/ (shadcn/radix) components; clean at the component level.
notable_patterns: Comment in storage.ts literally states "En una futura versión, esta función llamaría a una API REST" — acknowledging the backend doesn't exist yet
Simulated network latency via setTimeout in usePerfiles to fake a loading skeleton
Directory listing merges static seed array with localStorage array client-side

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, idiomatic Next.js App Router layout (app/, features/, components/ui vs shared, hooks/, lib/, types/, data/) suggesting AI-assisted scaffolding (AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md present) but organized sensibly.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (Psicologo, obtenerPerfiles, guardarPerfil) matching the target audience; TypeScript interfaces well defined in types/index.ts.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is detailed with setup instructions, project structure diagram, and an important disclaimer about not replacing emergency services; inline comments in storage.ts explain intended future API replacement.
code_organization.reasoning: Code is legible and consistently organized for its size (~3.3k LOC), but the tidy structure wraps almost no real logic — it's essentially a styled directory/filter UI over a static array.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: N/A — no secrets, no env vars, no server calls of any kind exist in the codebase.
production_readiness.security_notes: No auth means anyone can 'register' as a psychologist with no verification; data is stored only in the submitting browser's localStorage so it isn't even visible to other visitors — the directory feature is effectively non-functional for its stated purpose (only the hardcoded seed profiles are visible to real users). No input sanitization beyond basic form validation; Calendly URLs and social links entered by users are rendered without any server-side vetting.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: There is no shared backend to scale — the 'product' as shipped cannot actually connect real psychologists to real patients across different browsers/devices, which is fatal for the stated mission of matching earthquake victims with volunteer therapists.
production_readiness.reasoning: Deployed to Vercel (refugio-mental.vercel.app) as a static/client site with zero backend, zero persistence beyond one browser's localStorage, no auth, no error monitoring, and no environment configuration — it is a UI demo, not a working service.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: boilerplate/prototype
maturity.reasoning: Single commit, 1 contributor, no tests, no CI workflow, no Dockerfile, presence of both AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md strongly suggests fully AI-generated scaffold produced in a short hackathon burst; core 'directory' feature is a UI illusion backed only by localStorage, not a working shared registry.

viability.reasoning: The idea (connect volunteer psychologists with earthquake victims) is directly relevant and the UI is polished, but as implemented there is no real backend, so registrations by real psychologists never become visible to real patients on other devices — the core value proposition does not function beyond a demo on a single browser. Needs a real database/API and moderation/auth layer before it could help anyone.

key_features: Directory (directorio) page listing psychologist profiles with search/filter by specialty, language, modality, country
Registration form (features/registration/ProfileForm.tsx, 628 lines) for psychologists to add themselves to the directory
Static seed dataset of 6 example psychologist profiles focused on trauma/crisis care related to the '24J' Venezuela earthquake
Guidance pages for patients and psychologists (Calendly integration guide, info pages)
Dark/light theme toggle and toast notifications (sonner) — purely UI polish
analysis.merge_potential: Could be salvaged as a frontend/UI reference (form, directory filter UX) for a proper mental-health directory project, but the entire data layer (storage.ts) must be replaced with a real backend (DB + API) and auth/moderation before it provides any real-world value; not mergeable as-is with any project needing actual shared data persistence.
red_flags: No backend/API/database whatsoever — all 'registered' profiles live only in the submitting user's browser localStorage and are invisible to everyone else, making the directory non-functional for its core purpose
No tests, no CI, no error handling/logging, no auth — zero production infrastructure
Single commit / single contributor, AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md present, indicating rapid AI-scaffolded output with no real backend work done
storage.ts comment explicitly admits the API layer doesn't exist yet ('En una futura versión, esta función llamaría a una API REST')
analysis.summary: Refugio Mental is a Next.js/TypeScript frontend prototype styled as a directory connecting volunteer psychologists with earthquake victims in Venezuela, but it has zero backend: all profile data is either a hardcoded seed array or saved to the submitting browser's localStorage, meaning registered psychologists never actually appear to other users. No API routes, no database, no auth, no tests, no CI — it is a polished UI shell/demo, not a working product.
analysis.project_type: web app (frontend-only prototype)

database: browser localStorage (client-only, not shared/persistent across users)
infra_deploy: Vercel (static/client deployment, no CI config found)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Not ready. If promoted at all, it should be framed as 'volunteer-built concept/prototype' with a clear ask for backend/database contributors, not as a working service.
gaps: No live public demo URL provided (only GitHub repo link)
No demo video
No working shared backend — registered psychologists are invisible to real patients on other devices, so there is nothing genuinely usable to show a victim or donor
No screenshots included in analysis
No real users/testimonials
Single commit, single contributor, no proof of stability or continued development

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public live demo URL was provided (live_url points back to the GitHub repo itself). Even if the Vercel deployment mentioned in the analysis (refugio-mental.vercel.app) were checked, the analysis confirms the directory feature is client-only: profiles submitted by a psychologist save only to that browser's localStorage and are never visible to other users, so the core matching feature does not actually work across devices. No demo video exists to substitute for firsthand verification.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Refugio Mental imagina un directorio de psicólogos voluntarios para sobrevivientes del terremoto — pero hoy es solo una maqueta: sin backend, los perfiles nunca llegan a las víctimas.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Mental health support for earthquake victims and matching volunteer psychologists with survivors is a genuinely important and severe need after a humanitarian crisis. However, the implementation completely fails to solve it: with zero backend and data stored only in the submitting user's own browser localStorage, a psychologist who registers is never actually discoverable by a real victim on a different device. The directory — the entire value proposition — is non-functional beyond the hardcoded seed data and single-browser demos. So while the problem is real and severe, this project does not solve it, even partially, for anyone beyond the person testing it locally.

name: responsegrid.app
db_description: ResponseGrid es una plataforma orientada a la gestión guiada de logística y eventos, con foco en estandarizar procedimientos, documentar actuaciones, coordinar tareas y centralizar información durante todo el ciclo de vida de una emergencia. Su propuesta principal es la información verificada y la trazabilidad  en tiempo real.

analysis.summary: ResponseGrid is a full-featured, seriously engineered NestJS (hexagonal/DDD) + Next.js 16 platform for coordinating material aid logistics during emergencies, purpose-built and seeded with a real 'Terremoto Venezuela 2026' emergency with 574 real collection points imported from acopiove.org. It has 18 bounded contexts, Drizzle ORM with 53 versioned SQL migrations, OAuth (Google/Facebook) + JWT + API-key auth with a granular permission/grant system, 216+ spec/integration test files, a CI pipeline with real Postgres/Redis service containers and a migration-immutability guard, and a documented production deploy (Dockerized API + Caddy HTTPS + Datadog APM on EC2, Next.js on Vercel).
analysis.project_type: Full-stack humanitarian aid-logistics coordination platform (monorepo: NestJS API + Next.js web + shared typed API client)

infra_deploy: GitHub Actions CI (format/lint/build/test with migration-immutability guard)

architecture.pattern: Hexagonal architecture / DDD with bounded contexts (domain/application/infrastructure layers), enforced via ESLint no-restricted-imports so domain/application cannot import NestJS or Drizzle
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM with a typed query builder (explicitly preferred over raw SQL to avoid string-typed timestamptz/numeric bugs), 53 hand-written idempotent SQL migrations tracked by filename
architecture.api_design: RESTful NestJS controllers per bounded context, documented via Swagger/OpenAPI at /docs, with a generated typed client package (@reliefhub/api-client) consumed by the Next.js frontend
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Strong: 18 bounded contexts (needs, offers, supplies, logistics, volunteers, identity, organizations, accreditation, templates, notifications, audit, metrics, geocoding, files, etc.) each with domain/application/infrastructure subfolders and per-context domain exception filters
architecture.modularity: High — clear module boundaries, shared kernel for cross-cutting value objects (EmergencyId, Location, DomainEvent), ports/adapters pattern for repositories and event bus with in-memory/fake implementations for tests
notable_patterns: Ports & adapters (repository interfaces + Drizzle/in-memory implementations)
Domain events via BullMQ event bus
Permission-based access control (Principal → Grant(role@scope) → Permission)
API-key service accounts (X-API-Key) alongside JWT auth
Domain exception filters mapping domain errors to HTTP responses per context
CI guard preventing modification of already-merged migrations

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean pnpm monorepo: apps/api (NestJS hexagonal), apps/web (Next.js Atomic Design), packages/api-client (generated typed client), deploy/ (prod compose + Caddy + systemd), docs/features (frozen legacy specs), consistent per-context domain/application/infrastructure layering
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish/English mixed naming aligned with domain language (needs, offers, supplies, resources, expediciones)
code_organization.documentation_quality: Extensive: ARCHITECTURE.md, AGENTS.md (detailed engineering workflow and gotchas), CONTRIBUTING.md, per-app README/AGENTS.md, docs/deploy guides, docs/features specs, Definition of Done doc
code_organization.reasoning: Rigorous, consistently applied hexagonal/DDD layering across 18 contexts with enforced architectural boundaries, extensive documentation, and a disciplined multi-agent contribution workflow (issue-claiming protocol, CI gate) that is unusually mature for a hackathon submission.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env files (gitignored) with .env.example templates; JWT_SECRET length/strength validated at boot in production (must be ≥32 chars); OAuth client secrets and AWS credentials resolved via env/SDK default chain, not hardcoded
production_readiness.security_notes: Helmet security headers, CORS restricted to configured FRONTEND_URL (no wildcard), rate limiting via @nestjs/throttler, permission/grant-based authorization with API-key scoped service accounts, location-sensitivity redaction for privacy on public endpoints, contact info redacted in public API responses
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Stateless API container (files in S3) behind Caddy, Redis-backed BullMQ for async domain events, Postgres with pg_stat_statements enabled for DBM, Datadog APM tracing end-to-end; deploy target is a single EC2 t3.small documented as fine for current scale but would need horizontal scaling/managed DB for larger load
production_readiness.reasoning: This goes well beyond typical hackathon output: structured JSON logging, Datadog APM/logs/DBM integration, healthchecks, idempotent migration runner, HTTPS via Caddy, and a CI pipeline that gates deploys — genuinely deployable and operated infrastructure, not a demo.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, actively developed production system — not boilerplate. 300 commits in ~4 days, 216+ spec/integration test files, real seeded emergency data (574 collection points from acopiove.org), documented multi-agent contribution workflow with issue claiming and auto-merge CI gates.
maturity.reasoning: CI runs format/lint/build/test against real Postgres+Redis service containers with a migration-immutability guard; extensive unit/integration test coverage across domain, application and infrastructure layers; deploy pipeline and monitoring already wired up.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed (responsegrid.app / api.responsegrid.app) with a live emergency scenario preloaded with real Venezuelan collection-point data, a disciplined engineering process, and infrastructure (Datadog, S3, Caddy/HTTPS, CI/CD) suited for real operational use during a disaster response.

key_features: Multi-emergency material aid coordination isolated by emergency_id/slug
Collection/logistic points with declared material inventory per place
Validated needs with 48h freshness/expiry model
Material offers matched to needs, with volunteer suggestion matching
Shared canonical supplies/categories catalogue reused across contexts
Transport capacity and shipment logistics tracking
Volunteer and personnel task management
Field incident/stock/status reports
Real-time Leaflet map with clustering
Granular role/grant/API-key authorization system with organizations and groups
Public read-only API with Swagger docs and typed client generation
analysis.merge_potential: Strong standalone platform; could absorb or integrate with simpler donation/volunteer-signup tools as a backend of record, but its scope (logistics coordination) is broad enough that it likely doesn't need merging — other projects focused on people-finder/medical/mental-health domains would complement rather than merge into it.
red_flags: Development is dominated by a single author (38 of ~50 commits) with a handful of minor contributors, despite AGENTS.md describing a 'multi-agent' workflow — real-world team bus factor is unclear

Extremely rapid commit velocity (300 commits in ~4 days) suggests heavy AI-assisted/agentic development; while code quality appears high, this warrants scrutiny of whether all logic has been battle-tested under real field conditions
Deploy target is a single EC2 t3.small instance — adequate for pilot but would need a scaling/HA plan before handling nationwide earthquake-response load

diffusion.recommended_angle: Spotlight ResponseGrid as the most production-grade platform in the hackathon: a live, deployed, open-source aid-logistics coordination system already seeded with real 'Terremoto Venezuela 2026' emergency data (574 real collection points from acopiove.org) — donors and NGOs can use it TODAY to see verified collection points, validated needs, and coordinate volunteers/transport in real time.
gaps: No demo video exists — a 60-90s walkthrough would massively boost social shareability
No screenshots captured yet for social cards
Real end-user/NGO testimonials or usage numbers not yet documented (bus-factor / single-author development also worth transparency)

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Confirmed live via curl: https://responsegrid.app returns HTTP 200, is a real Next.js app (not a static landing page) titled 'ResponseGrid — Coordina ayuda en emergencias | Puntos de acopio, necesidades y voluntariado' with meta description covering collection points, validated needs, donations, transport, and volunteering. The backend API at api.responsegrid.app is also live and returns proper JSON 404s for undefined routes (indicating a real running NestJS server), and /docs (Swagger) responds 200. WebFetch tool itself got a 403 (likely bot/user-agent blocking), but direct curl with a browser user-agent succeeded, confirming the site is genuinely deployed and reachable for real users.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: ResponseGrid: la plataforma open source ya en vivo que coordina ayuda para el terremoto en Venezuela con 574 puntos de acopio reales, necesidades verificadas y voluntariado en tiempo real.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake response is chronically hampered by fragmented, unverified information about where aid is needed, where collection points are, and how to coordinate volunteers/transport/donations — leading to duplicated effort, spoiled/misdirected donations, and untracked needs. ResponseGrid directly targets this coordination gap with a purpose-built, production-grade logistics platform (multi-emergency isolation, validated 48h-freshness needs, offer-to-need matching, transport/shipment tracking, volunteer task management, real-time map) and it is already seeded with real, live 'Terremoto Venezuela 2026' data (574 actual collection points imported from acopiove.org), meaning it is not a hypothetical exercise but an operational tool addressing the exact real-world problem victims and responders face today.

name: ResQLink
db_description:
ResQLink AI es una plataforma gratuita de respuesta humanitaria diseñada para situaciones de emergencia sísmica. Permite conectar en tiempo real a personas que necesitan ayuda con voluntarios, organizaciones y recursos disponibles.

La plataforma utiliza inteligencia artificial para priorizar incidentes, detectar reportes duplicados y recomendar recursos automáticamente. Todo funciona desde el navegador, sin necesidad de instalar nada.

### ¿Qué puedes hacer con ResQLink AI?

- Reportar emergencias con ubicación GPS automática
- Ofrecer recursos como alimentos, agua, medicinas o transporte
- Visualizar un mapa en tiempo real con todos los incidentes y recursos
- Coordinar voluntarios y asignar tareas desde el centro de operaciones
- Consultar estadísticas actualizadas en el dashboard

> **Consejo:** ResQLink AI funciona como PWA (Progressive Web App). Puedes instalarla en tu celular desde el navegador para usarla como una app nativa, incluso sin conexión a internet.

architecture.pattern: Layered/clean-architecture Next.js app (domain/infrastructure/presentation split) with App Router, Supabase BaaS backend, Netlify scheduled function
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (postgREST) with hand-written query modules per entity (incidents.ts, resources.ts, locations.ts, social-feed.ts); raw SQL migration files define schema with PostGIS geography columns and RLS policies
architecture.api_design: Next.js API routes under src/app/api for AI classification/recommendation/duplicate-detection and social feed sync; most CRUD goes directly through Supabase client from client/server components rather than dedicated REST endpoints
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonably clean: domain entities/enums, infrastructure (supabase clients, queries, AI, storage), presentation (components/hooks/providers), shared (config/types/utils) are separated into distinct directories
architecture.modularity: Good modularity for a hackathon project; queries are split by entity, AI logic isolated in infrastructure/ai/openai, UI built from shadcn primitives
notable_patterns: Server/browser/middleware Supabase client separation (@supabase/ssr)
Row Level Security policies defined in SQL migrations for public read / auth-scoped write
Netlify scheduled function pulls USGS earthquake feed and Google News RSS to auto-populate incidents/social feed
AI features (priority classification, duplicate detection, resource recommendation, summarization) implemented as discrete OpenAI wrapper functions

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear domain/infrastructure/presentation/shared split typical of clean architecture, plus Next.js app router route groups (app)/(auth)
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming (getIncidents, classifyPriority, updateSession, etc.)
code_organization.documentation_quality: Has AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md (AI-assistant instructions), MANUAL.md and generated PDF manual/QA report/deck, but the actual README.md is unedited create-next-app boilerplate with no setup/env instructions for this project
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized layered structure for a ~7.5k LOC hackathon app, but public-facing README is generic boilerplate rather than real project documentation, which hurts a first-time deployer's onboarding.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No committed .env.example or secrets; .gitignore excludes .env*; env vars centralized in src/shared/config/env.ts using non-null assertions (will throw at runtime if missing rather than failing fast at build), OPENAI_API_KEY only used server-side
production_readiness.security_notes: Supabase auth + RLS policies exist (public read on incidents/resources/hospitals/shelters, auth.uid()-scoped writes for users/notifications), but middleware.ts only protects /dashboard, /operations, /settings routes — pages like /map, /incidents, /notifications, /resources under the same (app) route group are NOT gated by the auth check, and several RLS insert policies allow anonymous writes ('Anyone can report', service-level unrestricted update/delete on social_feed_items), which is a real exposure for a public safety app
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase/Postgres+PostGIS with geography columns is a reasonable scalable base; no caching, rate limiting, or queueing evident; Netlify scheduled function for external sync (USGS/RSS) is a lightweight but workable ingestion pattern
production_readiness.reasoning: Real auth, DB schema and AI integration exist and basic error handling wraps API routes, but there is no test suite, no CI, no structured logging/observability, an incomplete route-protection matrix, and the README is unmodified boilerplate — this is short of what should be trusted for a live disaster-response deployment without further hardening.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 15 commits over ~2 days show genuine iterative bug-fixing ('resolve all QA audit bugs', 'fix hydration errors', RLS policy fixes), a full Supabase schema with PostGIS/RLS, working OpenAI-backed triage/dedup/recommendation logic, and a live USGS+RSS data sync function — clearly a working MVP, not empty scaffolding — but it has zero automated tests and no CI pipeline.

viability.reasoning: A single contributor built a functioning full-stack MVP (auth, real-time-capable Supabase backend, geospatial incident/resource tracking, AI triage, live earthquake feed) in days, which is impressive for a hackathon, but the lack of tests/CI, incomplete route protection, and generic README indicate it needs meaningful hardening before being trusted to coordinate real earthquake response for victims.

key_features: Incident reporting with geolocation, photo upload, and category/priority classification
AI-powered triage: OpenAI-based priority classification, duplicate incident detection, resource recommendation, and Spanish-language incident summarization
Interactive Leaflet map with filters for incidents/resources/shelters/hospitals
Dashboard with live stats (active incidents, affected people, resources, volunteers, shelters, hospitals)
Automated data ingestion: Netlify scheduled function pulls USGS earthquake feed (magnitude>=4, Venezuela bounding box) and Google News RSS into the incidents/social-feed tables hourly
Supabase auth (email/password) with row-level security policies and role model (admin/organization/volunteer/citizen)
Resource and volunteer coordination entities (assignments table linking incidents, volunteers, organizations, resources)
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate to merge with/absorb a dedicated shelter-mapping or people-finder tool: it already has a solid incident/resource/volunteer schema and geospatial map, so a project focused purely on family reunification or medical triage detail could plug into this platform's Supabase schema and map UI rather than rebuilding infrastructure.
red_flags: No automated tests (unit/integration/e2e) anywhere in the repo
No CI pipeline (no .github/workflows)
README.md is unedited create-next-app boilerplate, not real project documentation
Auth middleware only protects /dashboard, /operations, /settings — other authenticated-looking routes (/map, /incidents, /notifications, /resources) under the same route group appear unprotected

RLS policies allow fully anonymous inserts on incidents/resources ('Anyone can report'/'Anyone can offer resources') with no validation/moderation layer visible, creating spam/abuse risk for a life-safety system
No structured logging/observability/error-tracking (only console.log/console.error)
Single contributor, 0 stars/forks, 15 commits over 2 days — very early-stage, unvetted by others
analysis.summary: ResQLink AI is a Next.js 16 + Supabase humanitarian-response platform for Venezuela earthquake relief, providing incident reporting, an AI-powered triage layer (OpenAI classification/dedup/recommendation), a Leaflet map of incidents/resources/shelters/hospitals, and an automated USGS earthquake + Google News RSS ingestion pipeline via a Netlify scheduled function. The codebase shows a genuine, actively-iterated MVP with a real PostGIS-backed Postgres schema, row-level security, and clean layered architecture, but it ships with zero tests, no CI, an unedited boilerplate README, and gaps in route-level auth protection and anonymous-write abuse controls that would need fixing before trusting it in a live emergency-response deployment.
analysis.project_type: web-app (full-stack humanitarian incident-response platform)

ai_ml: OpenAI API (gpt-4o-mini) for triage classification, duplicate detection, summarization, resource recommendation

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the AI-powered triage + live USGS earthquake feed angle: 'An MVP built in 2 days that automatically ingests real earthquake data and uses AI to triage who needs help first' — position as a promising, technically serious hackathon MVP rather than a finished product ready for victims to rely on today.
gaps: No screenshots of populated map/dashboard with real data provided or verifiable via static fetch
No evidence of real users/testers
Public README is unedited boilerplate, hurting credibility for open-source diffusion
Anonymous-write RLS policies pose an abuse risk that should be mentioned/fixed before heavy public promotion

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL loads and is not broken/404 — it has real routing (/, /map, /dashboard) with a Spanish landing page ('Conectamos necesidades con recursos en tiempo real'), a proper login-gated dashboard, and a map route with its own nav shell. WebFetch only retrieves static/initial HTML (no JS execution), so it could not confirm the Leaflet map actually renders populated incident markers or that the dashboard shows live stats once authenticated — that part remains unverified from outside. Combined with the code analysis (real Supabase/PostGIS schema, working OpenAI triage functions, and a Netlify scheduled function that pulls the real USGS earthquake feed + Google News RSS hourly), this reads as a genuine working MVP rather than a pure mockup, but the public-facing entry page itself shows no live data to an anonymous visitor.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: ResQLink AI: la plataforma que cruza datos sísmicos en vivo (USGS) con IA para priorizar auxilios y conectar víctimas con voluntarios y recursos en tiempo real — un MVP funcional, no una maqueta.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Coordinating incident reports, volunteers, and resources (food, water, medicine, transport, shelters, hospitals) during and after earthquakes is a genuine, life-critical coordination failure point in disasters — informal WhatsApp/social-media coordination is slow, duplicated, and hard to prioritize. ResQLink directly targets this with structured incident reporting, geolocation, an AI triage/dedup layer, and automated ingestion of real USGS earthquake data plus a resource/volunteer assignment schema, showing the team understood the actual operational bottlenecks of emergency response rather than building a generic app.

name: SARA Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible para Emergencias
db_description:
# ♿ SARA
## Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible para Emergencias

> **Una plataforma inclusiva que conecta a personas con discapacidad, voluntarios y organizaciones para coordinar ayuda en tiempo real durante emergencias y desastres naturales.**

---

## 🌎 Descripción

**SARA** nace como una solución desarrollada durante un hackatón para responder a las necesidades de las personas con discapacidad afectadas por un desastre natural.

La plataforma permite solicitar ayuda específica de forma rápida y geolocalizada, conectando a las personas afectadas con voluntarios, organizaciones humanitarias, profesionales de la salud y donantes cercanos.

Nuestro objetivo es garantizar que **nadie quede sin asistencia debido a una barrera de accesibilidad.**

---

# 🚨 Problemática

Después de un desastre natural, las personas con discapacidad enfrentan dificultades adicionales para acceder a:

- ♿ Equipamiento de apoyo
- 💊 Medicamentos
- 🚗 Transporte accesible
- 🏠 Refugios adaptados
- 🧏 Información accesible
- 🧠 Apoyo psicosocial

Además, la falta de coordinación entre ciudadanos, voluntarios y organizaciones retrasa la entrega de ayuda.

---

# 💡 Nuestra solución

SARA centraliza las solicitudes de asistencia en una plataforma accesible que permite coordinar la ayuda en tiempo real.

Cada solicitud se geolocaliza para facilitar la respuesta de voluntarios y organizaciones cercanas.

---

# 📱 Casos de uso

### 🧑‍🦽 Movilidad reducida

José perdió su silla de ruedas durante el terremoto y solicita una silla temporal o unas muletas.

### 🦻 Discapacidad auditiva

María necesita reemplazar su audífono y la plataforma la conecta con una organización especializada.

### 🧏 Comunicación accesible

Francisca requiere un intérprete de lengua de señas o información en un formato accesible.

### 👩‍🦯 Acompañamiento

Ana solicita apoyo para trasladarse de forma segura hasta un refugio accesible.

### 🧠 Apoyo psicosocial

Carlos necesita recursos de regulación emocional y orientación durante la emergencia.

---

# ⚙️ ¿Cómo funciona?

1. La persona crea una solicitud de ayuda.
2. Selecciona el tipo de necesidad.
3. Comparte su ubicación.
4. La solicitud aparece en el mapa.
5. Voluntarios y organizaciones reciben una notificación.
6. Se asigna la ayuda.
7. Se realiza seguimiento hasta cerrar el caso.

---

# 🆘 Tipos de ayuda

- ♿ Equipamiento de apoyo
- 💊 Medicamentos
- 🚗 Transporte accesible
- 🤝 Acompañamiento
- 🧏 Intérpretes
- 📖 Información accesible
- 🧠 Apoyo para personas neurodivergentes
- ❤️ Apoyo psicosocial

---

# ✨ Funcionalidades

- 📍 Solicitudes geolocalizadas
- 🗺️ Mapa de refugios accesibles
- 🤝 Red de voluntarios
- 🏥 Directorio de organizaciones
- 💬 Chat y seguimiento de solicitudes
- 🎁 Donación y préstamo de equipamiento
- 🚨 Priorización de casos críticos
- 📊 Estadísticas para la coordinación humanitaria

---

# 🚀 MVP

## Fase 1

- Registro de usuarios
- Perfil de necesidades
- Solicitud de ayuda
- Geolocalización
- Mapa de solicitudes
- Panel de voluntarios

## Fase 2

- Chat
- Notificaciones
- Directorio de organizaciones
- Gestión de donaciones y préstamos

## Fase 3

- Priorización mediante IA
- Dashboard para organismos humanitarios
- Datos abiertos para coordinación en tiempo real

---

# 🎯 Objetivo

Reducir el tiempo entre una solicitud de ayuda y la llegada del recurso adecuado, garantizando una respuesta inclusiva, accesible y coordinada durante las primeras horas de una emergencia.

---

# ❤️ Nuestra misión

Que cualquier persona con discapacidad pueda solicitar ayuda en minutos y encontrar una red de apoyo cercana cuando más lo necesita.

## Porque la accesibilidad también salva vidas.

analysis.summary: SARA is a Next.js + Express/Postgres emergency-response MVP for disaster relief in Venezuela, with one real, tested, end-to-end feature (SOS/help-request creation, geolocation search, volunteer acceptance, resolution) backed by a real Postgres schema with constraints, plus several other screens (shelter map, chat, directory, resources) that are entirely static/mock UI with no backend wiring.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web app (Next.js frontend + Express REST API backend), mobile-responsive PWA-style UI, no native mobile app despite README claims

database: PostgreSQL (raw SQL via node-postgres 'pg', no ORM)
infra_deploy: Vercel (vercel.json for backend as serverless function)
no Docker, no CI config found

architecture.pattern: Two separate apps: Next.js frontend (client components, mock data) and a standalone Express REST API for a single 'help_requests' resource
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw parameterized SQL via the 'pg' Pool client (backend/src/db.js), no ORM/query builder
architecture.api_design: Small REST API: GET/POST /api/help-requests, POST /:id/accept, POST /:id/resolve, plus /health; validation and normalization are cleanly separated into their own modules (validation/helpRequests.js, helpRequests/create.js, search.js, acceptance.js)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Backend is well-factored (routes -> validation -> db) for the one implemented feature; frontend has an api/ layer (helpRequests.ts) but most other pages read directly from a mocks/ folder with no data layer abstraction
architecture.modularity: Backend is modular per-concern (create/query/search/acceptance/shared); frontend is modular by route (app/) but several routes are disconnected islands of static content
notable_patterns: DB-level CHECK constraints enforce business invariants (status/assignment/resolution consistency) in schema.sql, a nice belt-and-suspenders design

No-auth-by-design for help request creation (explicit ai-specs doc: 'backend-create-help-request-without-auth'), reasonable for anonymous emergency reporting but leaves accept/resolve endpoints also unauthenticated
Only one domain object (help_requests) has a real backend; shelters/refugios, chat, directory, resources are hardcoded frontend mock arrays with zero API calls

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear frontend/backend split, App Router conventions followed, backend organized by feature folder (helpRequests/) with routes/validation separated
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming in both English (backend) and mixed Spanish/English (frontend, matching Venezuelan-facing UI copy)
code_organization.documentation_quality: Has root README, backend README, ai-specs/ change docs describing intended backend features; frontend has AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md agent-instruction files rather than developer docs
code_organization.reasoning: Backend portion is genuinely well organized with tests and docs; overall score pulled down by frontend's disconnected mock-only pages and total absence of CI/lint enforcement in the pipeline.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: backend/.env.example documents DATABASE_URL/PORT; dotenv used correctly; no secrets committed
production_readiness.security_notes: No authentication/authorization anywhere in the app — anyone can create, accept, or resolve help requests via the API with no rate limiting, CAPTCHA, or abuse protection; CORS is wide open (cors() with no origin restriction); only console.error for logging (no structured logging/monitoring)
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single Postgres table with indexes on status/created_at is fine for MVP scale; geolocation search likely uses naive bounding-box/haversine in SQL (not PostGIS), acceptable at hackathon scale but not for large deployments; vercel.json deploys backend as a single serverless function with a persistent pg Pool, which is a known anti-pattern for serverless (connection exhaustion risk)
production_readiness.reasoning: Only the help-requests slice is deployable and functional end-to-end; there is no authentication, no observability, no CI, and most of the product surface (shelters, chat, directory) is non-functional mock UI, so it is not ready for real-world emergency deployment as-is.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Mixed: backend help-requests feature is real, working, tested logic; frontend is largely AI-generated-looking scaffolding/mock screens (chat, shelters, directory) wired to hardcoded arrays instead of the API
maturity.reasoning: 35 commits, 4 contributors, real backend unit/integration tests (test/helpRequests.*.test.js) and a normalized SQL schema show genuine engineering effort on one feature, but most of the visible app surface is unconnected mock data, and there's no CI pipeline to validate any of it.

viability.reasoning: The core SOS-reporting flow (geolocation capture, accessible-needs categorization, submission to a real Postgres-backed API) is a credible, narrowly-scoped MVP that could plausibly help in an emergency, but the rest of the promised platform (shelter mapping, volunteer chat, directory) is fake/mocked, there is no auth or abuse protection on a public emergency API, and no deployment pipeline/CI exists, so it is far from something that could be safely turned on for real earthquake victims.

key_features: Multi-step accessible SOS request flow with geolocation capture and fallback coordinates
Backend REST API for creating, listing (with geo-radius + status filters), accepting, and resolving help requests
PostgreSQL schema with CHECK constraints enforcing status/assignment/resolution data integrity
Backend test suite covering validation and route behavior
Mocked shelter map (Leaflet) with accessibility-tag filters (rampas, baño adaptado, lengua de señas) — UI only, no backend
Mocked chat/messaging UI for volunteer coordination — static data, no real-time or backend integration
analysis.merge_potential: Could contribute its help-request/SOS backend (schema + REST API pattern with geo search and status-constrained workflow) as a solid reference or even direct component for a merged platform's emergency-request module; its mocked shelter map and chat UIs would need to be rebuilt against a real shelters/chat backend rather than merged as-is.
red_flags: No authentication or rate limiting on public endpoints that create/accept/resolve emergency help requests, open to spam or malicious abuse
Majority of frontend pages (shelters/refugios, chat, directorio, recursos) are hardcoded mock data with no backend connection despite appearing feature-complete in the UI
No CI/CD pipeline and no Docker/containerization; deployment relies solely on Vercel config with a persistent pg Pool in a serverless function (connection-pool risk)
No logging/monitoring beyond console.error, making incident response or abuse detection impossible in production

AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md files suggest significant AI-assisted/agent-driven development, and the sharp real-vs-mock split across features corroborates uneven, possibly rushed hackathon-style implementation

diffusion.recommended_angle: If polished, the accessible-SOS-for-disabled-victims niche is a genuinely differentiated and emotionally resonant angle for social content — but only once a live demo/video exists, since right now there is nothing visual to show.
gaps: No live demo URL or deployed app to link to
No demo video
No screenshots
Most visible screens (shelter map, chat, directory) are mock/fake, so a demo would misrepresent capability
No branding assets beyond README emoji-heavy markdown

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL is just the GitHub repo, not a deployed app. No demo video provided. Only one backend feature (help-request creation/accept/resolve) is real and tested; everything else visible in the described UI (shelter map, chat, directory, resources) is static mock data with no backend wiring, so even if someone spun up the repo locally, most of the app would not actually function as advertised.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: SARA busca que ninguna persona con discapacidad quede sin ayuda tras un terremoto — pero hoy es solo un prototipo de backend, sin demo pública que mostrar.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Accessibility for people with disabilities during disaster response is a well-documented, severe and often-overlooked gap in emergency relief (lost mobility aids, no accessible shelters, no sign-language interpreters, no accessible information). This is a real and specific problem for a vulnerable subgroup of earthquake victims in Venezuela, distinct from generic aid-matching apps. However, the analysis shows the team only built and tested one slice of the full vision (the SOS/help-request flow with geolocation, backed by a real Postgres schema with CHECK constraints and test coverage); the rest of the described solution (shelter map, volunteer chat, org directory, donations/loans of equipment) is hardcoded mock UI with zero backend integration, so most of the promised problem-solving capability does not actually exist yet.

name: Sismología VE
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?
Desarrollamos **Sismología VE**, un sistema de monitoreo y alerta temprana de sismos en tiempo real diseñado específicamente para Venezuela y sus regiones colindantes. El proyecto consta de:
1. Una aplicación web altamente interactiva y responsiva desplegada en Wasmer Edge ([terremotos.wasmer.app](https://terremotos.wasmer.app/)).
2. Una aplicación nativa para Android con un servicio en segundo plano que se ejecuta de forma persistente para recibir alertas inmediatas.

## ¿Para quién es?
Está dirigido a la población general de Venezuela, servicios de emergencia locales y cualquier persona que necesite notificaciones confiables e inmediatas de eventos sísmicos, especialmente en una región donde los canales oficiales suelen presentar demoras o caídas de servicio.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?
- **Entrega en tiempo real**: Utiliza una conexión WebSocket persistente directa con el EMSC (Centro Sismológico Europeo-Mediterráneo) y redes regionales (como el SGC de Colombia) para recibir y notificar sismos en cuestión de segundos, eliminando los retrasos de consulta tradicionales.
- **Mecanismos de respaldo (Resiliencia)**: Si la conexión a internet falla o el WebSocket se desconecta, el cliente cambia automáticamente a un flujo de respaldo (consulta al servidor de IONOS, lectura de caché local en localStorage y, finalmente, una base de datos local precargada).
- **Actualización de alta frecuencia**: Cuenta con un scraper optimizado en el servidor que descarga y procesa datos de FUNVISIS cada 20 segundos, evadiendo la caché CDN para garantizar datos frescos constantemente.
- **Diseño centrado en el usuario**: Incorpora filtros de búsqueda por magnitud y región, alarmas de audio configurables y un simulador visual para pruebas de preparación ciudadana.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?
1. Entrar a la web en vivo en [terremotos.wasmer.app](https://terremotos.wasmer.app/).
2. Probar el **Simulador de Sismos** integrado para generar un sismo de prueba y observar cómo reaccionan la interfaz, los marcadores en el mapa y la alerta sonora.
3. Descargar e instalar la aplicación de Android (disponible a través del banner superior de la web) para probar el servicio de monitoreo en segundo plano.

analysis.summary: A single-developer earthquake-monitoring tool for Venezuela that scrapes FUNVISIS official seismic data every minute, republishes it as a USGS-style GeoJSON on an IONOS-hosted WordPress site, and serves it through a Vite/Leaflet web map and an Android WebView-wrapper app with background push notifications for new quakes. Functionally it works end-to-end, but the repo commits plaintext production SSH credentials and a cron secret token in DEPLOY.md, ships APKs signed with the Android debug key by design, and has no CI, no meaningful automated tests, and a hand-rolled deploy process reliant on a single external host.
analysis.project_type: Android app (WebView-based) + companion Vite/Leaflet web map, backed by a Python scraper and a manually-managed IONOS/WordPress backend

backend: PHP cron endpoint on IONOS (sismos-cron.php, not in repo)
database: Flat JSON file (sismos_venezuela.json) as the data store, no real DB
infra_deploy: WordPress (ForjaDigital) used purely as static file host

architecture.pattern: Static-data fan-out: a cron-triggered Python scraper pulls FUNVISIS JSON, transforms it, and republishes a flat GeoJSON file that a web app and an Android WebView app poll with a 3-tier fallback (remote -> localStorage -> bundled local copy)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database; a single JSON file on disk is the entire persistence layer
architecture.api_design: No real API; clients GET static JSON files (sismos_venezuela.json, version.json) directly from a shared host, plus one undocumented PHP cron endpoint secured only by a static query-string token
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Weak: deploy/runtime configuration, secrets, and operational runbook are mixed directly into a Markdown file in the repo (DEPLOY.md) rather than environment variables or a secrets manager
architecture.modularity: Three loosely coupled artifacts (Android app, Vite web app, Python scraper) sharing a JSON contract; Android app itself is essentially a Compose WebView shell plus a foreground Service for polling/notifications, with an unused DataRepository/ViewModel boilerplate stub left over from the Android Studio template
notable_patterns: Offline-first fallback chain (network -> localStorage -> bundled asset) explicitly designed for post-earthquake connectivity loss
Foreground Android Service + polling loop for push notifications on new seismic events
Manually bundled offline Leaflet map tiles (thousands of PNGs committed to the repo) for offline map rendering
Deliberate practice of always shipping debug-signed APKs for web distribution to avoid signature-conflict update issues

code_organization.directory_structure: Confusing/duplicated: app/, test/, and web/ each contain near-full copies of the Android and web projects (e.g. app/app and test/app), with 'test/' actually being the primary web-app source tree rather than a test directory, and 'web/' being a generated build output committed to git
code_organization.naming_quality: Inconsistent — 'test' directory holds production source (src/main.js), not tests; real tests live under Android's standard test/androidTest paths
code_organization.documentation_quality: DEPLOY.md is extremely detailed operationally but functions as an internal runbook with hardcoded secrets rather than developer documentation; no README describing the project for outside contributors
code_organization.reasoning: Duplicated project trees, a build-output directory committed to source control, and a misleadingly named 'test' directory holding real app source create real navigability and hygiene problems.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Critical failure: DEPLOY.md (tracked in git, pushed to public GitHub) contains the production server hostname, SSH username and plaintext password, and a cron endpoint auth token in cleartext
production_readiness.security_notes: Plaintext production SSH credentials and a cron secret are committed to a public repo; the cron endpoint is protected only by a static, guessable-format query token; APKs are deliberately shipped signed with the Android debug key, which is insecure practice for distributed builds; no authentication/authorization exists anywhere since the app only reads public data
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Entire system depends on a single shared-hosting VPS (IONOS) with a flat JSON file as the data store and a 1-minute cron scrape; no redundancy, no CDN-appropriate caching strategy (deliberately disabled), and no monitoring/alerting beyond a text log file
production_readiness.reasoning: The app has real, working error handling (try/except in scraper, try/catch in Android polling loop) and a thought-out offline fallback chain, but committed production credentials, no CI, no auth, single-host dependency, and debug-signed release binaries make this unsuitable to deploy as-is for a real emergency-response use case without a security remediation pass.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working project with 403 commits and live production deployment, but padded with leftover Android Studio boilerplate (unused DataRepository/ViewModel, template unit tests) and duplicated directory trees
maturity.reasoning: 403 commits, an active FUNVISIS scraper with real timezone-correction bug-fix logic, a functioning Android background monitor service, and evidence of iterative production deploys (DEPLOY.md changelog) show this is a genuinely operated tool, not empty scaffolding — but test coverage is just the unmodified Android template test and there's no README or CI.

viability.reasoning: It solves a narrow, genuinely useful problem (real-time Venezuelan earthquake alerts with offline resilience) and is already live in production for actual users, which is a strong positive for a disaster-response hackathon; however, the committed plaintext server credentials are a serious, immediately exploitable security liability that must be fixed before any responsible reuse or scaling, and the single-host, no-CI, file-based architecture will not scale to broader emergency infrastructure without rework.

key_features: Scrapes official FUNVISIS seismic data every minute and republishes it as a USGS-style GeoJSON feed
Android app with foreground background service that polls for new earthquakes and pushes local notifications
Web map (Leaflet) with offline-bundled map tiles for use without internet access
Three-tier data fallback (live server -> localStorage cache -> APK-bundled snapshot) designed to keep working when connectivity drops after a quake
In-app update-check mechanism comparing a remote version.json against the installed APK version
analysis.merge_potential: Could serve as a real-time seismic alert data source or offline-map-tile reference for a broader Build4Venezuela disaster-response platform, but its scraped JSON feed and Android app should be integrated via its data API only after the credential leak is remediated and hosting is moved off a single hardcoded shared-hosting account.
red_flags: Plaintext production SSH host/username/password and a cron secret token committed to DEPLOY.md in a public GitHub repository
Release APKs for the general public are deliberately signed with the Android debug key rather than a proper release key
No CI/CD pipeline and only unmodified Android Studio boilerplate tests, effectively zero real automated test coverage
Single point of failure: entire backend is one IONOS shared-hosting account with a flat JSON file as the data store and no monitoring beyond a text log
Duplicated/confusing repo layout ('test/' holds production source code, 'web/' is a committed build artifact)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Alertas sísmicas en tiempo real para Venezuela, resilientes incluso sin internet tras un terremoto: web + app Android con datos de FUNVISIS/EMSC.
gaps: No demo video available
No screenshots asset captured/prepared for social posts
Plaintext production SSH credentials and a cron secret are committed to the public GitHub repo (DEPLOY.md) — should be remediated before driving heavy traffic/attention to the project
Android APKs are shipped signed with the debug key, not a proper release key
No README describing the project for outside contributors, which weakens credibility for press/partners who click through to the repo

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch confirmed the live URL loads a real, functional dashboard (not just a landing page): live stats (24h/monthly max magnitude/avg depth), time-range and magnitude filters, an interactive Leaflet map with a simulation mode, a notification system, and Android APK / iOS home-screen install prompts. At fetch time it showed 0 detected earthquakes (\"Cargando eventos sísmicos...\"), consistent with no recent seismic activity rather than a broken feed.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Sismología VE: alertas sísmicas en tiempo real para Venezuela que siguen funcionando aunque se caiga la conexión tras un terremoto.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Venezuela sits near active seismic zones and its official alert channels (FUNVISIS) are described by the team as slow or unreliable; a real-time, offline-resilient earthquake notification system (web + Android background service, with a network -> localStorage -> bundled-data fallback chain) directly addresses a life-safety gap for the general population and emergency responders during and immediately after a quake. This is a genuine, narrowly-scoped need distinct from Venezuela's broader crisis themes, and the project is already live with 403 commits of real iterative development rather than a hackathon stub.

name: SismoVzla
db_description:
# Páginas Amarillas: Sismo Vzla

**Páginas Amarillas: Sismo Vzla** es una plataforma web de contingencia y código abierto diseñada para centralizar, organizar y verificar herramientas de ayuda frente a la crisis sísmica en Venezuela. Ante la sobreinformación y dispersión de enlaces críticos que ocurre en situaciones de emergencia, este directorio actúa como un hub único, ágil y confiable para la ciudadanía.

### ✨ Características Principales:
*   **Directorio Centralizado:** Agrupa y clasifica bases de datos en tiempo real sobre desaparecidos, pacientes hospitalizados, centros de donación, refugios y mapas de riesgo.
*   **Búsqueda Inteligente (Algoritmo Flexible):** Implementación de un motor de búsqueda interno con *stemming* básico que permite a los usuarios encontrar recursos rápidamente, reconociendo raíces de palabras para tolerar errores tipográficos en momentos de estrés.
*   **Diseño Accesible (Estética Retro/Pixel-Art):** Interfaz limpia, de alto contraste y gamificada visualmente para reducir la carga cognitiva del usuario en momentos de crisis. 
*   **Internacionalización (i18n):** Soporte multilingüe nativo para facilitar el acceso a ONG y cuerpos de ayuda internacional.

Está construido bajo una arquitectura moderna y escalable utilizando:
*   **Frontend:** Next.js (React), TypeScript y Tailwind CSS.
*   **Infraestructura:** Despliegue en Vercel (Edge Network) con dominio propio (`sismove.info`) para garantizar alta disponibilidad ante picos de tráfico masivos.

architecture.pattern: Single Next.js App Router client app reading one static Firestore document via the Firebase JS SDK; no backend/API routes of its own
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Firebase Firestore client SDK (firebase/firestore) used directly from a client-side service module (getDoc on a single 'directory/all_sites' document); no server-side data layer, no ORM, no writes from the app
architecture.api_design: None — no REST/GraphQL endpoints; data fetched client-side directly from Firestore, cached in sessionStorage for 30 minutes
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable for its size: hooks/useSites.ts wraps services/sitesService.ts wraps lib/firebase.ts, with data/categories.ts and data/sites.ts as typed constants
architecture.modularity: Small, flat structure (components, hooks, services, lib, data, locales); everything fits in ~716 LOC
notable_patterns: Client-side session caching of Firestore reads
next-intl i18n middleware for es/en locales
Category-based client-side filtering with a custom fuzzy-match search function

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear conventional Next.js layout (src/app, components, hooks, services, lib, data, locales) appropriate for the app's small scope
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming (WebSiteItem, useSites, sitesService, CategoryId)
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is a compelling mission statement (origin story, how to contribute) but has zero technical/setup documentation (no env var list, no install/run instructions beyond implicit npm scripts)
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for its size but the codebase is extremely thin (~716 LOC total) — essentially a single-page directory listing app with almost no business logic.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Firebase config uses NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars (expected/acceptable for Firebase client SDK, but there is no .env.example in the repo and no documentation of required vars), .env*.local is gitignored
production_readiness.security_notes: No Firestore security rules are present in the repo (data is presumably admin-managed elsewhere), no authentication/authorization anywhere in the app, no rate limiting, no input validation beyond a client-side search filter — acceptable only because the app is read-only public content, but there is no visibility into how the single Firestore document is written/moderated
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Entire directory is stored as one Firestore document fetched wholesale on load; fine for tens/hundreds of entries but this pattern doesn't scale to large datasets or support server-side pagination/search
production_readiness.reasoning: Only a Dockerfile.dev (development-mode Docker, running `npm run dev`) exists — there is no production Dockerfile, no CI/CD workflow (the only .github file is CODEOWNERS), and no tests of any kind. Error handling exists (try/catch with a user-facing retry UI) but overall this is a lightly-productionized static content directory, not a robust system.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working code (not generic AI boilerplate) — it's a genuine, functioning small app reportedly live at sismove.info — but the scope is minimal: a single searchable/filterable card grid pulling from one Firestore doc, no server logic, no tests, no CI.
maturity.reasoning: 25 commits, single contributor, no tests, no CI/CD pipeline, no production deployment config in-repo; README focuses on mission/community rather than engineering rigor.

viability.reasoning: It is a simple, low-risk, already-deployed link directory that genuinely helps route people to other resources (hospitals, missing-persons lists, donation centers) during the earthquake response, and its simplicity is itself a virtue for fast, reliable operation; however it provides no unique functionality beyond curating external links, has no tests/CI/production Docker config, and its usefulness is entirely dependent on the quality/freshness of a single manually-maintained Firestore document with no visible moderation or update workflow in the code.

key_features: Bilingual (es/en) categorized directory of contingency-related websites/resources (missing persons, hospitals, donations, pets, volunteering, maps)
Client-side fuzzy search and category filtering over the directory list
SessionStorage caching of Firestore reads (30 min TTL) to reduce reads
Retro/pixel-art themed responsive UI with Venezuela flag color branding
Loading/error states with a manual retry button on Firestore fetch failure
analysis.merge_potential: Best used as a lightweight, standalone landing/directory page (e.g., embedded or linked from a more feature-rich hub) rather than merged code-wise into another project, since it has no shared data model, no API, and no auth to integrate with other tools; its Firestore 'directory' document schema (WebSiteItem list) could however be adopted as a simple resource-linking convention by other Build4Venezuela projects.
red_flags: No automated tests of any kind
No CI/CD workflow (only a CODEOWNERS file under .github)
No production Dockerfile — only Dockerfile.dev running `next dev`
No Firestore security rules in-repo and no documentation of how the single 'directory/all_sites' document is authored/moderated, so data integrity/trust cannot be verified from the code
Single contributor, only 25 commits, project created 3 days before this review (2026-06-28)
analysis.summary: Sismo Vzla is a small, real, and already-live (sismove.info) bilingual Next.js directory app that curates and lets users search/filter external contingency resources (missing persons, hospitals, donation centers, volunteering, maps) for the Venezuela earthquake response. It reads a single Firestore document client-side via the Firebase SDK, has no backend, auth, tests, CI, or production Docker config, and is essentially a well-executed but very thin 'link aggregator' rather than a full-featured aid platform.
analysis.project_type: web app (Next.js client-rendered directory/link-aggregator)

infra_deploy: implied Vercel/static hosting for sismove.info (no config in repo)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as an open-source 'yellow pages' for the earthquake response — one bilingual link to find missing-persons lists, hospitals, donation centers, and risk maps instead of scattered social media posts. Hold the push until the live directory reliably loads content and has a visible, credible pipeline for who curates/verifies the linked resources.
gaps: Live demo currently shows only a 'Cargando directorio...' loading state with no visible content in an automated fetch — needs manual browser verification that Firestore data actually renders for end users
No demo video or screenshots to show the populated directory in action
No documented data moderation/update process, so donors/press can't be told how fresh or trustworthy the listed resources are
No tests, CI, or production deploy config — a single-document Firestore backend fetched wholesale is fragile at any real traffic spike

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch on https://www.sismove.info/es rendered a page shell with the full category navigation (Ver Todo, Desaparecidos, Niños, Mascotas, Hospitales, Ayudas/Donaciones, Voluntariado, Denuncias, Mapas de Riesgo, Aportar) but the content area only showed "Cargando directorio..." (Loading directory...) with no directory entries visible. This may be a limitation of the fetch tool not executing the client-side Firebase/Firestore JS fetch (the app is architected as client-side-only data loading per the insight JSON), rather than proof the site is truly broken — but it means the automated check could not confirm the directory actually populates with real resource links. A manual browser check is needed before promoting.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: SismoVzla: las "páginas amarillas" del sismo — un solo hub bilingüe que centraliza desaparecidos, hospitales, refugios, donaciones y mapas de riesgo para no perderse en la avalancha de enlaces durante la emergencia.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: During a disaster, critical links (missing-persons registries, hospital status, donation centers, shelters, risk maps) get scattered across social media, WhatsApp forwards, and ad-hoc threads, making them hard to find and easy to lose amid misinformation. A single, bilingual, categorized, searchable directory that curates and verifies these links addresses a well-known and recurring pain point in emergency response (info fragmentation), and is genuinely useful to both victims and NGOs/international responders. However, the project itself is a thin link-aggregator (716 LOC, single Firestore document, no visible moderation workflow) rather than a feature-rich aid platform, so its real-world impact depends entirely on how well-populated and maintained the underlying resource list is — something not verifiable from the code or a quick live check.

name: Somos Huella
db_description:
Somos Huella permite registrar personas buscadas con foto, última ubicación y contacto.

Después, si alguien encuentra a una persona (desde chicos hasta personas lesionadas que no pueden comunicarse) puede subir una foto y el sistema compara ese rostro contra el registro para sugerir posibles candidatos usando visión artificial.

La idea es convertir cada rostro en una huella numérica y usar esa huella para buscar coincidencias de forma rápida y ordenada.

El flujo es simple:

La familia registra.
El sistema compara.
Una persona verifica.

Algunos detalles técnicos:

→ Cada rostro se convierte en una huella de 512 dimensiones.
→ La comparación típica tarda alrededor de 0,5 segundos.
→ Las coincidencias se ordenan por parecido.
→ El sistema está en producción con sitio, API y búsqueda por rostro.

analysis.summary: Somos Huella (ML-FaceRecognizer) is a real, working face-recognition system for family reunification of earthquake/disaster victims: families register a missing person with a photo, and finders upload a photo to get ranked candidate matches for human verification. It has a genuinely well-engineered FastAPI backend (auth, rate limiting, magic-byte upload validation, atomic writes, CORS), a facenet-pytorch (MTCNN + InceptionResnetV1) matching core, a local-GPU indexer / VPS-CPU search split, a Next.js frontend proxying requests server-side to hide the API key, and deploy configs already targeting a live domain (somoshuella.org).
analysis.project_type: web app (FastAPI backend + Next.js frontend) with an ML batch-indexing CLI tool

database: Flat-file index: numpy .npz embeddings matrix + index.json metadata (no relational DB); docs mention future migration to Supabase pgvector
infra_deploy: Docker (frontend Dockerfile, multi-stage, standalone Next output)
systemd unit for API on VPS (OVH)
ai_ml: facenet-pytorch (MTCNN face detector + InceptionResnetV1 VGGFace2 encoder)
PyTorch (CUDA for local indexer, CPU for VPS API)

architecture.pattern: Split GPU-indexer / CPU-search-API architecture with a proxy frontend: core/ (shared matching engine) + indexer/ (local GPU batch embedding CLI) + api/ (FastAPI search/register REST service on VPS) + frontend/ (Next.js UI with server-side route handlers proxying to the API to keep the API key server-only)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No ORM/DB — custom IndexStore class persists an in-memory (N,512) float32 matrix + JSON metadata list to disk atomically (tmp file + os.replace), loaded at API startup; explicitly designed to later swap for Supabase pgvector without changing the calling interface.
architecture.api_design: REST endpoints via FastAPI (/health, /search, /register, /people, /people/{id} DELETE, /index/reload), Pydantic response schemas, per-endpoint rate limits, all endpoints except /health gated behind x-api-key header dependency with constant-time comparison.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation between core matching logic, GPU indexer, API layer (auth/uploads/config/schemas as separate modules), and frontend; frontend never talks directly with the backend API key, only via its own server route handlers.
architecture.modularity: Well modularized: core/, indexer/, api/, frontend/, deploy/, docs/specs/ each with focused responsibility and even a legacy prototype/ folder kept for reference.
notable_patterns: magic-byte file type sniffing instead of trusting extension/mimetype
streaming upload size limiting to avoid loading whole file in memory
global threading.Lock to serialize writes to the shared index
atomic file writes via tmp+os.replace for index persistence
fail-fast boot if API_KEY missing (refuses to run unauthenticated)
query photos deleted immediately after embedding extraction (data minimization)

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear top-level split (core/indexer/api/frontend/deploy/docs/tests/registry/scripts) mirroring the documented specs in docs/specs/*.md; deploy/ further split by target (vps/cloudflare/scripts).
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish/English mixed naming, docstrings explain intent and reference spec numbers throughout.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Strong: README with architecture diagram, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, per-module docs/specs/*.md, per-folder README files (api/README.md, indexer/README.md, registry/README.md, deploy/vps/README.md), inline module docstrings explaining hardening decisions.
code_organization.reasoning: Directory layout matches a deliberate design (specs written before/alongside implementation), naming and docstrings are unusually thorough for a hackathon project, though there's no CI to enforce structure/quality over time.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env-based via pydantic-settings, .env.example provided with placeholders, API key never sent to browser (frontend proxies via server-only route handlers), boot fails hard if API_KEY unset.
production_readiness.security_notes: API key auth with constant-time compare, restricted CORS (never '*'), magic-byte + size-limited upload validation, rate limiting per IP via slowapi, audit logging of register/delete/reload actions with masked API key. Weaknesses: no user auth/roles (single shared API key for all admin actions), no HTTPS enforcement in code itself (relies on Caddy), no encryption at rest for photos/index, single VPS with single systemd worker is a scalability/availability bottleneck.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Explicitly single-worker (uvicorn --workers 1) with a global write lock serializing register/delete/reload — fine for low-volume MVP but will bottleneck under real disaster-response load; index is held fully in memory and grows unbounded; docs acknowledge a planned migration to Supabase pgvector for scale but that migration has not been implemented.
production_readiness.reasoning: Backend shows genuine production-hardening effort (auth, validation, rate limiting, atomic persistence, CORS) unusual for a hackathon submission, but lacks CI/CD, automated deploy pipeline, horizontal scalability, and a real database, and there is no evidence of monitoring/alerting beyond a PowerShell go-live-monitor script.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, working implementation — not boilerplate
maturity.reasoning: Only 10 commits from a single contributor over ~1 day, and no automated CI, but the code is functionally complete end-to-end (indexer -> API -> frontend), includes a genuine smoke test that exercises the real ML pipeline, and is already deployed with production-style hardening at a live domain.

viability.reasoning: This directly and concretely serves the hackathon's stated goal (reuniting missing persons after a disaster) with a working, already-deployed system (somoshuella.org), real ML matching, and sane security defaults; main risks to real-world use are scale (single VPS, single-writer file-based index, no real DB), lack of CI, and the ethical/legal weight of biometric matching on vulnerable populations which the team does acknowledge (human-verification disclaimer).

key_features: Face registration endpoint (photo -> 512-d embedding via MTCNN+InceptionResnetV1, stored with contact/location metadata)
Face search endpoint returning ranked candidate matches above a similarity threshold with a human-verification disclaimer
GPU-side batch indexer CLI for bulk-loading a registry of missing persons
API-key auth, IP rate limiting, magic-byte upload validation, atomic index persistence
Next.js frontend with photo capture/register/search UI and server-side API-key-hiding proxy routes
Deploy tooling for a VPS (systemd unit, Caddy snippet, docker-compose, go-live monitor script)
analysis.merge_potential: Could integrate as a specialized face-matching microservice behind a broader disaster-response platform (e.g., feeding its /search results into a shelter-mapping or aid-logistics coordination system), or accept missing-person registrations from a shared intake/database layer instead of maintaining its own flat-file index.
red_flags: No CI/CD or automated testing pipeline (tests exist but are manual smoke tests, not run in CI)
Single shared API key for all write operations — no per-user accounts, roles, or granular audit trail beyond masked key + IP

Index persisted as flat .npz/.json files with a single in-process lock and single uvicorn worker — will not scale or survive concurrent multi-instance deployment without the planned (unimplemented) Supabase/pgvector migration
Biometric facial recognition on vulnerable disaster-affected populations raises consent/privacy/legal concerns not addressed beyond a UI disclaimer and query-photo deletion

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After a major earthquake, families get separated and injured/disoriented survivors often cannot communicate who they are. Somos Huella lets families register a missing person with a photo and lets rescuers/finders upload a photo of someone found to get ranked face-match candidates for human verification, directly addressing family reunification — a well-documented acute need in disaster response.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://www.somoshuella.org returns HTTP 200 (Next.js prerendered page served via Cloudflare/Caddy, matching the documented deploy architecture). WebFetch tool itself was blocked by Cloudflare bot protection (403), but curl with a browser user-agent successfully retrieved full HTML: a polished Spanish-language landing page ('Volver a encontrar a quien falta') with working navigation to /registro (register a missing person), /buscar (search by photo), and /ejemplos (demo examples), plus a live 'expediente vivo' stats widget. This is consistent with the codebase's described FastAPI backend + Next.js frontend architecture, not just a static mockup.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Highlight the human story: 'Cada rostro deja una huella' — a working face-recognition tool that helps reunite families after disasters when lists and memory aren't enough, built and deployed to production in ~1 day.
gaps: No demo video (video_url empty) — a short screen recording of register+search flow would strengthen any social push
No documented real-world usage/real-users yet (only 1 star, 1 contributor, 10 commits over 1 day) — frame as 'ready MVP' not 'battle-tested'
Project's own metadata lists Argentina as country context, not explicitly Venezuela — messaging should tie it clearly to the Venezuela earthquake use case
Biometric/privacy concerns on vulnerable populations are acknowledged in code but not addressed in any public-facing consent/legal copy — worth flagging before large-scale promotion

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Somos Huella convierte cada rostro en una huella digital para reunir familias separadas por el terremoto — sube una foto, el sistema sugiere coincidencias en segundos.

name: TerraVE
db_description:
TerraVE es una plataforma web que combina memoria ciudadana, datos geoespaciales y visión computacional para apoyar la evaluación inicial tras un desastre.

Responde dos preguntas fundamentales:

¿Qué existía aquí?
Mediante reportes ciudadanos, documenta infraestructura, viviendas y espacios que fueron afectados o desaparecieron tras el evento.

¿Qué quedó en pie?
Analiza fotografías mediante inteligencia artificial para realizar una clasificación preliminar del daño visible. Esta clasificación tiene fines de apoyo, y no sustituye la evaluación realizada por especialistas.

El resultado es un mapa colaborativo en tiempo real, acompañado de un dashboard con indicadores de la población afectada y capas de información geoespacial que ayudan a visualizar el estado de la infraestructura después de un terremoto.

architecture.pattern: Next.js 14 App Router monolith (server + client) with API routes acting as a thin BaaS backend for Supabase
