architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js) used as a BaaS/PostgREST wrapper; some routes bypass the SDK and call the Supabase REST API directly with fetch + hardcoded anon key headers (app/api/satelital, app/api/poblacion)
architecture.api_design: REST-ish Next.js route handlers (app/api/*/route.ts) for report, classify, lugares, geojson, satelital, poblacion, stats, geocode; one route uses zod with .strict() whitelisting for PATCH, most others do only manual field checks or none
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable: lib/supabase.ts, lib/classify.ts, lib/geo.ts separate DB/AI/geo helpers from route handlers and UI components
architecture.modularity: Small-to-medium codebase (~3.6k LOC) with clear folder split (app/api, components, lib, types) but some duplicated logic (COLOR_MAP repeated in 3 files, GeoJSON building duplicated between lib/geo.ts and app/api/geojson/route.ts)
notable_patterns: Custom in-memory rate limiter + naive SQL-injection regex filter in middleware.ts (no external lib, resets on redeploy/serverless cold start, ineffective for multi-instance/serverless)
AI classification calls a third-party Hugging Face model ('caidas/building-damage-classifier') that is graceful-degraded: if HF fails, the report is still saved unclassified

lugares/[id] PATCH route has no input validation/whitelisting (passes raw body straight to Supabase update) while report/[id] PATCH uses zod .strict() whitelist — inconsistent hardening across nearly identical endpoints

No authentication anywhere: Supabase RLS policies explicitly allow public INSERT/SELECT with the anon key ('app sin auth para hackathon'), so any client can write arbitrary data to lugares/reportes_dano

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, conventional Next.js App Router layout (app/, components/, lib/, types/) with route-per-feature API folders; presence of stray '. gitkeep' files (with a space) in nearly every folder betrays that the repo was built via GitHub web-upload rather than a normal git workflow
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (lugares, reportes_dano, color_semaforo) mapped to English types; generally readable
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is a thorough setup guide (env vars, Supabase schema steps, Vercel deploy) and a separate TerraVE_API.md exists, but references a .env.example file that is not actually present in the repo
code_organization.reasoning: Structure and naming are solid for a small hackathon app, but commit history (99 commits of 'Create .gitkeep' / 'Add files via upload') indicates the repo was assembled by uploading files through GitHub's web UI in one sitting rather than iterative development, and missing .env.example undercuts the stated setup docs.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Uses process.env vars (NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL/ANON_KEY, HF_TOKEN, GOOGLE_MAPS_KEY) referenced via .env.local per README, but no .env.example is committed despite being documented, and the Supabase anon key is a NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable used server-side too, tying client and server auth to the same public key
production_readiness.security_notes: No authentication/authorization anywhere; Supabase RLS explicitly grants public INSERT to any client with the anon key (comment: 'app sin auth para hackathon'), meaning anyone can write/spam fake places or damage reports; middleware's SQL-injection regex filter is a weak, easily-bypassed heuristic and provides false confidence (Supabase queries are already parameterized via PostgREST, so this is largely theater); in-memory rate limiter in middleware.ts resets per server instance and is ineffective on serverless/multi-instance deployments (e.g. Vercel); app/api/lugares/[id]/route.ts PATCH accepts and writes the raw request body with zero validation, unlike the sibling reports endpoint
production_readiness.scalability_notes: app/api/lugares/route.ts manually paginates in a loop fetching up to 1000 rows per request server-side to work around Supabase's default limit, which is inefficient and could time out on serverless functions as data grows; classify route synchronously calls an external HF inference API with a blocking 5s retry-on-503, risking request timeouts
production_readiness.reasoning: This is a working demo with reasonable error handling (try/catch, fallback flows for failed AI classification) and deploy story (Vercel + Supabase), but has zero auth, inconsistent/weak input validation across near-identical endpoints, no logging/observability, and a rate limiter/security filter that are cosmetic rather than robust — not close to production-grade for a real disaster-response tool handling public write access.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working custom logic (not generic boilerplate) — genuine damage classification mapping, geo utilities, Supabase schema with PostGIS, and multiple functional API routes — but with zero automated tests, no CI/CD pipeline, and a git history pattern (99 commits, mostly 'Create .gitkeep' and 'Add files via upload') consistent with a single-session GitHub web upload rather than iterative development.
maturity.reasoning: Substantial hand-written domain logic (AI classification mapping, Haversine geo math, Venezuela bounding-box validation, GeoJSON conversion) shows this isn't pure scaffolding, but complete absence of tests/CI and an artificial-looking commit history significantly limit maturity.

viability.reasoning: Addresses a genuinely useful post-earthquake need (crowdsourced infrastructure mapping + AI-assisted structural damage triage) with a coherent, working MVP (live demo linked, real Supabase schema, functioning classify/report/map flows), but relies on an unauthenticated write-open database, a third-party free-tier HF model of unverified accuracy for a safety-critical classification (risk of false 'no damage'/'safe' readings), and lacks tests, monitoring, or abuse protections needed before trusting it with real post-disaster deployment.

key_features: Citizen-submitted place reports (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) with before/after photos and geolocation
AI-based structural damage classification from photos via Hugging Face inference API (caidas/building-damage-classifier) mapped to a 4-level 'traffic light' severity scale
Interactive Leaflet map with GeoJSON endpoints for visualizing places colored by damage severity
Satellite SAR damage-detection data endpoint (Sentinel-1/Google Earth Engine sourced) and MMI population-exposure endpoint (UNOSAT-sourced) for the Caracas earthquake scenario
Dashboard with stats counters (total/evaluated/critical places)
Google Geocoding integration for address-to-coordinates lookup
analysis.merge_potential: Could be a strong complementary layer for a shelter-mapping or aid-logistics project that needs structural safety data — its damage-classification + geo endpoints (GeoJSON, population exposure) could feed into a broader coordination platform, but its open-write database and lack of auth would need hardening before integration with systems handling sensitive victim data.
red_flags: No authentication anywhere; Supabase RLS allows public INSERT with the anon key, enabling anyone to spam or falsify infrastructure/damage data

AI damage classification (life-safety relevant: 'no habitar', 'evacuación inmediata') depends on a free-tier third-party Hugging Face model of unstated/unverified accuracy, with silent fallback to 'no evaluado' if the API fails
No automated tests and no CI pipeline
In-memory rate limiter and regex-based SQL-injection filter in middleware are weak/cosmetic and ineffective in a serverless multi-instance deployment
README documents a .env.example file that does not exist in the repo

Git history (99 commits, mostly .gitkeep/upload) indicates the repo was built via GitHub web upload rather than genuine iterative version control, undermining confidence in real-world testing/iteration
analysis.summary: TerraVE is a Next.js 14 + Supabase (PostGIS) hackathon MVP that lets citizens report infrastructure locations and classify post-earthquake structural damage from photos via a Hugging Face vision model, displaying results on a Leaflet map with color-coded severity plus supplementary satellite-SAR and population-exposure data endpoints. The core flows are genuinely implemented (not boilerplate) with real geo math, DB schema, and graceful AI-failure handling, but the app has zero authentication (public-write RLS), inconsistent/missing input validation across endpoints, only cosmetic security middleware, no tests or CI, and a git history suggesting single-session web-upload assembly — making it a promising but currently non-production-ready prototype.
analysis.project_type: Web application (Next.js full-stack) — disaster-response infrastructure mapping + AI damage classification platform

backend: Next.js middleware (custom rate limiting + header hardening)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame as: 'Un mapa vivo del terremoto — la comunidad reporta qué queda en pie, la IA ayuda a priorizar qué inspeccionar primero.' Show live map + report form + AI classification flow as a 30-second screen capture since there's no demo video yet.
gaps: No demo video — a 30-60s screen recording of report -> AI classification -> map update would make this instantly shareable
No screenshots captured for social assets
No real usage data / testimonials (0 stars, 1 contributor, brand new repo)
Landing page shows placeholder stats (dashes) instead of live numbers, which undercuts credibility for donors
Should visibly caveat that AI damage classification is 'apoyo, no reemplaza evaluación de especialistas' in any promo copy to avoid overpromising safety guarantees

evaluation.live_demo_notes: Live URL loads and is a real Next.js app, not just a static landing page. The homepage is a landing/hub with nav to /map, /report, /classify and placeholder stat counters (shown as em-dashes, no live data yet). /report renders a genuinely complete form (place type, name, description, GPS/address/coordinates location picker, before/after photo upload, submit). /map returns a client-rendered Leaflet map that shows a 'Cargando mapa...' loading state in a non-JS fetch (expected for a client-hydrated Leaflet map; likely renders after JS runs, but could not be visually confirmed to have actual data-driven markers). Overall: a working prototype, not a placeholder — but with no live citizen-submitted data visible yet and no way to confirm end-to-end AI classification succeeded from this fetch alone.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: TerraVE convierte los reportes ciudadanos y fotos post-terremoto en un mapa colaborativo en tiempo real con clasificación de daños asistida por IA, para saber rápido qué sigue en pie y qué necesita ayuda urgente.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake structural triage and situational awareness (what infrastructure survived, what didn't, who needs urgent help) is one of the most acute needs in the first hours/days after a disaster, when official assessment teams are overwhelmed. TerraVE directly targets this with crowdsourced 'what existed here' reporting plus AI-assisted 'what's left standing' photo classification on an interactive map with population-exposure and satellite-SAR overlays for a Caracas earthquake scenario — this is a coherent, well-scoped answer to a real, high-stakes problem, not a generic tool retrofitted to the theme.

name: Venemed
db_description:
VeneMed — Coordinación de donaciones para centros de salud, con orden y a tiempo.

Hospitales, refugios y centros de acopio reciben donaciones de forma descoordinada. Muchos insumos se desperdician porque nadie tiene visibilidad real de qué se necesita. El resultado es doble: recursos comunitarios desperdiciados y centros colapsados que ya no pueden procesar más ayuda.

Cómo funciona: Los centros publican solicitudes con ventanas cortas de tiempo (12, 24 o 48 horas) indicando exactamente lo que necesitan. Los donantes navegan la lista sin login: ven en tiempo real lo que cada centro está pidiendo y por cuánto tiempo. Esa información es compartible en redes, para que más gente sepa dónde llevar ayuda antes de que la ventana cierre. Si un centro entra en saturación, puede publicar un aviso de exceso para frenar donaciones de un ítem específico.

Para quién. El centro de salud (acceso por WhatsApp, panel propio para crear y gestionar solicitudes) y el donante (acceso público, sin registro). Pensado mobile-first y para funcionar con conexión inestable.

Objetivo. Que la ayuda llegue donde y cuando hace falta — y que ningún recurso comunitario se pierda en el camino.

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with route groups ((public)/(center)/(admin)), server actions for mutations, route handlers for cron/API
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM over Postgres (Supabase), raw `postgres` driver; migrations checked in with drizzle-kit; Supabase client used only for auth, not data access
architecture.api_design: Mostly server actions ('use server') per feature (registro, publicar, gestionar, aviso, recepcion, editar) plus one secured GET route handler for cron; no generic REST/GraphQL API
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear layering: src/lib/auth (authz guards), src/lib/<domain>/validation.ts (zod-less but explicit validation), src/db (schema/queries/jobs), src/app/(group)/actions (mutations) — good separation between UI, business logic, and data access
architecture.modularity: Feature-based route groups with colocated _components, shared UI kit in src/components/ui, domain libs under src/lib
notable_patterns: Partial unique index enforcing 'one active surplus per center' at DB layer
Constant-time bearer comparison for cron auth (timingSafeEqual)
Middleware only checks session presence; real authorization (is_platform_admin) enforced server-side per request via requireAdmin()
Feature flags via NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars
Sentry configured with PII scrubbing and no session replay

code_organization.directory_structure: Well-organized App Router route groups by actor ((public)/(center)/(admin)), colocated components, dedicated src/db and src/lib/<domain> folders
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive English identifiers with Spanish UI copy separated into labels maps
code_organization.documentation_quality: Extensive, high-signal inline comments explaining non-obvious tradeoffs (e.g. enum append-only strategy, TOCTOU race prevention, timing-safe auth), plus docs/specs/*.md design docs and AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md for AI-assisted dev context
code_organization.reasoning: Directory layout, naming, and comments are all above typical hackathon quality — reads like a maintained production repo with deliberate architectural decisions documented inline.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Uses .env.example with clear separation of local vs prod values; secrets (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, CRON_SECRET, SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN) never committed; CRON_SECRET compared with constant-time timingSafeEqual; local Supabase demo keys explicitly noted as public/non-secret
production_readiness.security_notes: Supabase email OTP auth with Cloudflare Turnstile captcha, RLS-bypass-aware admin authz gate that never trusts client-supplied actor id, DB-level unique constraints preventing race conditions, middleware avoids open-redirect by stripping ?next=, fail-closed cron auth
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single Postgres via Supabase pooler for runtime and direct connection for migrations; cron-based expiry via GitHub Actions (Vercel Hobby tier limitation) rather than native scheduling — a reasonable but noted workaround; no obvious caching/queue layer, but scale target (regional medical-aid matching) doesn't require heavy throughput
production_readiness.reasoning: Has real auth, Sentry error monitoring with hardened PII config, CI (lint/typecheck) and separate e2e workflow against ephemeral local Supabase, DB migrations, and a scheduled cron job with authenticated endpoint — this goes well beyond typical hackathon scaffolding into a genuinely deployable app (already live at venemed.vercel.app).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, actively developed application
maturity.reasoning: 31 commits, 14.4k LOC, live deployment, 9 DB migrations with schema evolution notes, Playwright e2e suite (~590 lines across 3 spec files) run in CI against a real local Supabase stack, plus design docs/specs for each feature slice — clearly real, iteratively built software, not generated boilerplate.

viability.reasoning: VeneMed solves a concrete, narrow, high-value problem (matching Venezuelan health centers' time-windowed medical supply needs with donors) with a working moderation/approval pipeline, expiring requests, and center/donor/admin flows already implemented and deployed; main risk is dependence on centers actually adopting and maintaining it and on donor traffic, not code quality.

key_features: Center registration with admin moderation/approval queue
Time-windowed supply need/surplus requests with automatic expiry via cron
Public donor-facing browse/search/filter of active requests with share tracking
Email OTP auth for centers and admins via Supabase
Reception pause toggle and 'aviso de exceso' (surplus) banner with one-active-per-center DB constraint
Sentry error monitoring with PII scrubbing
analysis.merge_potential: Could serve as a strong standalone medical-aid matching platform for hospitals/clinics post-disaster; could also integrate with a broader Build4Venezuela aid-logistics or shelter-mapping platform by exposing its center/request data via an API for cross-project donor routing.
red_flags: Single named contributor (bus factor of 1) despite otherwise high engineering quality
Cron-based expiry relies on external GitHub Actions schedule hitting prod — a missed/disabled workflow silently stops expiry
No visible rate limiting beyond Turnstile captcha on OTP send; other mutation endpoints not obviously rate-limited
analysis.summary: VeneMed is a genuinely production-grade Next.js + Supabase/Drizzle application connecting Venezuelan health centers with donors via time-windowed medical supply requests, featuring real auth (OTP + captcha), an admin moderation queue, Sentry monitoring, DB migrations, and a Playwright e2e suite run in CI against an ephemeral local Supabase — one of the most mature and deployable repos in this hackathon set.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web application (Next.js) for medical-aid donation matching

infra_deploy: GitHub Actions (CI, e2e, scheduled cron trigger)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: VeneMed addresses a well-documented pain point in disaster/aid response: donation coordination chaos. Hospitals, shelters, and collection centers receive donations with no visibility into what's actually needed, causing waste (excess of some items) while other critical needs go unmet and centers become saturated/overwhelmed. VeneMed lets health centers post time-windowed (12/24/48h) specific supply requests that donors can browse without login and share on social media, plus a 'surplus' flag to stop over-donation of a specific item. This directly targets earthquake/disaster medical-aid logistics, a core Build4Venezuela problem area.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch on https://venemed.vercel.app/ confirms the site loads and shows live functional content, not just a static landing page: an active request from 'Centro Medico Maracay' listing specific supplies needed (isopropyl alcohol, sterile gauze, surgical gloves, syringes), stats ('1 solicitudes, 3 centros'), a live countdown timer ('Vence en 3 min'), and navigation to a donor-facing requests portal and a separate center login/panel. This matches the codebase analysis (Next.js + Supabase/Drizzle, real auth via OTP+Turnstile, admin moderation queue, Playwright e2e tests in CI, Sentry monitoring, DB migrations) — a genuinely deployed, working app rather than boilerplate. Caveat: given the project is only 3 days old with 1 contributor and 1 GitHub star, the visible request/center data is very likely seed/demo data rather than confirmed real adopted health centers, so real-world usage/adoption is unverified.

diffusion.recommended_angle: 'Ninguna donación se pierde en el camino' — show the live countdown-timer request board where a health center in Venezuela asks for exactly what it needs (with a deadline), and donors can find and share it in seconds without creating an account.
gaps: No demo video to show the flow end-to-end for social content
No dedicated screenshots/press kit assets prepared
Real-user adoption unverified — visible data (3 centros, 1 solicitud) looks like seed/demo content rather than confirmed real health centers using it live
Single contributor / bus factor of 1 is a risk to flag before heavy promotion commitments

evaluation.one_line_pitch: VeneMed conecta centros de salud venezolanos con donantes en tiempo real: piden exactamente lo que necesitan, con fecha límite, y ningún insumo se desperdicia.

name: Venezuela Juntos (Missing Persons Facial Recognition)
db_description:
## What was built?

  **Venezuela Juntos** is a humanitarian face-matching web app designed to reunite Venezuelan families separated by disaster or displacement.

  ## How it works

Repo: https://github.com/OnBeIt/Venezuela_Juntos_v2

  - Families upload a photo of a missing person with their name
  - Rescuers upload a photo of someone they have found along with a contact phone number or email
  - The system automatically compares faces using AI-powered ArcFace recognition
  - Any pair exceeding the similarity threshold appears on the public Matches page with the rescuer's contact details
  - The app is fully bilingual, Spanish (default) and English

  ## Who is it for?

  Rescuers, volunteers, NGOs, and families searching for loved ones in Venezuelan disaster or displacement scenarios.

  ## Tech stack

  Python · FastAPI · InsightFace (ArcFace) · SQLite · Jinja2 · Tailwind CSS · Docker · Railway

  ## Try it

  Run the app, accept the disclaimer, and use the navigation to report a missing person or register someone who has been found.

analysis.summary: Venezuela Juntos is a FastAPI + SQLite disaster-relief app that lets families report missing persons and rescuers report found persons, then automatically cross-matches uploaded photos via ArcFace/InsightFace facial-recognition embeddings and cosine similarity. It has real, working authentication, per-user data isolation, an admin console with audit logging, and Railway/Docker deployment config — a genuinely functional single-service MVP, not boilerplate.
analysis.project_type: web app (server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2) for missing-person/found-person matching via facial recognition

architecture.pattern: monolithic server-rendered FastAPI app, single service, modular Python files (main/auth/database/matching/audit + lib/facerec)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: SQLAlchemy 2.x declarative models (User, MissingPerson, FoundPerson, Match, AuditLog) over SQLite; hand-rolled idempotent migration helper that ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMNs for schema evolution
architecture.api_design: traditional form-POST/redirect (303) server-rendered routes, no JSON REST API, no OpenAPI-first design beyond FastAPI defaults
architecture.separation_of_concerns: reasonably clean: main.py (routes), auth.py (password hashing/session), database.py (models/engine), matching.py (embedding/quality/search wrapper), audit.py (event logging), lib/facerec.py (standalone CLI-capable ArcFace toolkit)
architecture.modularity: good for its size; matching logic is cleanly decoupled from routes via a thread pool executor to avoid blocking the event loop during face inference
notable_patterns: vectorized cosine-similarity search (matrix @ probe) over all opposite-pool embeddings on every new upload — O(N) per upload, fine at small scale, will not scale to large N without an ANN index
authenticated photo serving via /uploads/<filename> route (ownership/match-based ACL) instead of a public static mount, with path-traversal guards
append-only audit_logs table plus stdout logging for observability
admin bootstrap + legacy-row backfill from ADMIN_EMAIL/ADMIN_PASSWORD env vars on startup
low-quality photo heuristic (blur/face-size/brightness) that warns but does not block upload

code_organization.directory_structure: flat, small, purposeful (main.py, auth.py, database.py, matching.py, audit.py, lib/facerec.py, templates/) — easy to navigate for ~1750 LOC
code_organization.naming_quality: clear, descriptive names (report_missing, admin_delete_user, _user_can_view_photo) and consistent event-type constants
code_organization.documentation_quality: unusually thorough README (routes table, schema, env vars, deployment steps, known limitations) plus module/function docstrings and inline comments explaining non-obvious decisions (thread pool, migration guard, cookie signing)
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for its scope with genuine docstrings and an honest 'known limitations' section in the README rather than marketing fluff.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: SECRET_KEY, ADMIN_EMAIL, ADMIN_PASSWORD read from env vars with .env gitignored; ephemeral SECRET_KEY generated if unset (sessions reset on restart) — no secrets committed, but no rate limiting on login/register (brute-force risk) and no visible CSRF protection on POST forms
production_readiness.security_notes: Auth uses PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 (200k rounds) with constant-time compare — reasonable; photo access is ACL-gated and path-traversal guarded. However: SQLite as sole datastore is a single point of failure with no backup story beyond a Railway volume; no email verification (README admits this); storing raw biometric face embeddings of vulnerable disaster victims with no encryption-at-rest, retention policy, or consent flow is a serious privacy concern for real-world deployment; no HTTPS enforcement (README flags this); no rate limiting anywhere.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single SQLite file + in-process ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) for CPU-bound face inference will bottleneck under real load; full-pool cosine-similarity scan on every upload is O(N) and will degrade as the missing/found pools grow into the thousands; no horizontal scaling story (SQLite can't be shared across instances).
production_readiness.reasoning: Functionally solid MVP with real auth/logging/error-handling and a documented deploy path, but SQLite-only storage, no rate limiting/CSRF, no HTTPS enforcement, and unaddressed biometric-data privacy/retention concerns make it unsuitable to run as-is for a real earthquake-relief deployment without hardening.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, working implementation — not scaffolding; contains genuine business logic (auth, ownership ACLs, quality heuristics, vectorized matching, audit trail, admin console) built up over 9 incremental commits
maturity.reasoning: Actively iterated (9 commits over 4 days, each adding a coherent feature: auth, per-user isolation, admin console, quality flags, async warmup) with a detailed README, but zero automated tests and no CI pipeline to guard against regressions.

viability.reasoning: A working, single-service MVP that directly targets the stated hackathon problem (missing-person reunification after a disaster) with genuine facial-matching logic and reasonable UX for a low-tech environment (server-rendered, no build step); but it needs security hardening (rate limiting, CSRF, biometric data policy), a non-SQLite datastore, notification integration (SMS/email, explicitly listed as a gap), and load testing before real deployment to earthquake victims.

key_features: Self-signup email/password auth with signed session cookies
Report missing person / report found person with photo upload
Automatic ArcFace facial-embedding cosine-similarity cross-matching between missing and found pools
Photo quality heuristics (blur, face size, brightness) that warn without blocking upload
Per-user data isolation: users only see matches involving their own uploads
Authenticated, ACL-gated photo serving (no public static file exposure)
Admin console for managing/deleting missing/found entries, matches, and users, with report reassignment on user deletion
Append-only audit log of all noteworthy events, viewable at /admin/logs
Idempotent SQLite schema migration on startup for zero-downtime column additions
Dockerfile + documented Railway deployment with persistent volume for DB and model cache
analysis.merge_potential: Could serve as the biometric-matching backend/module for a broader Build4Venezuela platform (e.g., feeding matches into a shelter-mapping or volunteer-coordination system), or be merged with an SMS/email notification service to close the "no proactive notification" gap it self-identifies.
red_flags: No automated tests or CI pipeline at all
SQLite as the only datastore — not viable for multi-instance or high-load production use
No rate limiting or visible CSRF protection on login/register/upload endpoints
Stores raw biometric (facial) data for vulnerable disaster victims with no encryption-at-rest, retention policy, or consent flow disclosed
No HTTPS enforcement (self-acknowledged in README) despite session cookies and biometric data being transmitted
No email/SMS notification on match — users must manually check the site (self-acknowledged limitation)

gaps: No public live demo/URL to click and try — repo-only, so donors/press cannot interact with it directly
No screenshots extracted for social cards
No evidence of real users or real matches made
Biometric privacy/consent story needs a public-facing explanation before wide promotion, given it stores facial data of vulnerable people
diffusion.recommended_angle: Feature the demo video walkthrough with a clear 'missing person facial-matching for reunification' narrative, framed as a promising hackathon prototype/backend module rather than a live tool people can use today

evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public web demo exists — the live_url points back to the GitHub repo, not a hosted instance. A 9-commit, ~1750 LOC FastAPI app with real auth, ArcFace facial matching, admin console, and audit logging was built and documented with a Railway/Docker deploy path, but nothing is verifiably deployed and reachable right now. A YouTube demo video (https://youtu.be/k4PaAmArSfU) exists showing the working flow, which is the only presentable artifact.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuela Juntos usa reconocimiento facial con IA para reunir a familias separadas por el desastre — sube una foto, y la tecnología busca la coincidencia.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family separation and missing-persons search after disasters/displacement is one of the most acute, well-documented needs in humanitarian response — matching found individuals to missing-person reports quickly can be life-or-death for reunification. This project directly targets that problem with a genuinely functional mechanism (ArcFace facial embedding cosine-similarity matching) rather than a superficial form. However, it is a single-service MVP with no field validation, no notification system (a self-acknowledged gap — users must manually check for matches), and serious unresolved concerns around storing raw biometric data of vulnerable disaster victims without encryption-at-rest, retention policy, or consent flow, which would need to be resolved before deployment to real earthquake victims.

name: Venezuela Response Hub
db_description:
## Venezuela Response Hub

Plataforma de coordinación humanitaria construida en respuesta al terremoto de Venezuela (junio 2026), desarrollada durante el ecosistema Build4Venezuela.

### ¿Qué hace?
- Centraliza información verificada de refugios, rutas de evacuación y puntos de ayuda
- Conecta organizaciones humanitarias, voluntarios y afectados en tiempo real
- Integra **SismoLab**, plataforma científica de monitoreo sísmico para Venezuela

### Tecnología
Desarrollado con IA aplicada a coordinación de crisis: procesamiento de datos en tiempo real, geolocalización de recursos y alertas verificadas.

### Impacto
Operativo en horas tras el desastre. Diseñado para escalar a futuras emergencias en América Latina.

### Construido por
Laura Zuluaga- Cofounder & CGO - Young AI Leaders Bogotá Hub · UN AI for Good / ITU Network

analysis.summary: Venezuela Response Hub is a single-page, static React (TanStack Start) website that acts as a curated directory of external humanitarian links (missing-persons sites, shelter maps, donation portals, emergency numbers) for the fictional June 2026 Venezuela earthquake response. It has no backend, no database, no auth, and no original data collection logic — it is essentially a styled landing page built almost entirely from Lovable/shadcn scaffolding with hardcoded arrays of third-party URLs and a hand-rolled i18n dictionary.
analysis.project_type: Static informational landing page / link directory (no backend)

backend: TanStack Start server entry (SSR only, no API routes)

architecture.pattern: Single-page SSR app (TanStack Start) with one real route (src/routes/index.tsx) rendering a long static component tree; src/server.ts wraps the generated SSR handler purely for error normalization
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database of any kind exists in the codebase
architecture.api_design: None; no API routes, no data endpoints, all 'data' is hardcoded TS arrays of external URLs embedded directly in index.tsx
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Minimal separation: nearly all business content (tool lists, emergency numbers, donation info) is hardcoded inline inside a single ~1300-line route component; only i18n text is externalized to src/lib/i18n.tsx
architecture.modularity: Low — one giant page file with many small local functions (Hero, Tools, Push, Prepare, etc.) rather than reusable modules; src/components/ui/* is untouched shadcn boilerplate
notable_patterns: Lovable.dev-generated scaffold (marked in AGENTS.md and .lovable/project.json)
Custom SSR error-capture/error-page fallback for h3 swallowed errors
Client-side i18n context (es/en) with a large string dictionary
All actionable functionality is outbound links to third-party sites, not in-app features

code_organization.directory_structure: Standard Vite/TanStack Start layout (src/routes, src/components/ui, src/lib) but src/components/ui is 100% untouched shadcn generated boilerplate (accordion, dialog, sidebar, chart, carousel, etc.) most of which is never used by the single page
code_organization.naming_quality: Reasonable naming within the one real file (Hero, QuickAccess, Tools, Emergency, Push, Prepare, Trust, Footer)
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is marketing-style and overstates scope ('platform', 'integrated platforms', 'SismoLab'); AGENTS.md is just Lovable's auto-generated warning; no code comments explaining data provenance/verification of the linked third-party tools
code_organization.reasoning: The repo is a thin single-page wrapper around dozens of unused shadcn components, with almost all real content crammed into one file; low modularity for what is otherwise a simple task.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets used or referenced anywhere (no env vars, no API keys) — nothing to leak, but also nothing configurable
production_readiness.security_notes: Low risk since there's no backend/data layer, but the site links out to numerous third-party 'unverified' and 'community' tools (labelled as such in the UI) without any vetting mechanism beyond a static trust badge the devs assign by hand; no CSP or rate limiting relevant since it's static content
production_readiness.scalability_notes: As a static/SSR page with no DB, it scales trivially (CDN-cacheable), but it also cannot grow into real coordination functionality (data entry, search across shelters, live updates) without a full rebuild — there is no data layer to scale
production_readiness.reasoning: Only real production concern (SSR crash) is handled via a custom error-page fallback in server.ts/start.ts, which is a genuinely thoughtful touch; but there are no tests, no CI, no environment configuration, and the 'platform' claims in the README (verified stats, live updates) are not backed by any actual backend.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Mostly boilerplate/scaffold (Lovable-generated shadcn starter) with one hand-authored content page layered on top; no tests, no CI pipeline, no GitHub Actions
maturity.reasoning: 306 commits and active push history (per signals) show real iteration on the single page's content/copy, but the project never grew beyond a curated links page — no data model, no persistence, no automated checks.

viability.reasoning: As a link directory it can genuinely help people find other tools quickly and cheaply (low risk, zero infra cost, easy to keep updating), but it duplicates functionality of the tools it links to rather than solving a coordination problem itself, has no mechanism to verify/refresh link validity beyond manual trust badges, and cannot be considered a 'humanitarian coordination platform' as the README claims — it is a landing page pointing to other people's platforms.

key_features: Curated, categorized directory of ~20 external humanitarian tools (missing persons, damage maps, shelters, donations, comms) with hand-assigned 'verified/community/unverified' trust badges
Bilingual (es/en) UI via a custom React i18n context
Static list of Venezuelan emergency phone numbers and official government/NGO channels
Manually compiled donation guide (money transfer options, in-kind donation drop-off addresses across multiple cities/countries)
Earthquake preparedness accordion content (before/during/after tips)
Custom SSR error-handling wrapper to avoid raw h3/TanStack Start crash pages reaching users
analysis.merge_potential: Could serve as a lightweight 'index/landing page' front door linking to more substantive projects in the hackathon (e.g., a real shelter-mapping or people-finder backend), but has no data model or API to merge/integrate with other projects at a code level — any integration would be purely a link/reference addition.
red_flags: README describes it as a 'humanitarian coordination platform' with 'integrated platforms' (Response Hub, SismoLab) but the code contains none of that — it's a single static page of outbound links
No tests, no CI/CD pipeline despite claiming to be a live production site at vzlaresponsehub.org
No backend or database at all; all 'live' stats (1,430+ dead, 68,900+ missing, 'Join 3,247 helpers') are hardcoded strings in meta tags and JSX, not real data

Trust/verification badges for third-party tools ('verified', 'community', 'unverified') appear to be manually assigned by the developer with no stated verification process, which is risky in a disaster context if a link is inaccurate or unsafe
Large amount of unused shadcn/ui boilerplate (60+ component files) inflating the codebase without corresponding usage

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position it honestly as a curated one-stop directory of vetted humanitarian links (missing persons, shelters, donations, emergency numbers) for Venezuela's earthquake — a fast, zero-cost way for victims/donors to find real help in one place — rather than as a 'coordination platform', since it has no backend or live data.
gaps: No real backend/data layer to back up the 'platform' framing or the hardcoded casualty/helper stats shown on the homepage
No verification process disclosed for the manually-assigned 'verified/community/unverified' trust badges on outbound links
No screenshots asset captured/confirmed separately from live demo
Unclear if it has any real users/testimonials beyond the hardcoded '3,247 helpers' counter

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch confirms https://vzlaresponsehub.org loads and renders: a crisis dashboard with earthquake stats, ~22 categorized outbound links (missing persons, structural assessment, shelters, medical, donations), emergency numbers, preparedness guides, and bilingual (es/en) content. It is real and accessible, but per the code analysis all of the 'live' stats (1,430+ dead, 68,900+ missing, '3,247 helpers') are hardcoded strings, not real data, and every actionable feature is an outbound link to a third-party tool rather than in-app functionality.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuela Response Hub: un solo lugar bilingüe con enlaces verificados a búsqueda de personas, refugios, ayuda médica y donaciones para el terremoto de Venezuela — encuentra ayuda real en segundos, no en 20 pestañas.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: During a disaster, victims and volunteers genuinely struggle to find scattered emergency resources (shelters, missing-persons registries, donation channels), so a curated, categorized, bilingual directory of vetted links does address a real (if secondary) coordination pain point — reducing search friction. However, the project is not itself a coordination platform: it has no backend, no database, no way to submit/verify new data, and duplicates functionality already offered by the tools it links to. Its README and homepage also overstate its capabilities (calling itself a 'humanitarian coordination platform' with hardcoded fake live stats), which is misleading in a crisis context and undermines trust if promoted as more than a link directory.

name: VidasVE
db_description:
Un mapa colaborativo para reportar el estado de edificios y zonas afectadas.
La idea es que un familiar en el exterior, un vecino o cualquier persona cercana pueda marcar un lugar en el mapa, indicar qué está pasando allí y ayudar a difundir dónde se necesita apoyo.
Así, quienes no están cerca pueden consultar el estado del edificio o zona donde viven sus seres queridos, ver si hay reportes recientes y compartir la información para buscar ayuda más rápido.

analysis.summary: VidasVE (MapaRescate) is a production-oriented missing-persons / disaster reporting map for Venezuela: a Vite/React SPA and an Azure Functions TypeScript API backed by Cosmos DB, with real anti-abuse (proof-of-work, rate limiting, Turnstile captcha), owner-token privacy model, moderation queue, geo search, PWA offline outbox, Bicep IaC, and a working GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with Azure OIDC deploy.
analysis.project_type: web app (PWA) with serverless API backend

architecture.pattern: Serverless functions API + SPA frontend, monorepo with npm workspaces (app/, api/, infra/)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw Cosmos DB SQL API queries via @azure/cosmos SDK, wrapped in a ReportStore interface with two implementations: CosmosReportStore (prod) and MemoryReportStore (in-memory fallback/tests), selected via env var (COSMOS_ENDPOINT / USE_IN_MEMORY_STORE)
architecture.api_design: Azure Functions HTTP triggers per resource (reportsCreate, reportGet, reportEvents, reportOwnerEvents, search, places, media, adminModeration, health, config, mapsToken, challenge, externalImage, posts) with shared lib modules for cross-cutting concerns (cors, validation, sanitize, rateLimit, captcha, crypto, priority, geo)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation: lib/ holds pure logic (priority scoring, status derivation, validation, sanitize, geo bbox/polygon) decoupled from functions/ HTTP handlers and store.ts persistence layer; frontend api/client.ts isolates HTTP calls from components
architecture.modularity: Well-modularized into small single-responsibility files (adminAuth.ts, captcha.ts, rateLimit.ts, crypto.ts, sanitize.ts, deriveStatus.ts, priority.ts, geo.ts) each independently unit-tested
notable_patterns: Interface-based store abstraction allowing swap between Cosmos and in-memory implementations
HMAC-based admin auth with constant-time comparison (safeEqual) and derived actor hash
Proof-of-work challenge for mutation anti-abuse plus Cloudflare Turnstile captcha with local text-fallback
Owner-token privacy model scoped per report, delivered via URL hash fragment (not query param)
PII encryption key and HMAC secret required as separate env secrets
Non-destructive/append-only report events with derived status computed from multiple event signals
Offline outbox pattern in frontend (lib/outbox.ts) with service worker for PWA install
Bounded/allow-listed geographic bboxes (ALLOWED_BBOXES_JSON) restricting map/search scope to disaster-affected zones

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear top-level split (app/api/infra/scripts) with consistent internal structure (src/functions, src/lib, tests) mirrored between app and api workspaces
code_organization.naming_quality: Descriptive, consistent naming (reportsCreate, deriveStatus, adminModeration) matching domain concepts
code_organization.documentation_quality: README.md (Spanish) is thorough and operationally specific: env vars, deployment steps, custom domain gotchas, P0 rules list; no inline JSDoc but code is largely self-explanatory
code_organization.reasoning: Consistent, professional layout across three sub-projects with strong naming discipline and an unusually detailed operational README, though it lacks in-code documentation/comments.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Secrets (ADMIN_API_TOKEN, APP_HMAC_SECRET, PII_ENCRYPTION_KEY, COSMOS_KEY, TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY) read from process.env only; local.settings.example.json and main.parameters.example.json provided as templates (not committed with real secrets); GitHub Actions uses Azure OIDC (federated credential) rather than long-lived publish profiles
production_readiness.security_notes: Admin auth uses bearer token compared via HMAC + constant-time safeEqual; anti-abuse stack includes proof-of-work challenge, rate limiting by IP/device/contact/report/geoCell, honeypot field, and Cloudflare Turnstile with a fallback text captcha; contacts are HMAC-hashed and excluded from public responses by default; CORS restricted to ALLOWED_ORIGINS; moderation queue and non-destructive removal flow exist
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Cosmos DB with configurable RU/s (free tier default 1000 RU/s) and TTL-based retention; server-side query limits capped (TOP 500, 100 for search) with client-side truncation warning; no server-side clustering for map yet, noted as a known limitation in README
production_readiness.reasoning: Real IaC (Bicep) provisioning Cosmos, Function App, Static Web App, App Insights, budget alerts, and optional Blob Storage; working CI (lint, unit tests for both workspaces, Playwright e2e, build) and two deploy workflows; env var docs are extensive and match code; a few operational rough edges (single free-tier Cosmos, no server-side map clustering) but overall close to genuinely deployable for a hackathon-grade MVP.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, non-trivial implementation
maturity.reasoning: 17 API test files plus a frontend offline test and Playwright e2e spec exercise real business logic (priority scoring, status derivation, PoW replay, rate limiting, admin auth, sanitize, CORS, media upload); 49 commits with incremental feature history (not a single AI-dump commit); this is clearly hand-iterated, working code rather than generated scaffolding.

viability.reasoning: Directly addresses a core earthquake-response need (crowd-sourced missing-person/status reporting with location, geographically scoped to Venezuela disaster zones) with a deployable, moderated, abuse-resistant public system; main risks are operational (single free-tier Cosmos, single contributor, no load testing evidence, no automated content moderation beyond flag counting) rather than code quality.

key_features: Public map-first SPA to report missing/found persons without login
Owner-token private links (HMAC-derived) for report owners to update status
Priority scoring and derived status computed from multiple report/event signals
Full-text search across reports, persons, posts, and locations excluding private contact info
Moderation queue with abuse flagging and non-destructive removal
Proof-of-work + rate limiting + honeypot + Turnstile captcha anti-abuse stack
Geofenced map/search restricted to configurable affected bboxes
Optional media upload (PNG/JPEG/WebP/PDF up to 5MB) to Azure Blob Storage behind a feature flag
PWA manifest/offline outbox and CSV import script for verified reports
IaC (Bicep) with budget alerts and CI/CD via Azure OIDC
analysis.merge_potential: Strong candidate for a core people-finder/mapping module in a combined platform; its store interface (ReportStore) and moderation/anti-abuse layer could be adapted or referenced by other teams building similar reporting tools, and its geofenced bbox + priority/status derivation logic is reusable for other disaster-reporting use cases.
red_flags: Single contributor and very young repo (created 2026-06-26, ~49 commits) — no evidence of load testing or real-world usage yet
Relies on a single Azure subscription's Cosmos free tier (1000 RU/s) noted explicitly as a constraint in README, limiting scale without manual upgrade
No automated/AI content moderation — abuse handling depends on community flags and a manual moderation queue with a shared admin token
No rate-limit/captcha bypass audit or penetration testing evidence beyond unit tests

gaps: No demo video
No screenshots on file
No real reports/data yet to show social proof
No branding/press kit assets confirmed
diffusion.recommended_angle: Technical-credibility angle: 'A production-grade, abuse-resistant crowdsourced missing-persons map for Venezuela earthquake zones — live and open source' aimed at developers, NGOs and donors rather than a viral consumer post (since it has zero real reports so far).

evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live URL (https://www.vidasve.help/) returns HTTP 200 and serves a real Vite/React SPA (title 'VidasVE', Spanish meta description about a public map of trapped persons/life signs/last known location in Venezuela). It is not just static HTML - the CSP headers reference a live Azure Functions API (maparescate-api-j5oyin3m4kbek.azurewebsites.net), Azure Maps (atlas.microsoft.com), Cloudflare Turnstile captcha, and Azure Blob Storage, confirming a real deployed backend. I directly verified the backend: GET /api/health returned {"ok":true,...}, and GET /api/search returned a valid empty-but-well-formed JSON payload ({"reports":[],"people":[],"posts":[],"locations":[]}) — i.e., the API works but currently has zero reports/data (no real usage yet). WebFetch could not render the JS-driven map UI itself (SPA content not visible to the fetch-and-summarize tool), but combined with curl verification this is clearly a working, live full-stack product, just without real user-generated content yet.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: VidasVE: un mapa colaborativo en vivo donde cualquier persona puede reportar el estado de un edificio o zona afectada en Venezuela, para que familiares en el exterior sepan dónde se necesita ayuda — ya desplegado y funcionando hoy.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After a Venezuelan earthquake, the most acute need is fast, reliable information about who is safe, who is missing, and which buildings/zones are affected — especially for the Venezuelan diaspora abroad who cannot check on relatives directly. VidasVE addresses this precisely: a public, no-login map for reporting missing/found persons and building/zone status, with owner-token private links for verified updates, geofencing to actual disaster-affected bboxes, priority/status scoring, and a moderation queue with anti-abuse protections (proof-of-work, rate limiting, captcha) to keep the crowdsourced data trustworthy. This is a well-scoped, realistic tool for the stated crisis rather than a generic app retrofitted to the hackathon theme.

name: Vzla Encuentra
db_description:
# Reencuentros

## Reunimos familias separadas por la emergencia humanitaria en Venezuela

**Reencuentros** es una plataforma web que usa reconocimiento facial para ayudar a localizar personas desaparecidas y reconectarlas con sus seres queridos.

---

## ¿Cómo funciona?

### Si estás buscando a alguien

Sube una foto de la persona que buscas. El sistema la compara con todos los registros de personas encontradas y te muestra los candidatos con mayor similitud facial, ordenados por nivel de coincidencia.

### Si encontraste a alguien

Registra a la persona con sus fotos y datos. Si alguien ya la está buscando, el sistema genera una alerta automática para conectar a ambas partes.

### Si quieres verificar antes de registrar

Subí una foto para saber si alguien está buscando a esa persona, sin necesidad de crear un registro.

---

## Características principales

- **Búsqueda por foto**: tecnología de reconocimiento facial que encuentra coincidencias incluso cuando no hay nombres ni documentos.
- **Protección de menores**: los nombres de niños, niñas y adolescentes se muestran protegidos en todas las respuestas públicas, incluso para administradores.
- **Alertas automáticas**: cuando un familiar busca a alguien que ya fue registrado como encontrado — o viceversa — el sistema avisa inmediatamente.
- **Moderación de contenido**: administradores revisan y aprueban las publicaciones antes de que aparezcan en búsquedas públicas.
- **Testimonios de reencuentros**: cualquier persona puede compartir fotos o videos de reuniones exitosas, inspirando a otras familias a no perder la esperanza.
- **Canales de reporte**: cualquier usuario puede reportar fallas técnicas o publicaciones inadecuadas.

---

## ¿Para quién es?

- **Familiares** que buscan a una persona desaparecida.
- **Rescatistas, voluntarios y organizaciones** que encuentran personas y necesitan identificar a quién avisar.
- **Administradores** que moderan contenido, gestionan reportes y mantienen la integridad de la plataforma.

---

## El problema que resolvemos

En contextos de crisis humanitaria, migración masiva y desapariciones, las familias quedan separadas sin canales efectivos para reencontrarse. Los métodos tradicionales (carteles, redes sociales, listas de nombres) dependen de que alguien reconozca un nombre o una foto.

Reencuentros usa reconocimiento facial para cerrar esa brecha: una foto vale más que mil nombres, especialmente cuando se trata de niños que no hablan, adultos en shock, o personas sin documentos.

---

## Privacidad y seguridad

- Los datos de menores se almacenan pero se protegen en todas las respuestas públicas.
- Solo administradores autenticados pueden moderar, eliminar o acceder al listado completo de registros.
- Las imágenes se almacenan de forma segura y las publicaciones pasan por moderación antes de ser visibles en búsquedas públicas.

---

Hecho con ❤️ para Venezuela.

analysis.summary: Reencuentros (leer_rostros) is a production-grade FastAPI facial-recognition service to reunite missing persons with families after disasters: familiares upload a photo to search, rescatistas register found persons, and pgvector/InsightFace ArcFace embeddings do similarity matching, with a real admin panel, moderation, minors-privacy masking, testimonios, reportes, traceability history, and a live CI/CD deploy pipeline to a real domain (vzlaencuentra.com / symtechven.com).
analysis.project_type: web app (FastAPI backend API + minimal static HTML admin/front) with facial recognition ML pipeline

frontend: single static frontend/index.html (305 lines) — thin admin/demo UI, not a full SPA
infra_deploy: GitHub Actions CD (deploy.yml, rsync+ssh to DigitalOcean droplet, pm2, nginx)

architecture.pattern: layered / bounded-context architecture: FastAPI routes -> use_cases -> repositories -> Postgres, with a pure domain layer (matching policy, privacy rules, entities) independent of SQL/HTTP
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: raw SQL via psycopg3 connection pool + pgvector extension, wrapped in per-context Repository classes (PersonaRepository, ReporteRepository, TestimonioRepository); no ORM
architecture.api_design: REST-ish JSON/multipart FastAPI endpoints, documented extensively via OpenAPI tags/descriptions, with legacy-array and paginated {data,meta} response variants for backward compatibility
architecture.separation_of_concerns: strong: domain/ (pure logic, no I/O), personas|reportes|testimonios/{repositories,use_cases}, shared/ (exceptions, helpers, SSRF-safe net), auth.py, storage.py, faces.py, database.py cleanly separated; main.py has no inline SQL for core tables
architecture.modularity: high — bounded-context folders each with their own repository + use_cases + fakes for testing
notable_patterns: Use-case pattern with dependency-injected repositories, testable via FakePersonaRepository/FakeReporteRepository without a DB or InsightFace
Domain exception -> HTTP status mapping centralized in _use_case_execute
Multi-embedding augmentation per photo (base + ±15° rotations) for pose robustness
MenoresPrivacy: minors' names masked below a confidence threshold across API boundary
SSRF-hardened URL image downloader (resolves + validates every redirect hop against private/loopback/link-local ranges) for the bulk-import endpoint
Idempotent schema migrations via ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS + advisory lock to avoid multi-worker race on startup

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean bounded-context layout (app/{domain,personas,reportes,testimonios,shared}) mirrored in tests/, plus openspec/ change-management docs, scripts/ for local tooling and batch import, docker/ configs for standalone and compose deploys.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish domain naming (personas/buscada/encontrada) matching the business domain; use-case files named as verbs (RegistrarBusqueda, ModerarPersona) mapping 1:1 to endpoints.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Excellent — CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, ARQUITECTURA.md, API.md, DEPLOY.md, DOCKER.md, plus rich inline docstrings and an extensive OpenAPI description embedded in main.py.
code_organization.reasoning: Directory layout, naming and documentation all reflect a deliberately engineered, professionally organized codebase rather than hackathon scaffolding.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env-based config via pydantic-settings with .env.example and env_template.py; JWT_SECRET/DB creds not committed; CI uses GitHub Actions secrets (SSH_PRIVATE_KEY, SSH_HOST, SSH_USER) for deploy; app fails fast / warns loudly if JWT_SECRET is unset.
production_readiness.security_notes: bcrypt password hashing, JWT with expiry, admin-only endpoints via HTTPBearer dependency with is_active check, generic 401 to avoid user-enumeration, CORS restricted to real production origins (not '*'), SSRF protection on the URL-import endpoint (validates every redirect hop against private IP ranges, size-capped), minors' PII masked below match-confidence threshold. No rate limiting visible on public endpoints (login, search) which is a residual risk for brute force.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Uses a connection pool, pgvector HNSW index for fast ANN search at scale, pagination on all list endpoints, and multi-worker-safe schema init via Postgres advisory lock; InsightFace runs on CPUExecutionProvider only, which will be a throughput bottleneck under real load without GPU or horizontal scaling of the recognition step.
production_readiness.reasoning: Has real auth, structured error handling mapped to HTTP codes, environment-driven config, working CI (required status check) and CD (auto-deploy with health check) to an actual production domain — this is far beyond a hackathon demo in operational maturity; the main gaps are no structured logging/observability and no explicit rate limiting.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real, substantial hand/AI-engineered application with genuine domain logic, not boilerplate
maturity.reasoning: 12,259 LOC, 114 commits from 5 contributors, ~30 use-case test files with fake repositories achieving reported 100% coverage on use cases/domain/shared, working CI required-check + CD pipeline deploying to a live production host with a post-deploy health check, and iterative architecture-change tracking under openspec/.

viability.reasoning: This is already deployed to production (vzlaencuentra.com/symtechven.com) with a real facial-recognition matching pipeline, admin moderation, minors-privacy protections, and traceability history — directly addressing family reunification after a disaster; it is one of the most technically complete and operationally ready projects plausible for this hackathon category.

key_features: Facial similarity search (ArcFace/InsightFace + pgvector HNSW cosine) matching missing-person photos against found-person photos with confidence percentages
Dual-flow registration: familiar searches ('buscados') and rescatista registers found persons ('encontrados'), each surfacing reverse matches/alerts
Text-only fallback flow (no photo) matching by cédula/nombre for cases without a usable image
Traceability/history of sightings per found person (public trimmed view + full admin view with contact info)
Minors' PII masking below a match-confidence threshold across all public/admin responses
Admin panel: JWT+bcrypt auth, moderation of listings, reportes (bug/inappropriate content) triage, testimonios (reunion stories) approval workflow, real dashboard stats
Bulk import endpoint with SSRF-hardened image downloading and idempotency by external id
CI required test gate + automated CD to a live droplet with rsync, pm2 restart, and post-deploy health check
analysis.merge_potential: This is a strong, largely self-contained flagship candidate rather than a merge target — other simpler people-finder or shelter-mapping projects in the hackathon could integrate as data sources/importers (the /encontrados/importar bulk-import endpoint is explicitly designed for this) rather than this project merging into something else.
red_flags: No structured logging/observability (only print statements at startup) — will hamper incident response in a real deployment
No visible rate limiting on public endpoints like /admin/login or the search endpoints, leaving some brute-force/DoS exposure
InsightFace runs on CPUExecutionProvider only; will need infra scaling (GPU or horizontal workers) to handle real disaster-scale load
Frontend is a single 305-line static HTML file — the actual public-facing UI is minimal/thin compared to the backend's sophistication and would need real frontend work for broad public use

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family separation after a disaster is one of the most acute needs in a humanitarian emergency — survivors, rescuers, and shelters have no fast way to cross-reference 'missing' reports against 'found' persons, especially for children, unconscious/shocked adults, or people without documents. VzlaEncuentra directly targets this with facial-recognition matching (ArcFace/InsightFace + pgvector similarity search) between 'buscados' and 'encontrados' registries, plus a text-fallback flow for cases without usable photos, minors' PII protection, and admin moderation to prevent abuse of a sensitive dataset.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://vzlaencuentra.com/ loads a real, installable PWA (manifest.webmanifest, Vite-built React SPA bundle at /assets/index-*.js returning 200 OK) titled "VzlaEncuentra — S.O.S. Búsqueda de personas" with copy explicitly referencing the Venezuela earthquake and facial recognition to reunite families. This sits on top of a genuinely production-grade FastAPI + PostgreSQL/pgvector backend (12,259 LOC, 114 commits, 5 contributors, CI required-check + automated CD to a DigitalOcean droplet with post-deploy health checks, JWT+bcrypt auth, SSRF-hardened bulk import, minors-privacy masking). Note: the technical insight report describes the frontend as a thin 305-line static admin HTML file, which understates what is actually deployed publicly — the live domain serves a full built SPA/PWA, not a bare static page.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the human story: a working facial-recognition tool, live today at vzlaencuentra.com, that can reunite Venezuelan families separated by the earthquake — upload one photo and get matched against everyone registered as found. Pair the live demo link with the Instagram reel to show it in action.
gaps: No confirmed real-user/real-match testimonial yet (a testimonios feature exists but needs a live success story for the post)
Could add a screenshot carousel of the actual search/upload flow for a static social post in addition to the video

evaluation.one_line_pitch: VzlaEncuentra: sube una foto y el reconocimiento facial reúne en minutos a las familias separadas por el terremoto — ya en vivo en vzlaencuentra.com.

name: zerena
db_description:
## El problema

El 24 de junio de 2026, dos terremotos fuertes (magnitudes 7.2 y 7.5) sacudieron Venezuela con segundos de diferencia, con epicentro cerca de San Felipe y Yumare, en Yaracuy. Las réplicas no han parado: más de 140 hasta ahora, y cada una revive el miedo. El saldo es devastador: edificios y viviendas colapsadas, fallecidos, miles de heridos y muchas personas aún desaparecidas bajo los escombros. Familias enteras quedaron separadas o esperando noticias de los suyos.

Detrás del daño físico hay un impacto emocional enorme: pánico, duelo, insomnio, culpa, y un miedo que vuelve con cada temblor. Las organizaciones de salud señalaron el apoyo psicosocial como una de las necesidades prioritarias, pero la ayuda profesional no alcanza para todos, y mucha gente, asustada y a veces sin señal, está sola justo en el momento en que más necesita a alguien.

## La respuesta: Zerena

Zerena es una app de Primeros Auxilios Psicológicos (PAP) pensada para ese momento. Es offline y anónima: no pide registro, se instala en el teléfono como una app y funciona sin conexión, así que sostiene incluso cuando la red no responde. Todo su contenido se basa en protocolos reconocidos de salud mental en emergencias (OMS/OPS, IFRC, NCTSN, Save the Children, Pauline Boss) y está en revisión por profesionales.

Tiene dos caminos, según lo que necesites en el momento: calma para ti o herramientas para acompañar a alguien más.

## Si necesitas calma para ti

- Respira conmigo y ejercicios para volver al presente cuando el cuerpo se desborda.
- ¿Cómo estás ahora?: un chequeo breve que te orienta hacia lo que puede ayudarte.
- Esto que sientes es normal: entender el miedo, la culpa o el insomnio sin sentir que algo anda mal contigo.
- Apoyo específico para descansar de noche, el duelo, perderlo todo, esperar por un desaparecido, las réplicas y estar lejos mientras tu gente sufre.
- Una mano con el doomscrolling: seguir las noticias sin que te consuman.
- Diario privado que se guarda solo en tu teléfono.

## Si quieres ayudar a alguien

- Entrenamiento para voluntarios: 14 módulos breves, una pantalla a la vez, con ejemplos y frases concretas. Cubre observar, escuchar, conectar, qué decir y qué no, pánico y shock, niños, adultos mayores, quién necesita más ayuda, pérdida ambigua (personas no encontradas), réplicas, acompañar desde lejos, y cómo cuidarte tú.
- Guía rápida para el momento real: qué decir, ayudar a un niño o a un adulto mayor, acompañar a distancia, y qué hacer si alguien no mejora.

## Además

- Directorio de ayuda verificado con líneas de emergencia.
- Red de apoyo: voluntarios, profesionales y organizaciones pueden sumarse.
- Una luz del día: un mensaje breve de aliento que cambia cada día.

Zerena no es terapia y no reemplaza la atención profesional ni los servicios de emergencia. Es la mano que puede estar ahí, en el bolsillo, mientras llega el resto.

## Pruébala primero

Ábrela y elige "Necesito calma para mí" para respirar, o "Quiero ayudar a alguien" para hacer el entrenamiento. Instálala desde el navegador y te queda offline.

analysis.summary: Zerena is a Spanish-language offline-capable PWA delivering Psychological First Aid (PAP) content for Venezuelan earthquake survivors, built with TanStack Start + Supabase, deployed via Lovable Cloud. It has real, well-structured content routes (grief, panic, breathing, helping others), a staff triage inbox backed by Supabase with RLS policies and role-based access control, and anonymous public submission forms for support requests and volunteer network signups.
analysis.project_type: web app (PWA)

architecture.pattern: Full-stack React meta-framework (TanStack Start) with file-based routing and server functions; Supabase as BaaS for auth/db/RLS
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (postgREST) for both client-side (anon key, RLS-scoped) and server-side (service role key for admin ops); no ORM, raw table queries via supabase-js
architecture.api_design: TanStack Start createServerFn RPC-style handlers, not REST/GraphQL; zod input validation on mutation endpoints
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation: client-safe supabase client vs client.server.ts (service role, admin-only, explicitly documented not to leak to bundle), auth-middleware.ts enforces JWT bearer auth before handler execution, recepcion.functions.ts adds defense-in-depth authorization checks on top of DB-level RLS
architecture.modularity: Content routes are simple, focused files per topic (para-mi.*, para-ayudar.*); shared logic in src/lib; shadcn ui components isolated in components/ui
notable_patterns: Defense-in-depth: explicit server-side role/ownership checks (assertCaseAccess) layered on top of Postgres RLS policies
Custom service worker with shell/runtime cache versioning for offline-first PWA behavior
Zod-validated server functions with typed responses
12 incremental Supabase SQL migrations building up support_requests/network_signups/session_notes/user_roles schema with RLS INSERT-only policies for anonymous submitters (no public SELECT)

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean, conventional TanStack Start layout: src/routes (file-based), src/components (+ui subfolder for shadcn primitives), src/lib (business logic/server fns), src/integrations/supabase (typed client layer), supabase/migrations (versioned SQL)
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish route naming (para-mi.*, para-ayudar.*) matching the app's dual audience (self-help vs helping others); functions and types clearly named
code_organization.documentation_quality: README and AGENTS.md present but mostly Lovable boilerplate/meta notes; inline code comments in Spanish explain security rationale (RLS + defense-in-depth) which is a good sign of real engineering thought, not just AI scaffolding
code_organization.reasoning: Structure and naming are clean and idiomatic for the framework; comments show deliberate security reasoning, though top-level docs are thin and largely tool-generated.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: The .env file (containing Supabase project URL and anon/publishable JWT key) is committed to git history; while these are publishable/anon keys (not the service-role secret) so not catastrophic, committing .env is poor practice and the service-role key presumably lives only in Lovable Cloud's env, not verifiable in-repo
production_readiness.security_notes: Auth middleware validates Bearer JWT structure and calls supabase.auth.getClaims; server functions add explicit role/ownership checks beyond RLS; RLS policies restrict anonymous inserts with length/enum constraints and no public SELECT, which is solid for protecting victim data. No rate limiting visible on public anonymous insert endpoints (support_requests, network_signups), which could be abused for spam.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Relies entirely on Supabase managed Postgres and Lovable Cloud hosting; no explicit caching/CDN config beyond the PWA service worker; fine for hackathon/early-deployment scale but unproven under load.
production_readiness.reasoning: Real auth, RLS-backed data access, input validation, and a working offline PWA shell put this above a prototype, but absence of CI/tests/Dockerfile and a committed .env keep it short of a fully production-hardened release.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: 128 commits, 12 incremental SQL migrations, substantial hand/AI-crafted PAP content across ~20 routes, and a functioning staff triage workflow indicate genuine iterative development, not empty scaffolding — but there are zero automated tests and no CI pipeline.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed live at zerena.lovable.app, addresses a real and underserved need (psychological first aid content + anonymous request routing to volunteers/professionals) with working offline PWA support and a functional staff backend; main gaps for real-world use are spam/abuse protection on public forms and lack of tests, not fundamental architecture problems.

key_features: Offline-first PWA with custom service worker caching app shell
Bilingual dual-track content: self-help ('para mí') and helper guidance ('para ayudar') for panic, grief, children, elderly, distance support
Anonymous public forms for requesting psychological support or joining as volunteer/professional/organization
Staff triage inbox (recepcion) with role-based access (coordinador/profesional/voluntario), case assignment, urgency/status tracking, and session notes
Supabase RLS-enforced data model with defense-in-depth server-side authorization checks
Personal diary/journaling feature (src/lib/diary.ts) and breathing exercise UI
analysis.merge_potential: Could integrate with a shelter-mapping or people-finder platform as its mental-health support module, or share its volunteer/professional signup network with other aid-logistics projects; its RLS+defense-in-depth Supabase pattern is a good reference for other Supabase-based hackathon entries.
red_flags: .env file committed to git (contains Supabase project URL and anon key; not catastrophic but poor hygiene and should be rotated/removed from history)
No automated tests or CI pipeline despite handling sensitive support-request data
No visible rate-limiting/anti-abuse controls on public anonymous insert endpoints (support_requests, network_signups), risking spam or abuse of a crisis-support form

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake psychological first aid is a documented priority gap: physical rescue/shelter get most attention, but panic, grief, insomnia, ambiguous loss (missing persons), and aftershock-triggered fear are widespread and under-resourced. Zerena directly targets this with an offline-capable, anonymous PWA offering evidence-informed (WHO/PFA, IFRC, NCTSN, Save the Children, Pauline Boss) self-help content plus a volunteer-training track and a triage/referral pipeline to professionals. It's not solving logistics/rescue but a real, distinct, and often-ignored need.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://zerena.help/ loads and is a real, navigable Spanish-language PWA (not just a static landing page): it has a homepage hub with four real pathways (self-help, help-others, professional connection form, volunteer/organization signup) plus deep content routes (e.g. /para-mi) delivering genuine, specific PFA content (breathing/grounding exercises, grief, missing-person/ambiguous-loss guidance, aftershock anxiety, doomscrolling, private journal) rather than placeholder copy. Backend has Supabase-backed anonymous forms and a staff triage inbox with RLS and role-based access per the code analysis.

diffusion.recommended_angle: 'La app que te acompaña cuando tiembla la tierra y no hay señal': an offline, anonymous psychological first-aid PWA for Venezuelan earthquake survivors — install it from the browser, no signup, works without internet, with real content for panic, grief, and supporting others (children, elderly, people waiting for missing loved ones).
gaps: No confirmed real_users/usage evidence yet (0 GitHub stars, brand-new repo, no testimonials)
No rate-limiting/anti-abuse on public anonymous forms, a risk if promoted at scale
env file with Supabase keys committed to git history (hygiene issue, low severity since anon key only)
Screenshots not independently verified in this pass (only text-fetch of live site)

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Zerena: primeros auxilios psicológicos offline y anónimos en tu bolsillo para cuando tiembla la tierra en Venezuela — sin señal, sin registro, sin juicio.

name: GeoResponde
db_description:
## **GeoResponde**

GeoResponde es una plataforma humanitaria de interoperabilidad de código abierto diseñada para conectar iniciativas existentes de respuesta a emergencias, en lugar de reemplazarlas.

Tras el terremoto de Venezuela de 2026, surgieron decenas de plataformas independientes para reportar personas desaparecidas, refugios, centros de acopio, daños en infraestructura y necesidades humanitarias. Aunque cada iniciativa aporta información valiosa, los datos permanecen fragmentados entre múltiples sitios web y organizaciones.

GeoResponde busca resolver este desafío mediante una arquitectura federada que permite a las personas buscar, visualizar y enviar información a través de múltiples proveedores desde una interfaz unificada.

## Capacidades actuales
- Búsqueda federada entre múltiples proveedores humanitarios.
- Arquitectura basada en un Provider Gateway.
- Capas de Inteligencia Científica.
- Mapa de la Sala de Situación.
- SDK abierto para proveedores (en desarrollo).

## Visión

GeoResponde evoluciona en cuatro etapas:

- Federación — Conectar plataformas existentes sin duplicar la información.
- Interoperabilidad — Definir una interfaz abierta que las organizaciones puedan implementar.
- Protocolo de Intercambio para Emergencias — Permitir el intercambio estandarizado de información entre sistemas independientes.
- Ecosistema Abierto — Impulsar una comunidad de proveedores, SDKs, capas científicas y herramientas humanitarias.

Únete a GeoREsponde

Damos la bienvenida a:

- Organizaciones humanitarias.
- Desarrolladores.
- Especialistas en SIG (GIS).
- Diseñadores UX.
- Expertos en gestión de emergencias.
- Proveedores de datos.

**GitHub:**
https://github.com/GeoResponde/georesponde

analysis.summary: GeoResponde is a real, well-architected open-source geospatial disaster response platform for Venezuela: a React/MapLibre map visualizing earthquakes, faults, Copernicus/NASA damage layers, hospitals and citizen reports, plus a Fastify backend gateway with pluggable 'provider adapters' that scrape/federate real third-party sites (venezuelatebusca.com missing-persons search, terremotovenezuela.com building-damage reports) via a Remix single-fetch/turbo-stream deserializer with actual unit tests and fixtures. It is a monorepo (pnpm workspaces) with a catalog validation package, CI (build/typecheck/lint), and live Vercel deployment, but the backend has no auth/rate-limiting, at least one hardcoded localhost URL bug, no persistence layer, and heavy reliance on scraping fragile third-party endpoints.
analysis.project_type: web app (disaster-response geospatial situation room: map dashboard + people-finder search + damage-report submission gateway)

backend: custom Remix turbo-stream deserializer for scraping
database: none (no persistent DB; reads static GeoJSON/JSON catalog files, proxies live third-party APIs)

architecture.pattern: monorepo with adapter/gateway pattern: frontend SPA + Fastify backend 'Provider Gateway' that routes search/submit requests to per-source adapter classes implementing a common BaseAdapter interface
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: none — no database; backend proxies third-party HTTP APIs/scrapes HTML/turbo-stream responses and serves static GeoJSON files from public/data
architecture.api_design: small REST-ish Fastify API (/api/providers, /api/search, /api/submit, /api/providers/:id/geojson, /health) plus a debug/inspect endpoint left in production code (/api/dev/inspect/venezuelatebusca)
